At the ice front, latent heat of fusion is released as water changes to ice, and this heat is conducted back outward through the frozen layer, eventually being removed by the refrigerant. The system reaches the storage temperature T_a when the centre of the food crosses T_f and the entire mass is ice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Differentiate between the freezing point curves of pure water and a typical food material." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The freezing point curve of pure water is a **sharp, single-plateau curve** at 0 °C, indicating that pure water freezes at one definite temperature (0 °C) and the temperature remains constant throughout the phase change.

In contrast, the freezing point curve of a typical food material is a **sloping curve** without a sharp plateau, because:
- Foods contain dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, acids) that depress the freezing point by colligative effects.
- Freezing occurs over a **range of temperatures**, not at a single point.
- The curve shows a small **supercooling dip** (1–2 °C below the initial freezing point) before ice nucleation.
- After nucleation, the temperature rises back to the true freezing point and then decreases progressively as the remaining solution becomes more concentrated.

Hence, the food curve is broader and has no flat plateau, whereas the water curve is steep with a horizontal plateau. **(3 marks — 1.5 marks for water, 1.5 marks for food)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"List any three common food materials and their initial freezing points. Why do foods freeze at temperatures below 0 °C?" (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Food Material | Initial Freezing Point (°C) |
|---------------|-----------------------------|
| Beef muscle | −1.7 |
| Whole milk | −0.5 |
| Fish (cod) | −1.1 |

*(any three valid pairs accepted — 1.5 marks)*

**Why do foods freeze below 0 °C?** Foods contain dissolved solutes such as sugars, salts, acids, and proteins. These solutes cause **freezing point depression** (a colligative property described by Raoult's Law), lowering the temperature at which the water in the food begins to form ice. The more solutes present, the lower the freezing point. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the principle of food freezing with a neat temperature–time freezing curve. Discuss the significance of the latent heat of fusion in the freezing process. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive Plank's equation for an infinite slab and state the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle of Food Freezing:**

Food freezing is a preservation technique in which the temperature of the food is reduced below its freezing point, converting a large portion of the free water into ice crystals. This:
- Inhibits microbial growth (most bacteria stop at −10 °C).
- Slows enzymatic reactions (Q₁₀ effect).
- Preserves sensory and nutritional quality for months.

**Significance of Latent Heat of Fusion (L):**
- L is the heat released when 1 kg of water freezes, ≈ **334 kJ/kg** for pure water.
- In foods, the effective L is lower (typically 200–280 kJ/kg) because only the *free water* freezes.
- The latent heat removal step is the **most energy-expensive stage** of freezing — it is 5 to 8 times the energy needed for sensible cooling.

**Temperature–Time Freezing Curve Description:**

| Zone | Description | Energy Type |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| A → B | Cooling of fresh food from T_i to T_f | Sensible heat |
| B → C | Supercooling (1–2 °C below T_f) | No phase change |
| C → D | Ice nucleation and rise back to T_f | Latent heat release |
| D → E | Progressive freezing, slope downwards | Latent heat removal |
| E → F | Sub-cooling of frozen mass to T_a | Sensible heat |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Assumptions of Plank's Equation:**
1. The food is a regular infinite slab, infinite cylinder, or sphere.
2. The freezing point T_f and latent heat L are constant.
3. Thermal conductivity of the frozen layer is constant.
4. The product is initially at its freezing point (no pre-cooling).
5. The surface is instantly at the refrigerant temperature T_a (in the simplified form).
6. The surface heat transfer coefficient v is constant.

**Derivation (Step by Step):**

For an infinite slab of half-thickness D, the temperature distribution in the frozen layer at any time is approximately linear:

$$T(x) = T_a + (T_f - T_a) \cdot \frac{D - x}{D - \xi}$$

Heat flux at the surface:

$$q = \frac{k \, (T_f - T_a)}{D - \xi}$$

At the moving ice front, the latent heat release rate equals the heat conducted outward:

$$\rho \, L \, \frac{d\xi}{dt} = \frac{k \, (T_f - T_a)}{D - \xi}$$

Separating variables and integrating from ξ = D to ξ = 0 over t = 0 to t = t_f:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \, L}{k \, (T_f - T_a)} \int_0^D (D - \xi) \, d\xi = \frac{\rho \, L \, D^2}{2 \, k \, (T_f - T_a)}$$

This is the simplified Plank equation for an infinite slab, with shape factor P₂ = 1/2. The full Plank equation includes the surface resistance terms as shown in §3.1.5.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the freezing point curve of a food material. Compare it with the freezing curve of pure water. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following data are given for a slab-shaped food product being frozen in a blast freezer: thickness 2D = 6 cm, density ρ = 1100 kg/m³, latent heat L = 230 kJ/kg, freezing point T_f = −2 °C, thermal conductivity of frozen food k = 1.4 W/(m·K), refrigerant temperature T_a = −30 °C. Use Plank's equation to estimate the freezing time. Take the shape factor P₂ for an infinite slab as 1/2. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Freezing Point Curve of a Food (Diagram in text-form):**

```
T (°C)
  |
T_i|*
  |  \
  |   \        (Sensible cooling)
  |    \
T_f|----* (initial freezing point)
  |     \   /
  |      \ /  (supercooling dip + rise)
  |       *
  |        \
  |         \___ (Progressive freezing, sloping)
  |              \
T_a|_______________\____ (storage temperature)
  |_________________________ t (time)
       A   B   C   D   E
```

**Comparison Table (5 marks in answer):**

| Feature | Pure Water | Food |
|---------|-----------|------|
| Freezing point | Sharp single value at 0 °C | Range, e.g., −0.5 to −3 °C |
| Plateau | Sharp, flat at 0 °C | No plateau; sloping curve |
| Supercooling | Minimal (in pure, undisturbed water) | Visible dip of 1–2 °C |
| Solute effect | None | Freezing point depression (Raoult) |
| After freezing | Temperature drops rapidly | Slower drop due to cryoconcentration |

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- 2D = 6 cm → D = 0.03 m
- ρ = 1100 kg/m³
- L = 230 × 10³ J/kg
- T_f = −2 °C, T_a = −30 °C
- k = 1.4 W/(m·K)
- P₂ = 1/2

Temperature difference:
$$T_f - T_a = -2 - (-30) = 28 \, \text{K}$$

Apply the simplified Plank equation for an infinite slab:

$$t = \frac{\rho \, L \, D^2}{2 \, k \, (T_f - T_a)}$$

Substituting values:

$$t = \frac{1100 \times 230 \times 10^3 \times (0.03)^2}{2 \times 1.4 \times 28}$$

$$t = \frac{1100 \times 230000 \times 0.0009}{78.4}$$

$$t = \frac{227700}{78.4} \approx 2904.34 \, \text{s}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t \approx \frac{2904.34}{3600} \approx 0.807 \, \text{h} \approx 48.4 \, \text{minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The freezing time is approximately **2904 s or 48.4 minutes**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** — most students lose marks by mixing seconds with W and kg. Always check: ρ [kg/m³] × L [J/kg] × D² [m²] = J/m, divided by k [W/(m·K)] × ΔT [K] = W/m = J/(s·m). The m cancels and the answer comes in **seconds**. Always convert final answer to hours/minutes.
> 2. **D in the equation** — for an infinite slab, D is the **half-thickness**, NOT the full thickness. If the question says "thickness 6 cm", D = 0.03 m.
> 3. **Shape factor confusion** — P₂ for slab is 1/2, for cylinder is 1/4, for sphere is 1/6. Mixing them up will give a wrong numerical answer.
> 4. **Sign convention** — T_f − T_a is always positive (absolute temperature difference). Do not put a negative sign.
> 5. **L for foods is not 334 kJ/kg** — the actual latent heat of foods is lower (200–280 kJ/kg) because not all water freezes. Use the value given in the problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Food Freezing**

- **Definition:** Freezing = heat removal below the freezing point, converting free water → ice, for preservation.
- **Pure water freezing point:** 0 °C, with a flat plateau on the T–t curve.
- **Food freezing point:** A *range* (typically −0.5 °C to −3 °C), due to **freezing point depression** by dissolved solutes (colligative property / Raoult's Law).
- **Supercooling:** Common in foods; the food cools 1–2 °C below T_f before ice nucleation.
- **Progressive freezing:** Solutes concentrate in the unfrozen phase as freezing proceeds.
- **Three thermal resistances:** (1) Surface convection, (2) Conduction through frozen layer, (3) Sub-cooling of frozen mass.
- **Latent heat of fusion (L):** ~334 kJ/kg for pure water; 200–280 kJ/kg for typical foods.
- **Plank's Equation (general form):**
  $$t = \frac{\rho L}{\Delta T} \left[ \frac{P_1 D}{v} + \frac{R_1 D^2}{v^2} + \frac{P_2 D^2}{k} \right]$$
- **Simplified Plank (infinite slab):** $t = \rho L D^2 / [2k(T_f - T_a)]$.
- **Shape factors:** Slab P₂ = 1/2; Cylinder P₂ = 1/4; Sphere P₂ = 1/6.
- **Critical scaling law:** Freezing time $t \propto D^2$ (doubling thickness → 4× freezing time).
- **Common freezing points:** Milk −0.5 °C, Beef −1.7 °C, Fish −1.1 °C, Orange juice −1.3 °C, Apple −1.4 °C.
- **Industrial relevance:** Thin packaging, high-velocity blast freezing, and high thermal conductivity of ice (k ≈ 2.2 W/m·K) are the engineering levers for fast freezing.
- **Limitations of Plank:** Constant T_f assumption, no bound-water correction — modern methods (Cleland-Earle, Pham) address these.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freezing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Freezing Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Freezing System (Definition):** A freezing system is an integrated engineering assembly of thermal, mechanical, and control components designed to remove sensible and latent heat from a food product at controlled rates, lowering its temperature below its initial freezing point (typically **−1 °C to −4 °C** depending on solute concentration) and subsequently maintaining it in a frozen state at the recommended storage temperature (≤ **−18 °C** as per Codex Alimentarius and FSSAI regulations). The system converts the food's free water into ice crystals by extracting the **latent heat of fusion (≈ 333 kJ/kg or 80 kcal/kg for pure water)**, while ensuring product quality attributes—texture, color, nutritional value, and microbial safety—are preserved. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Ice-Cream Factory":** Imagine you are making ice cream at home. You have a sweet liquid mixture (cream + sugar + flavor). To freeze it, you need to (a) cool it below 0 °C, (b) remove a huge amount of heat—the *latent heat*—to convert water into ice, and (c) keep it cold afterwards. A **freezing system in food processing is simply an industrial "ice-cream factory"** that does this for tons of food—fish, meat, fruits, vegetables, ready meals—using either cold air, cold plates, cold liquids, or extremely cold gases like liquid nitrogen. The faster you remove the heat, the smaller the ice crystals, the better the texture. **Speed of freezing = Quality of food.** ## 1.3 Classification of Freezing Systems (Big Picture) | Family | Mechanism | Typical Refrigerant / Medium | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanical (Vapour Compression)** | Evaporation of refrigerant absorbs latent heat | Ammonia (R-717), Freon (R-404A, R-22) | Most industrial freezers | | **Cryogenic** | Direct contact with liquefied gas | Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂, −196 °C), Liquid CO₂ (−78 °C) | High-value IQF products | | **Indirect Contact** | Heat removed via chilled surface | Brine / Glycol (secondary refrigerant) | In-pack freezing | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Fact to Memorize:** The freezing point depression of food follows **Raoult's Law**. For every 1 mole of solute dissolved per kg of water, the freezing point drops by **1.86 °C** (the cryoscopic constant of water). Most foods freeze in the range **−1 °C to −4 °C**, NOT at 0 °C. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principal Freezing Methods (Detailed) ### A. Air Blast Freezing (Most Common Industrial System) - Cold air at **−30 °C to −40 °C** is forced over the product at velocities of **1.5 m/s to 6 m/s** by axial or centrifugal fans. - Operates in **batch** (tunnel) or **continuous** (spiral/belt) configurations. - Heat transfer mode: **forced convection**. - Slow-to-medium freezing rate; product center reaches 0 °C in 30 min – 4 h depending on size. ### B. Plate Freezing (Horizontal / Vertical) - Product is pressed between hollow metal plates carrying the evaporating refrigerant. - Plates operate at **−35 °C to −40 °C**. - Heat transfer mode: **conduction** (very efficient). - Used for regular-shaped products: fish blocks, meat patties, butter slabs. - Limitation: product must be of uniform thickness (typically ≤ **80 mm**). ### C. Immersion / Brine Freezing - Product is submerged in a non-toxic, low-freezing-point liquid (NaCl brine, **−20 °C**; propylene glycol, **−30 °C**; or sugar solution). - Heat transfer mode: **convection + conduction** — the fastest of the classical systems. - Risk: leaching of soluble solids (salt, sugar, vitamins) from cut surfaces. ### D. Cryogenic Freezing (LN₂ / LCO₂) - Product travels on a conveyor through a tunnel where **liquid nitrogen** sprays/vapour contacts it. - Temperature of LN₂: **−196 °C**; LCO₂: **−78 °C** at 1 atm. - Very fast freezing: 3–10 mm penetration per minute. **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing)** capability. - Energy intensive; cost-effective only for high-margin, high-value foods (berries, shrimp, herbs). ### E. Fluidized Bed Freezing - A specialized form of air blast where upward airflow suspends small product pieces (peas, diced carrots, shrimp) in a **fluidized state**. - Excellent heat transfer coefficient (**h ≈ 50–150 W/m²·K**), giving very rapid freezing. - Particle size limitation: typically **5 mm to 50 mm**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Thermal History of a Freezing Food 1. **Pre-cooling (Sensible Cooling):** Temperature falls from ambient to initial freezing point (Tifp). Heat removed = *m · cp,above · ΔT*. 2. **Ice Nucleation (Phase Change Initiation):** Supercooling to ~ **−2 °C to −5 °C** before ice crystals form. 3. **Latent Heat Removal (Plateau Phase):** Most of the free water (typically **70–80 % of moisture**) converts to ice. Temperature remains nearly constant. This is the **largest energy demand**. 4. **Sub-cooling (Sensible Cooling below 0 °C):** Final cooling to storage temperature (≤ **−18 °C**). Heat removed = *m · cp,frozen · ΔT*. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat to be removed | kJ | | 2 | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (above or below freezing point) | kJ | | 3 | $Q_{total} = m [c_{p,above}(T_i - T_{ifp}) + L_f + c_{p,below}(T_{ifp} - T_f)]$ | Total refrigeration load | kJ | | 4 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{D} + \dfrac{b \cdot D}{2a}\right)$ | **Plank's Equation** for freezing time of an infinite slab | s, min, or h | | 5 | $t_{cylinder} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{D} + \dfrac{b \cdot D}{4a}\right)$ | Plank's Equation for infinite cylinder | s, min, or h | | 6 | $t_{sphere} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{a}{D} + \dfrac{b \cdot D}{6a}\right)$ | Plank's Equation for sphere | s, min, or h | | 7 | $T_{ifp} = -1.86 \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression (Raoult's, dilute) | °C | | 8 | $NRE = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for air flow in freezers | dimensionless | | 9 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (turbulent flow over plates) | dimensionless | | 10 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K | | 11 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of refrigeration cycle | dimensionless | | 12 | $q_{mass} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot L_f}{A}$ | Mass flux based refrigeration load | W/m² | | 13 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_{frozen}}{\rho \cdot c_{p,frozen}}$ | Thermal diffusivity of frozen food | m²/s | | 14 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k}$ | Biot number (lumped capacitance validity: Bi < 0.1) | dimensionless | | 15 | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t}{L_c^2}$ | Fourier number (dimensionless time) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Constants & Standard Plank Coefficients to Memorize:** > - **Slab:** a = 1/2, b = 1/8 > - **Cylinder:** a = 1/4, b = 1/16 > - **Sphere:** a = 1/6, b = 1/24 > - Lf for water = **333.55 kJ/kg**; for typical food ≈ **250–300 kJ/kg** > - ρfood ≈ **1000 kg/m³** (approximation) > - kfrozen food ≈ **1.0–1.4 W/m·K** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Freezing systems are central to: - **Cold-chain logistics** (sea-food export from Kerala — a KTU-relevant industrial application). - **Frozen ready-meal manufacturing.** - **Cryo-grinding** of spices (downstream powder technology). - **Cryo-concentration** of liquid foods. - **Lyophilization (freeze-drying)** preprocessing. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Freezing Time Equation (Slab Geometry) ### Assumptions (Required for Plank's Model) 1. Constant surface temperature (Ts) — valid for **contact plate freezers** with negligible surface resistance. 2. Uniform initial temperature (Ti) throughout the slab. 3. Latent heat Lf is released only at the freezing front (a sharp interface). 4. Constant thermal conductivity k in the frozen layer. 5. Convective resistance at the surface is **NOT neglected** (unlike pure conduction models). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Geometric Setup:** Consider an infinite slab of half-thickness *D* (total thickness 2D). Let the freezing front be at position *x = ξ* from the centre, with the surface at *x = 0* and centre at *x = D*. **Step 2 — Heat Conducted Through Frozen Layer:** By Fourier's law, the steady conductive heat flux through the frozen layer of instantaneous thickness *(D − ξ)* is: $$ q = \dfrac{k \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_s)}{D - \xi} $$ **Step 3 — Heat Released at Freezing Front:** As the front moves inward by *dξ* in time *dt*, the latent heat released per unit area is: $$ q = \rho \cdot L_f \cdot \dfrac{d\xi}{dt} $$ **Step 4 — Surface Heat Transfer (Convection):** The heat arriving at the surface is convected away: $$ q = h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air}) $$ **Step 5 — Equating Conduction and Latent Release:** $$ \rho \cdot L_f \cdot \dfrac{d\xi}{dt} = \dfrac{k \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_s)}{D - \xi} $$ **Step 6 — Eliminate Ts by Series Resistance (Conduction + Convection):** At steady state, the conduction flux equals the convective flux. Eliminating Ts: $$ T_{ifp} - T_{air} = (T_{ifp} - T_s) + (T_s - T_{air}) = \dfrac{q \cdot (D - \xi)}{k} + \dfrac{q}{h} $$ $$ T_{ifp} - T_{air} = q \left[ \dfrac{D - \xi}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h} \right] $$ **Step 7 — Substitute q from Step 3:** $$ T_{ifp} - T_{air} = \rho \cdot L_f \cdot \dfrac{d\xi}{dt} \left[ \dfrac{D - \xi}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h} \right] $$ **Step 8 — Separate Variables and Integrate from ξ = D to ξ = 0:** $$ \int_{0}^{t_f} dt = \int_{0}^{D} \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f}{T_{ifp} - T_{air}} \left[ \dfrac{D - \xi}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h} \right] d\xi $$ **Step 9 — Evaluate the Integral:** $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f}{T_{ifp} - T_{air}} \left[ \dfrac{D^{2}}{2k} + \dfrac{D}{h} \right] $$ **Step 10 — Express in Plank's Standard Form:** Rearranging, and defining Plank's geometric constants for a slab (*a = 1/2, b = 1/8*): $$ \boxed{\, t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \left( \dfrac{a \cdot h \cdot D}{k} + b \right) \,} $$ or equivalently, with *a = 1/2* and *b = 1/8*: $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{D \cdot h}{8k} \right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** The first term inside the bracket **1/2** represents *convective* resistance (the air-to-surface film). The second term **D·h / 8k** represents the *conductive* resistance of the frozen layer. **If h → ∞** (perfect contact), the second term dominates; **if k → ∞**, the first term dominates. This is why **plate freezers are faster than air-blast freezers** for the same product—eliminating the convective resistance drastically reduces tf. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A slab of fish 6 cm thick (half-thickness *D* = 0.03 m) is to be frozen in an air-blast freezer. The air temperature is **−30 °C**, the convective heat transfer coefficient is **h = 20 W/m²·K**, the initial freezing point is **−1.5 °C**, ρ = **1000 kg/m³**, Lf = **250 kJ/kg**, k (frozen) = **1.2 W/m·K**. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation. ### Solution **Step 1 — Identify Constants for Slab Geometry:** a = 1/2, b = 1/8 **Step 2 — Compute Temperature Difference:** Tifp − Tair = −1.5 − (−30) = **28.5 K** **Step 3 — Compute the Two Resistance Terms:** Term 1 (convective): $$ \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$ Term 2 (conductive): $$ \dfrac{D \cdot h}{8 \cdot k} = \dfrac{0.03 \times 20}{8 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{0.6}{9.6} = 0.0625 $$ **Step 4 — Sum the Bracket:** 0.5 + 0.0625 = **0.5625** **Step 5 — Compute Prefactor:** $$ \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} = \dfrac{1000 \times 250000 \times 0.03}{20 \times 28.5} $$ **Conversion logic:** Lf in kJ/kg → multiply by 1000 to convert to J/kg. $$ = \dfrac{7{,}500{,}000}{570} = 13{,}157.89 \text{ s} $$ **Step 6 — Multiply by Bracket:** $$ t_f = 13{,}157.89 \times 0.5625 = 7401.31 \text{ s} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to Hours:** $$ t_f = \dfrac{7401.31}{3600} \approx \mathbf{2.06 \text{ hours}} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always carry forward at least **4 significant digits** in intermediate steps to avoid cumulative rounding errors. Board examiners award full marks only when unit conversions (kJ ↔ J, mm ↔ m, s ↔ h) are shown **explicitly**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mechanical Freezing System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[COMPRESSOR
R717 ammonia gas
pressurized hot vapor] -->|high pressure hot gas| nodeB[CONDENSER
heat rejected to
cooling tower water] nodeB -->|high pressure liquid| nodeC[EXPANSION VALVE
Joule Thomson
pressure drop] nodeC -->|low pressure
low temp liquid vapor mix| nodeD[EVAPORATOR
COIL INSIDE FREEZER
absorbs heat from food] nodeD -->|low pressure vapor| nodeA nodeE[FOOD PRODUCT ON
TRAY OR BELT] -.->|sensible plus latent heat| nodeD nodeF[CONTROL PANEL
temperature sensor
thermostat] -.->|monitors and regulates| nodeC nodeG[FAN UNIT
axial blower] -.->|cold air circulation| nodeE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** This is a **vapour-compression refrigeration cycle** (VCRS) — the heart of every mechanical freezer. Heat is "pumped" from a low-temperature region (the freezer interior) to a high-temperature region (the ambient air via the cooling tower) by doing work in the compressor. The expansion valve is the only component that actually creates the cold; everything else merely moves the refrigerant around. ## 4.2 Comparative Process Flow — Five Freezing Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[FOOD INPUT
fresh or pre cooled] --> splitA{Choose Freezing System} splitA -->|option A| airBlast[Air Blast Tunnel
cold air minus 35C
forced convection] splitA -->|option B| plateF[Plate Freezer
direct contact with
evaporator plates] splitA -->|option C| immerF[Immersion Freezer
brine or glycol bath
minus 20C to minus 30C] splitA -->|option D| cryoF[Cryogenic Tunnel
LN2 spray minus 196C
or LCO2 minus 78C] splitA -->|option E| fluidF[Fluidized Bed Freezer
upward cold air
suspends particles] airBlast --> endOut[STORAGE
minus 18C cold room] plateF --> endOut immerF --> endOut cryoF --> endOut fluidF --> endOut ``` ## 4.3 Temperature Profile Inside a Freezing Slab (Plank's Conceptual Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSurf[SURFACE
Ts minus 30C] --> nodeFz[ICE FRONT
Tifp minus 1.5C
moving inward] nodeFz --> nodeCent[CENTRE
Tinitial plus 5C
last point to freeze] nodeSurf -.->|frozen zone
conduction k| nodeFz nodeFz -.->|unfrozen zone
still liquid| nodeCent ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The thermal resistance lies **entirely in the frozen layer** (because the unfrozen zone is mostly water-like with very low resistance). This is why as freezing proceeds and the frozen shell thickens, the freezing rate slows down — a phenomenon KTU examiners often test by asking *"Why does the last 10 % of freezing take as long as the first 50 %?"* # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Define a *Freezing System*. List the four main methods of food freezing used in industry. **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** A freezing system is an engineering assembly of equipment that removes heat from food to convert its free water into ice, lowering the product temperature below its initial freezing point (typically −1 °C to −4 °C) and maintaining it at a storage temperature of −18 °C or lower. > **Four Industrial Methods:** > 1. Air-blast freezing (forced cold air convection) > 2. Plate freezing (conduction through chilled plates) > 3. Immersion freezing (cold liquid bath) > 4. Cryogenic freezing (LN₂ or LCO₂ direct contact) *(Marks Distribution: Definition 1M, List 2M = 3M)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** State Plank's equation for freezing time of an infinite slab and explain the significance of the two terms inside the bracket. **Model Answer:** > **Plank's Equation for Infinite Slab:** > $$t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{D \cdot h}{8k} \right)$$ > **Significance of the Two Terms:** > - **Term 1 (1/2):** Represents the *convective heat-transfer resistance* at the air-to-product surface film. > - **Term 2 (D·h / 8k):** Represents the *conductive resistance* of the frozen layer between the surface and the moving ice front. *(Marks Distribution: Equation 1M, Significance 2M = 3M)* ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Describe in detail the construction, working principle, advantages, limitations, and typical applications of an **Air-Blast Freezer** used in food processing industries. State the typical operating parameters and explain why air-blast freezers dominate frozen-food plants worldwide. **Model Answer — Section A (a) — 7 Marks:** **(i) Construction:** - Insulated rectangular tunnel or cabinet with **polyurethane foam (PUF) insulation, 80–100 mm thick** (thermal conductivity ≈ 0.02 W/m·K). - **Axial or centrifugal fans** mounted on the ceiling or side wall to circulate air. - **Fin-and-tube evaporator coils** containing the refrigerant (NH₃ or R-404A), typically at **−35 °C to −40 °C**. - Product trolleys, trays, or a continuous conveyor belt. - Air-distribution baffles/plenum to ensure uniform velocity profile. - Temperature sensors and a digital controller for −18 °C to −40 °C set-points. **(ii) Working Principle:** - Refrigerant evaporates inside the coil at low pressure, absorbing heat from the surrounding air. - Fans force the chilled air across the product at **1.5–6 m/s** in cross-flow. - Heat flows from product → air (convection) → coil (conduction) → refrigerant (latent heat of vaporization) → compressor (work input) → condenser (heat rejection to ambient). - The product cools through sensible + latent stages until its centre reaches −18 °C. **(iii) Typical Operating Parameters:** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Air temperature | **−30 °C to −40 °C** | | Air velocity | **1.5 m/s to 6 m/s** | | Product temperature at exit | **−18 °C** | | Heat transfer coefficient (h) | **10–30 W/m²·K** | **Model Answer — Section A (b) — 7 Marks:** **(iv) Advantages:** - Versatile — handles irregular shapes, large volumes, packaged and unpackaged products. - Hygienic and dry operation (no brine contamination). - Continuous or batch operation possible. - Lower capital cost than cryogenic systems. - Easy to retrofit into existing cold rooms. **(v) Limitations:** - Slowest among conventional methods (high convective resistance, low h). - Large space footprint; surface drying ("freezer burn") if air velocity too high. - High fan-power consumption (often 30–40 % of total electrical load). - Uneven freezing across load (cold spots / warm spots) if air distribution is poor. **(vi) Typical Applications:** - Bulk freezing of meat quarters, fish, poultry. - Frozen vegetable tunnels (peas, beans, cauliflower). - Carton freezing in cold-chain distribution centres. **(vii) Why Air-Blast Dominates:** - **Cost-effectiveness per kg frozen** is unmatched for high-throughput operations. - **Mature technology** with reliable components and skilled labour available. - **Flexibility** in product size, shape, and packaging format. - **Energy efficiency** of modern scroll/screw compressors has improved dramatically. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** A fish fillet of thickness **80 mm** is to be frozen in an air-blast freezer operating at **−35 °C**. The convective heat-transfer coefficient is **h = 25 W/m²·K**. The fish has: ρ = **1050 kg/m³**, Lf = **260 kJ/kg**, kfrozen = **1.3 W/m·K**, initial freezing point Tifp = **−1.2 °C**. Using Plank's equation, calculate the freezing time in hours. State any two assumptions of Plank's model. **Model Answer — Section B (a) — 7 Marks (Calculation):** **Step 1 — Geometric Data:** Slab half-thickness: $$ D = \dfrac{80 \text{ mm}}{2} = 0.04 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Plank's Slab Constants:** a = 1/2, b = 1/8 **Step 3 — Driving Temperature Difference:** $$ T_{ifp} - T_{air} = -1.2 - (-35) = 33.8 \text{ K} $$ **Step 4 — Prefactor Calculation:** $$ \dfrac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot D}{h \cdot (T_{ifp} - T_{air})} = \dfrac{1050 \times 260000 \times 0.04}{25 \times 33.8} $$ **Conversion logic:** Lf = 260 kJ/kg = 260,000 J/kg. Numerator: 1050 × 260,000 × 0.04 = 10,920,000 Denominator: 25 × 33.8 = 845 Prefactor: $$ = \dfrac{10{,}920{,}000}{845} = 12{,}923.08 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Bracket Calculation:** Convective term: 1/2 = **0.5** Conductive term: $$ \dfrac{D \cdot h}{8 \cdot k} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 25}{8 \times 1.3} = \dfrac{1.0}{10.4} = 0.0962 $$ Bracket sum = 0.5 + 0.0962 = **0.5962** **Step 6 — Freezing Time in Seconds:** $$ t_f = 12{,}923.08 \times 0.5962 = 7704.0 \text{ s} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to Hours:** $$ t_f = \dfrac{7704.0}{3600} \approx \mathbf{2.14 \text{ hours}} $$ **Model Answer — Section B (b) — 7 Marks (Assumptions & Discussion):** **Two Assumptions of Plank's Equation:** 1. **Uniform initial temperature** throughout the product before freezing begins. 2. **Latent heat is released only at the moving freezing front** (sharp interface between frozen and unfrozen regions); sensible heat contributions are neglected. 3. (Bonus) **Constant thermal conductivity** of the frozen layer and **constant surface temperature** during freezing. **Engineering Discussion:** - For this fish, the **conductive resistance (0.0962)** is much smaller than the **convective resistance (0.5)**. This indicates that the **air-side film controls the freezing rate** — characteristic of an air-blast system. - **Recommendation:** To reduce tf, either increase air velocity (raise h) or reduce the thickness of the fillet (smaller D). - **Comparison with plate freezer:** A plate freezer eliminates convective resistance (h → ∞), reducing the bracket to 0.0962 alone, making it about **5× faster** for this product. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Freezing-Time Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to halve the slab thickness.** KTU problem statements often give the *full thickness* (e.g., 80 mm). Students must convert to *half-thickness* D = 40 mm = 0.04 m for Plank's equation. **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > 2. **Unit error in Lf.** kJ/kg must be converted to J/kg by multiplying by 1000. Skipping this gives answers **1000× too small or too large**. > 3. **Using Tifp − Tair incorrectly.** If the air temperature is −35 °C, the difference is **33.8 K**, NOT −33.8 K. The equation uses *magnitude* of the temperature difference. > 4. **Using a, b values for the wrong geometry.** Many students memorize a = 1/2, b = 1/8 for slab but apply it to a cylinder. **KTU 2024 explicitly tests this.** > 5. **Mixing up the term "sensible heat" with "latent heat"** in numerical questions. The total refrigeration load includes three parts: pre-cooling sensible, freezing latent, and sub-cooling sensible. Marks are awarded only if all three are identified. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Recap — Memorize Before the Exam:** > - **Definition:** Freezing = sensible + latent + sensible heat removal. > - **Initial Freezing Point of food:** −1 °C to −4 °C (NOT 0 °C). > - **Storage Temperature:** ≤ −18 °C (Codex/FSSAI standard). > - **Four Industrial Methods:** Air-Blast, Plate, Immersion, Cryogenic. > - **Latent Heat of Water:** 333.55 kJ/kg (or 80 kcal/kg). > - **Cryoscopic Constant of Water:** 1.86 °C·kg/mol. > - **Plank's Equation (Slab):** tf = (ρ Lf D) / [h (Tifp − Tair)] × (1/2 + D·h / 8k). > - **Plank's Constants — Slab:** a = 1/2, b = 1/8 — **Cylinder:** a = 1/4, b = 1/16 — **Sphere:** a = 1/6, b = 1/24. > - **COP of Refrigeration System:** COP = Qcooling / Wcompressor. > - **Air-Blast h:** 10–30 W/m²·K. **Fluidized Bed h:** 50–150 W/m²·K. **Plate Freezer h → ∞.** > - **LN₂ temperature:** −196 °C. **LCO₂ temperature:** −78 °C. > - **Freezer Burn:** Caused by sublimation of surface ice at low RH → use proper packaging. > - **Refrigerant for industrial freezers:** Ammonia (R-717) is the most common in food plants (high COP, no ODP/GWP). > - **VCRS Cycle:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → back to Compressor. > - **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing):** Achieved by fluidized bed or cryogenic systems; produces free-flowing non-clumped products. > - **Biot Number Criterion:** Bi < 0.1 → Lumped Capacitance valid; for freezing, Bi is always large → spatial temperature distribution matters. > - **Fast Freezing Advantages:** Smaller ice crystals → better texture, less cell damage, lower drip loss on thawing.

topics.title: Direct Contact Systems, Air Blast Immersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Contact Systems, Air Blast Immersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Direct Contact Systems — Formal Definition

In food process engineering, **Direct Contact Systems** are a class of heat transfer operations in which the cooling or heating medium comes into **physical, intimate contact** with the food surface without any intervening barrier. The two principal industrial variants studied in this module are:

1. **Air Blast Freezing** — Cold air (typically at **−30 °C to −40 °C**) is forced across the product at high velocity (2.5–10 m·s⁻¹).
2. **Immersion Freezing** — The product is directly submerged in a chilled liquid such as **NaCl brine, propylene glycol, or sugar solution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Direct contact systems transfer heat between the refrigerant and the food product through direct interaction. The most widely used methods are air-blast freezers and immersion freezers, classified under Individual Quick Freezing (IQF) technologies.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cold Wind vs. Ice Bath" Intuition

Think of two ways you could cool a hot boiled potato on a winter morning:

- **Air Blast (Cold Wind):** You stand outside where the wind is **−30 °C and blowing at 30 km/h**. The cold wind continuously strips heat from the surface by sweeping away the warm boundary layer. This is *convective* heat removal — effective, but the air must keep moving.
- **Immersion (Ice Bath):** You drop the potato into a tub of **salted ice water**. The liquid physically touches the potato and conducts heat 20–30× faster than air because liquid has 1000× higher density and much higher thermal conductivity.

**In short:** Air blast = *forced convection with a gas*; Immersion = *convective + conductive cooling with a liquid bath*. Both are "direct contact" because **no metal plate** separates refrigerant from food.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics (Recall Values):**
> - Air Blast Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient: **h ≈ 10–100 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**
> - Immersion (Brine) Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient: **h ≈ 500–2000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**
> - This is why immersion freezing is **20–50× faster** than air blast.

## 1.3 Why "Direct Contact" is Critical in Food Engineering

Direct contact systems dominate the **freezing** stage of food preservation because:

- The product is **pre-shaped or portioned** (peas, shrimp, berries, diced meat).
- High throughput is required (tons per hour).
- **Ice nucleation control** is possible — small, fast crystals preserve cell structure.
- The freezing rate determines **drip loss, texture, and color** post-thaw.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking the *comparison* between air blast and immersion. Memorize the 3 C's: **Contact area, Conductivity of medium, Convective velocity.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The 5-Step Logic Chain

For an **Air Blast Freezer**, the heat transfer pathway follows this strict sequence:

1. **Refrigerant vaporization** in the evaporator coil absorbs heat (Q_refr) from the secondary coolant (typically an evaporating refrigerant like NH₃, R-404A, or a chilled glycol loop).
2. **Forced air circulation:** Axial or centrifugal fans draw cold air through a ducted plenum at velocities of **V = 2.5–10 m·s⁻¹**.
3. **Convective heat transfer** at the food surface, governed by Newton's Law of Cooling:

   $$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{surface} - T_{air})$$

4. **Internal conduction** through the food from the thermal center to the surface (Fourier's Law).
5. **Latent heat removal** during the phase change (water → ice) — this dominates ~70–80% of the total refrigeration load.

For an **Immersion Freezer**:

1. The liquid refrigerant or secondary coolant is chilled to **−20 °C to −35 °C** in a separate heat exchanger.
2. The chilled liquid is pumped into the freezing tank containing the food.
3. The liquid **completely wets the surface**, eliminating the stagnant air film (no boundary layer resistance).
4. Heat transfers by **natural + forced convection** in the liquid phase.
5. The liquid is continuously circulated, filtered, and re-chilled.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why forced air?** Natural convection in air gives h ≈ 5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹; forced convection at 5 m·s⁻¹ gives h ≈ 30–50 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. A **10× enhancement** for a small fan power input.
- **Why high velocity?** Increases Reynolds number → turbulent flow → thinner thermal boundary layer → higher h.
- **Why immersion is faster?** Liquid thermal conductivity (~0.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ for brine) is **~25× that of air** (~0.02 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹). Plus density is 1000× higher, so convective capacity (ρ·Cp) is 4000× higher.
- **Why use brine?** Brine has a depressed freezing point (eutectic ≈ −21 °C for NaCl) so it stays liquid at sub-zero temperatures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear every year in KTU ESE Part B questions.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Overall convective heat transfer | W |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number (fluid property) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Nu = \dfrac{h D}{k_{air}} = C \cdot Re^m \cdot Pr^{0.33}$ | Nusselt correlation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{k_{frozen}}\right]$ | Plank's equation for freezing time | s |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V \cdot A_{duct}$ | Air mass flow rate | kg·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $P_{fan} = \Delta p \cdot \dot{V} / \eta_{fan}$ | Fan power requirement | W |
| 8 | $h_{brine} \approx 0.35 \cdot k_{brine} \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Pr^{0.33} / D$ | Brine-side h (cylinder) | W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\Delta T = T_f - T_{medium}$ | Driving temperature difference | K |
| 10 | $Q_{load} = m \cdot L_{ice} + m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{pre/post}$ | Refrigeration load | kJ |

**Geometric Constants (P, R) for Plank's Equation:**

| Geometry | P | R |
|----------|---|---|
| Infinite slab (thickness D) | 1/2 | 1/8 |
| Infinite cylinder (diameter D) | 1/4 | 1/16 |
| Sphere (diameter D) | 1/6 | 1/24 |

**Standard Air Properties at −30 °C (1 atm):**
- $\rho_{air} \approx 1.45$ kg·m⁻³
- $C_{p,air} \approx 1.005$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $\mu_{air} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $k_{air} \approx 0.022$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $Pr_{air} \approx 0.72$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Air blast tunnels are the workhorse of frozen food industries (Birds Eye, McCain, etc.).
- **Seafood Industry:** Immersion in NaCl brine is standard for shrimp and tuna — preserves color, prevents dehydration.
- **Energy Audits:** Knowing h and Q allows precise sizing of compressors and evaporators (capital cost).
- **Quality Engineering:** Freezing rate (°C·min⁻¹) directly controls ice crystal size — small crystals = less cell damage = better texture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Equation for Freezing Time (High-Yield KTU Derivation)

Plank's equation is the **single most important derivation** in this module. It predicts the time required to freeze an object from its initial temperature down to its center reaching the freezing point.

### Assumptions

1. Uniform initial temperature $T_i$ throughout the food.
2. Constant surface temperature equal to medium temperature (lumped assumption in the convective term is invalid; Plank retained it).
3. Negligible sensible heat above and below freezing (latent heat dominates).
4. Constant thermal conductivity $k_f$ in the frozen layer.
5. One-dimensional heat conduction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the freezing time differential:**

The time to freeze a thin layer of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the surface equals the latent heat removed divided by the heat flux.

$$dQ_{latent} = \rho \cdot L \cdot A \cdot dx$$

**Step 2 — Express the conductive heat flux through the frozen layer (Fourier's Law):**

$$q = \dfrac{k_f \cdot A \cdot (T_f - T_s)}{x}$$

where $T_s$ is the surface temperature (assumed ≈ medium temperature in Plank's model).

**Step 3 — Equate the two (steady-state quasi-equilibrium):**

$$\rho L A \, dx = \dfrac{k_f A (T_f - T_s)}{x} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate from surface to center (x = 0 to x = D/2):**

$$\int_0^{D/2} x \, dx = \dfrac{k_f (T_f - T_s)}{\rho L} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\dfrac{x^2}{2}\bigg|_0^{D/2} = \dfrac{k_f (T_f - T_s)}{\rho L} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 5 — Substitute x = D/2:**

$$\dfrac{(D/2)^2}{2} = \dfrac{D^2}{8} = \dfrac{k_f (T_f - T_s)}{\rho L} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 6 — Solve for t_f (conduction-limited case):**

$$t_{cond} = \dfrac{\rho L D^2}{8 k_f (T_f - T_s)}$$

For other geometries, replace $1/8$ with **R** (the geometric shape factor).

**Step 7 — Add the surface convective resistance term (Plank's full equation):**

The full Plank's equation combines the surface convection and internal conduction in series:

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{k_f} \right]$$

where $\Delta T = T_f - T_{air}$ (or $T_f - T_{brine}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor 1/(T_f − T_s) appears as 1/ΔT** — note that the (T_f − T_s) cancels in the convective term only when surface temperature equals medium temperature. This is Plank's simplifying assumption.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A slab of fish 4 cm thick is to be frozen in an air blast freezer operating at −30 °C. The fish has the following properties:

- Density $\rho = 1050$ kg·m⁻³
- Latent heat $L = 235$ kJ·kg⁻¹
- Freezing point $T_f = -2$ °C
- Thermal conductivity of frozen fish $k_f = 1.8$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹
- Convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 30$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹

For an infinite slab: $P = 1/2$, $R = 1/8$.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$D = 0.04 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta T = T_f - T_{air} = -2 - (-30) = 28 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the convective term:**

$$\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} = \dfrac{(0.5)(0.04)}{30} = \dfrac{0.02}{30} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the conductive term:**

$$\dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{k_f} = \dfrac{(0.125)(0.04)^2}{1.8} = \dfrac{(0.125)(0.0016)}{1.8} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Sum and substitute into Plank's equation:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T} \left[ 6.667 \times 10^{-4} + 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \right]$$

$$t_f = \dfrac{1050 \times 235000}{28} \times \left[ 7.778 \times 10^{-4} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{2.4675 \times 10^8}{28} \times 7.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_f = 8.8125 \times 10^6 \times 7.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 6854 \text{ s} \approx 1.90 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

The convective resistance (6.67×10⁻⁴) is **6× larger** than the conductive resistance (1.11×10⁻⁴). This means **surface convection controls the freezing rate** in this air blast system — increasing air velocity would be the most effective way to speed up freezing.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Transfer Coefficient from Air Side (For a Flat Plate, Turbulent)

For air flowing parallel to a flat slab at velocity V:

$$Re_L = \dfrac{\rho_{air} \cdot V \cdot L_{char}}{\mu_{air}}$$

For $Re_L > 5 \times 10^5$ (turbulent), the Dittus-Boelter type correlation applies:

$$Nu_L = 0.037 \cdot Re_L^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.33}$$

Therefore:

$$h = \dfrac{Nu_L \cdot k_{air}}{L_{char}} = \dfrac{0.037 \cdot k_{air} \cdot Re_L^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.33}}{L_{char}}$$

This h value feeds directly into Plank's equation.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Air Blast vs. Immersion

| Parameter | Air Blast Freezing | Immersion Freezing |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Medium | Cold air (gas) | Brine / glycol / sugar solution (liquid) |
| h range (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) | 10–100 | 500–2000 |
| Medium ΔT range | 30–40 K | 18–30 K |
| Typical freezing time (4 cm slab) | 1.5–3 h | 10–30 min |
| Product suitability | Irregular shapes, packaged | Hermetically sealed, dense |
| Capital cost | Moderate (fans + ducting) | High (corrosion-resistant tanks) |
| Energy efficiency | Lower | Higher (lower ΔT needed) |
| Cross-contamination risk | Low | Medium (requires filtration) |
| Weight loss | Yes (3–5%) | No (liquid seals surface) |
| Crystal size | Medium | Very small (excellent texture) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Air Blast Freezer (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Unit] --> B[Condenser Coil]
    B --> C[Expansion Valve]
    C --> D[Evaporator Coil minus 35 deg C]
    D --> E[Cold Air Plenum Chamber]
    F[Axial Fan Bank] --> E
    E --> G[Product Conveyor Tunnel]
    G --> H[Frozen Product Out]
    G --> I[Warm Product In]
    E --> F
    J[Defrost Cycle] -.-> D
    K[Insulated Enclosure] --- E
    K --- G
    L[Control Panel and Sensors] --> F
    L --> A
    M[Insulation Panels] --- K
```

**Interpretation:** The compressor drives a vapour-compression refrigeration cycle. Cold air is generated in the plenum and circulated by axial fans over the food product moving on a conveyor.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Immersion Freezing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Product In] --> B[Loading Conveyor]
    B --> C[Immersion Tank with Brine]
    C --> D[Stirrer Impeller]
    C --> E[Outlet Conveyor]
    E --> F[Drain Surface and Wash]
    F --> G[Packaging Unit]
    C --> H[External Plate Heat Exchanger]
    I[Refrigeration Unit] --> H
    H --> C
    J[Filter Screen] --> C
    K[Salinity and Temp Sensors] --> H
    K --> I
    C --> L[Sludge Removal Port]
```

**Interpretation:** The food is submerged in cold brine. Heat is removed by an external heat exchanger (not by directly refrigerating the tank, which would cause localized freezing). Continuous circulation and filtration maintain uniform conditions.

## 4.3 Combined Functional Flow — Heat Transfer Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Refrigerant minus 35 C] --> B[Heat Exchanger Wall]
    B --> C[Air or Brine Stream]
    C --> D[Convective Boundary Layer]
    D --> E[Food Surface]
    E --> F[Frozen Layer Conduction]
    F --> G[Thermal Center of Food]
    H[Latent Heat Release at G] --> F
    F --> E
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
```

**Interpretation:** A closed-loop depiction of heat flow from the food's thermal centre (where ice is forming last) all the way back to the refrigerant. The arrows show the two-way flow: heat going outward, refrigerant absorbing it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages of air blast freezing over conventional slow freezing methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Rapid freezing** — High air velocity (5–10 m·s⁻¹) produces h ≈ 30–50 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, leading to small intra-cellular ice crystals (1–5 µm) that minimize cell rupture.
2. **Better product quality** — Less drip loss on thawing, improved texture and color retention, and reduced protein denaturation in meat and fish.
3. **Continuous and scalable** — Suitable for conveyorized IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) operation; high throughput (tonnes per hour) and uniform freezing across the batch.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is immersion freezing generally faster than air blast freezing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher heat transfer coefficient** — Liquid media (brine/glycol) provide h ≈ 500–2000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ compared to air's 10–100 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.
2. **Higher thermal conductivity** — Brine k ≈ 0.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ vs air k ≈ 0.022 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ (≈ 25× higher).
3. **Complete surface contact** — The liquid eliminates the stagnant air boundary layer and surrounds every contour of the product, giving 100% effective contact area.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is critical.** Mixing cm with m in Plank's equation causes a 100× error. Always convert D to metres first.
> 2. **Do not confuse P and R values.** Slab: P = 1/2, R = 1/8. Cylinder: P = 1/4, R = 1/16. Sphere: P = 1/6, R = 1/24.
> 3. **Plank's equation is for *latent* heat only.** If the problem gives sensible heat data, you must add an extra term.
> 4. **Always state the assumption** that $T_s \approx T_{air}$ when using Plank's full equation — this earns a half mark in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Sign convention:** ΔT is always taken as positive in Plank's equation (use absolute difference). Students often put a negative sign and lose a mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive Plank's equation for the freezing time of an infinite slab of food, clearly stating the assumptions. Mention the significance of the geometric constants P and R. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 cm thick slab of meat (ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³, L = 250 kJ·kg⁻¹, T_f = −2 °C, k_f = 1.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) is to be frozen in an air blast at −30 °C with h = 25 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. Calculate the freezing time and identify the rate-controlling resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Statement of assumptions — 2 marks.

1. Initial uniform temperature.
2. Latent heat dominates sensible heat.
3. Constant k in frozen layer.
4. One-dimensional heat flow.
5. $T_s = T_{air}$ (surface temperature equals medium temperature).

Step 2: Set up differential heat balance:

$$\rho L A \, dx = \dfrac{k_f A (T_f - T_s)}{x} \, dt$$

Step 3: Integration:

$$\int_0^{D/2} x \, dx = \dfrac{k_f (T_f - T_s)}{\rho L} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

Step 4: Result for slab:

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L D^2}{8 k_f (T_f - T_s)}$$

Step 5: Inclusion of surface resistance → full Plank's equation:

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P D}{h} + \dfrac{R D^2}{k_f}\right]$$

**Significance of P and R:** P represents the surface-area-to-volume ratio influence (convective term); R represents the geometry's effect on internal conduction path length. For a slab, P = 1/2, R = 1/8; cylinder P = 1/4, R = 1/16; sphere P = 1/6, R = 1/24.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: D = 0.05 m, ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³, L = 2.5 × 10⁵ J·kg⁻¹, T_f = −2 °C, T_air = −30 °C, k_f = 1.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, h = 25 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.

Step 1: ΔT = T_f − T_air = −2 − (−30) = **28 K**

Step 2: Slab constants: P = 0.5, R = 0.125

Step 3: Convective term:

$$\dfrac{P D}{h} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.05}{25} = \dfrac{0.025}{25} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 4: Conductive term:

$$\dfrac{R D^2}{k_f} = \dfrac{0.125 \times (0.05)^2}{1.5} = \dfrac{0.125 \times 0.0025}{1.5} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 5: Sum:

$$1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 2.083 \times 10^{-4} = 1.208 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 6: Prefactor:

$$\dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T} = \dfrac{1000 \times 2.5 \times 10^5}{28} = 8.929 \times 10^6 \text{ s·K·m}^{-2}$$

Step 7: Final t_f:

$$t_f = 8.929 \times 10^6 \times 1.208 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.078 \times 10^4 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 10785 \text{ s} \approx 3.0 \text{ hours}}$$

**Rate-controlling resistance:**
Convective term (1.0×10⁻³) ÷ Conductive term (2.083×10⁻⁴) ≈ **4.8× larger**. Therefore **surface convection is rate-controlling** → increasing air velocity (and thus h) would be the most effective way to reduce freezing time.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of an air blast freezer. List its industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare air blast and immersion freezing on the basis of (i) heat transfer coefficient, (ii) freezing time for a 4 cm beef slab, (iii) product weight loss, and (iv) typical products handled. Calculate the % time saved if the same slab is immersion-frozen at h = 1000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ in brine at −25 °C. Use: ρ = 1050 kg·m⁻³, L = 235 kJ·kg⁻¹, T_f = −2 °C, k_f = 1.4 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Description (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Block Diagram — Air Blast Freezer** in Section 4.1. The functional flow is:

**Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator Coil → Cold Air Plenum → Fan Circulation → Product Tunnel → Frozen Product Out.**

- The compressor (1) circulates refrigerant; the condenser (2) rejects heat; the expansion valve (3) drops pressure; the evaporator coil (4) chills air to −30 °C to −40 °C.
- Axial fans (5) draw the cold air through a plenum (6) and force it across the product (7) on a conveyor (8) inside an insulated tunnel (9).
- Sensors (10) monitor air temperature and product temperature; control panel (11) regulates fan speed and refrigerant flow.
- Defrost cycle (12) periodically removes frost from the evaporator.

**Industrial Applications (3 marks):**
1. Freezing of peas, corn, beans, diced carrots (IQF vegetables).
2. Freezing of shrimp, fish fillets, and chicken portions.
3. Freezing of berries, diced fruits for the dairy and bakery industry.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Air Blast | Immersion |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| h | 10–100 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ | 500–2000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ |
| Freezing time (4 cm slab) | ~1.5–3 h | ~10–30 min |
| Weight loss | 3–5% | < 1% |
| Typical products | Vegetables, fruits, packaged meat | Shrimp, fish, poultry in brine |

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: D = 0.04 m, ρ = 1050 kg·m⁻³, L = 2.35 × 10⁵ J·kg⁻¹, T_f = −2 °C, T_brine = −25 °C, k_f = 1.4 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, h = 1000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, P = 0.5, R = 0.125.

Step 1: ΔT = −2 − (−25) = **23 K**

Step 2: Convective term:

$$\dfrac{0.5 \times 0.04}{1000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

Step 3: Conductive term:

$$\dfrac{0.125 \times 0.0016}{1.4} = 1.429 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 4: Sum = 1.629 × 10⁻⁴ m²·K/W

Step 5: Prefactor:

$$\dfrac{1050 \times 2.35 \times 10^5}{23} = 1.073 \times 10^7$$

Step 6: t_f (immersion):

$$t_{imm} = 1.073 \times 10^7 \times 1.629 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1748 \text{ s} \approx 29.1 \text{ min}$$

Step 7: For the air-blast case (assuming h = 25 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹, T_air = −30 °C):

Convective term: 0.5 × 0.04 / 25 = 8.0 × 10⁻⁴
Conductive term: 1.429 × 10⁻⁴
Sum = 9.429 × 10⁻⁴

ΔT = 28 K
Prefactor: 1050 × 2.35 × 10⁵ / 28 = 8.812 × 10⁶

t_air = 8.812 × 10⁶ × 9.429 × 10⁻⁴ ≈ **8308 s ≈ 138.5 min**

Step 8: % time saved:

$$\% \text{ saved} = \dfrac{138.5 - 29.1}{138.5} \times 100 \approx 79\%$$

**The immersion freezer reduces freezing time by approximately 79% compared to the air blast freezer.**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [ ] **Direct Contact =** No barrier between refrigerant and food surface.
- [ ] **Air Blast** uses cold air at **−30 to −40 °C** and velocity **2.5–10 m·s⁻¹**.
- [ ] **Immersion** uses chilled brine/glycol at **−20 to −35 °C** with h ≈ 1000+ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.
- [ ] **h_air ≈ 10–100**; **h_brine ≈ 500–2000** W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ — the 20–50× gap is the entire reason immersion is faster.
- [ ] **Plank's Equation** (memorize):

  $$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P D}{h} + \dfrac{R D^2}{k_f}\right]$$

- [ ] **Geometric constants:** Slab (P=1/2, R=1/8), Cylinder (P=1/4, R=1/16), Sphere (P=1/6, R=1/24).
- [ ] **Assumption:** $T_s \approx T_{medium}$ — always state this.
- [ ] **Dominant resistance:** Compare PD/h vs RD²/k_f; whichever is larger controls the rate.
- [ ] **Reynolds number for air flow:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ — at 5 m·s⁻¹ over a 10 cm slab with ρ=1.45, μ=1.6×10⁻⁵, $Re \approx 4.5 \times 10^4$ (laminar) to $10^5$ (turbulent).
- [ ] **Nusselt correlation (turbulent):** $Nu = 0.037 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.33}$.
- [ ] **Air properties at −30 °C:** ρ ≈ 1.45 kg·m⁻³, μ ≈ 1.6×10⁻⁵ Pa·s, k ≈ 0.022 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, Pr ≈ 0.72.
- [ ] **Applications:** Air blast → vegetables, fruits, packaged foods; Immersion → shrimp, fish, whole poultry.
- [ ] **Weight loss:** Air blast 3–5% (dehydration); Immersion < 1% (no exposure to dry air).
- [ ] **Refrigeration load** = latent + sensible heat; latent dominates (~80%).
- [ ] **Key dimensionless numbers:** Reynolds (flow), Prandtl (fluid), Nusselt (heat transfer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changes in Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Changes in Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of food process engineering, **Changes in Foods** refer to the systematic alterations—both desirable and undesirable—that occur in the physical, chemical, biochemical, microbiological, nutritional, and sensory attributes of a food matrix when it is subjected to unit operations such as heating, cooling, drying, freezing, mixing, mechanical separation, irradiation, or storage. These transformations dictate the final quality, shelf-life, safety, and consumer acceptability of the processed product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the foundational bridge between *raw material characterization* (Module 1 & 2) and *specific unit operations* (Modules 4 & 5). Examiners frequently test the ability of students to **correlate a specific change with its controlling unit operation.**

### 1.2 Classification of Changes in Foods

The broad taxonomy of changes that food undergoes can be classified as follows:

| **Category** | **Sub-Classification** | **Typical Manifestation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Changes** | Thermal | Phase change, glass transition, crystallization |
| | Mechanical | Size reduction, attrition, agglomeration |
| | Rheological | Viscosity shift, gelation, retrogradation |
| | Optical | Color shift (browning, bleaching), opacity |
| **Chemical Changes** | Browning | Maillard reaction, caramelization |
| | Oxidation | Lipid peroxidation, ascorbic acid degradation |
| | Hydrolysis | Protein hydrolysis, starch hydrolysis |
| | Isomerization | cis–trans isomerization in lipids |
| **Biochemical Changes** | Enzymatic | Polyphenol oxidase (PPO), pectin methyl esterase |
| | Respiratory | Post-harvest respiration in fruits/vegetables |
| **Microbiological Changes** | Spoilage | Putrefaction, mold growth, yeast fermentation |
| | Beneficial | Controlled fermentation in dairy/olives |
| **Nutritional Changes** | Vitamin Loss | Ascorbic acid oxidation, thiamine degradation |
| | Protein | Denaturation, Maillard-induced lysine loss |
| **Sensory Changes** | Flavor | Off-flavor generation, aroma loss |
| | Texture | Hardening, softening, crispness loss |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Banana Story"**
> Imagine a freshly harvested **green banana**. As days pass, it turns yellow, then develops brown spots, and finally becomes black and soft. What you are observing is a sequence of simultaneous changes:
> - **Biochemical:** Chlorophyll → Carotenoid (color change via enzymatic unmasking).
> - **Chemical:** Polyphenol oxidase reacts with cellular phenols → brown melanin pigments.
> - **Physical:** Starch granules hydrolyze to sugars (texture softening, sweetness increase).
> - **Microbiological:** Surface microbes initiate surface rotting.
>
> **Just like the banana, every food is a "living system" that continuously transforms during processing and storage. The food engineer's job is to control the *rate* and *direction* of these changes.**

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Parameters

- **Reaction Rate Constant ($k$):** Measured in $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{min}^{-1}$.
- **Activation Energy ($E_a$):** Measured in **kJ/mol** or **kcal/mol**.
- **Temperature Coefficient ($Q_{10}$):** Dimensionless (typically 2–3 for food reactions).
- **Half-life ($t_{1/2}$):** Measured in **seconds, minutes, hours, or days**.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Measured in **°C or K**.
- **Water Activity ($a_w$):** Dimensionless, range **0 to 1**.
- **pH:** Dimensionless, typical food range **0–7 (acidic)** to **7–14 (alkaline)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Changes in Foods

Physical changes are reversible or irreversible transformations that do **not alter the chemical identity** of the constituents, but modify structural, mechanical, or optical properties.

#### 2.1.1 Thermal Physical Changes
- **Phase Transition:** Water undergoes melting (0 °C), vaporization (100 °C at 1 atm), and sublimation. During freezing, water forms ice crystals whose size and location damage cellular ultrastructure.
- **Glass Transition ($T_g$):** Amorphous foods (e.g., dried fruits, freeze-dried powders) transition from a glassy (rigid) to a rubbery (flexible) state. Storage above $T_g$ accelerates quality loss.
- **Crystallization:** Sugars (e.g., in honey) and lipids (e.g., in chocolate "bloom") can crystallize, altering texture and appearance.

#### 2.1.2 Rheological and Textural Changes
- **Gelation:** Proteins (e.g., egg albumin, gelatin) and polysaccharides (e.g., pectin, starch) form three-dimensional networks, increasing viscosity and firmness.
- **Retrogradation:** Linear amylose chains in gelatinized starch re-associate over time, causing bread staling and syneresis in starch gels.
- **Softening:** Pectin degradation by pectinolytic enzymes in ripening fruits.

#### 2.1.3 Optical (Color) Changes
- **Pigment Degradation:** Chlorophyll → pheophytin (green → olive-brown) under heat/acid.
- **Carotenoid Isomerization:** Heat converts *trans*-carotenoids to *cis*-forms, reducing vitamin A activity.
- **Anthocyanin Bleaching:** Heat and high pH shift red/blue anthocyanins to colorless carbinol pseudo-bases.

#### 2.1.4 Mechanical / Structural Changes
- **Cellular Disruption:** Size reduction (comminution, homogenization) breaks cell walls, releasing enzymes and substrates that accelerate spoilage.
- **Porosity Change:** Drying creates internal pores, increasing surface area and oxygen exposure.

### 2.2 Chemical Changes in Foods

Chemical changes are **non-reversible** and produce new molecular species. They are predominantly responsible for both **desirable** (flavor, color development) and **undesirable** (off-flavors, nutrient loss) quality attributes.

#### 2.2.1 Browning Reactions

**(a) Enzymatic Browning (cold chain disruption):**

The reaction is catalyzed by **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** in the presence of molecular oxygen, converting mono- and di-phenols into *o*-quinones, which polymerize into **melanins** (brown pigments).

$$\text{Phenol} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{PPO}} o\text{-Quinone} \xrightarrow{\text{polymerization}} \text{Melanin (brown)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Methods:** Blanching (heat inactivates PPO), sulphiting ($SO_2$ inhibits PPO), acid treatment (lowers pH below 3.0), and vacuum/O$_{2}$ exclusion.

**(b) Non-Enzymatic Browning:**

Two principal pathways exist:
- **Maillard Reaction:** Amino acid (–NH$_2$) + Reducing sugar (C=O) → Schiff base → Amadori compound → Melanoidins (brown polymers).
- **Caramelization:** Thermal degradation of sugars *without* amino acid involvement, producing diacetyl, furfural, and hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF).

#### 2.2.2 Oxidation Reactions
- **Lipid Oxidation:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination. Peroxides form initially, later decomposing into aldehydes and ketones (rancid off-flavors).
- **Ascorbic Acid Oxidation:** L-Ascorbic acid → Dehydroascorbic acid (loss of vitamin C activity).

#### 2.2.3 Hydrolysis Reactions
- **Protein Hydrolysis:** Peptide bond cleavage by acids, alkalis, or enzymes (proteases) → peptides → free amino acids.
- **Starch Hydrolysis:** $\alpha$- and $\beta$-amylases cleave $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic bonds → maltose → glucose.

### 2.3 Biochemical Changes in Foods

These are changes driven by the food's own **endogenous enzymes**, which remain active post-harvest or post-slaughter until inactivated by heat, pH, or denaturation.

- **Post-Harvest Respiration:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy (heat)}$
- **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME) Activity:** Demethylates pectin → low-methoxyl pectin → Ca$^{2+}$ crosslinking → firming (e.g., in tomato paste).
- **Proteolysis & Lipolysis in Meat:** Endogenous calpains and lipases tenderize meat and generate free fatty acids.

### 2.4 Microbiological Changes

- **Spoilage:** Putrefaction (protein breakdown by anaerobic bacteria), mold growth (aerobic fungi producing mycotoxins), and surface sliming.
- **Beneficial Fermentations:** LAB convert sugars → lactic acid (yogurt, sauerkraut); yeasts convert sugars → ethanol + CO$_2$ (bread, wine).
- **Inactivation Kinetics:** Governed by **D-value** (decimal reduction time) and **z-value** (temperature sensitivity).

### 2.5 Nutritional Changes

- **Vitamin Degradation:** Ascorbic acid (water-soluble, heat-labile, O$_2$-sensitive) is the most labile. Thiamine ($B_1$) is heat-sensitive in alkaline conditions.
- **Protein Quality Loss:** Maillard reaction irreversibly binds lysine, reducing available lysine (a critical KTU point).
- **Mineral Stability:** Generally stable to heat, but can leach into processing water (e.g., blanching).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Application in Food Processing** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Order Reaction Rate** | $-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_0$ | $C$ = concentration (mol/L), $t$ = time (s), $k_0$ = mol/(L·s) | Pigment degradation, non-enzymatic browning at high $a_w$ |
| **First-Order Reaction Rate** | $-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C$ | $k_1$ = s$^{-1}$ | Microbial inactivation, vitamin loss, enzyme inactivation |
| **First-Order Integrated Form** | $\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k_1 t$ | $C_0$ = initial concentration | Quality loss during storage |
| **Half-Life** | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_1} = \frac{0.693}{k_1}$ | seconds | Shelf-life estimation |
| **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = kJ/mol, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = K | Predicting reaction rate at any temperature |
| **$Q_{10}$ Temperature Coefficient** | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\!\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Dimensionless | Rule-of-thumb: rate doubles per 10 °C rise |
| **D-Value (Decimal Reduction)** | $D = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0 / N)}$ | minutes | Sterilization/pasteurization |
| **z-Value** | $z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1 / D_2)}$ | °C | Thermal death time curves |
| **Water Activity Effect** | $\ln\!\left(\frac{k}{k_0}\right) = -m \cdot a_w + c$ | $m$, $c$ = constants | Moisture-sensitivity of reactions |
| **Fick's 1st Law of Diffusion** | $J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J$ = flux (kg/m²·s), $D$ = m²/s | Mass transfer (drying, salting) |
| **Heat Transfer (Conduction)** | $q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$ | $q$ = W, $k$ = W/(m·K) | Thermal processing of solids |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In calculation questions, always **carry units through every step** and **state the order of reaction explicitly** before applying the integrated form.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shelf-Life Modeling:** Predicting expiry dates for packaged foods using Arrhenius kinetics.
- **Process Optimization:** Choosing blanching temperature/time that maximizes PPO inactivation while minimizing vitamin C loss.
- **Food Product Design:** Selecting unit operations (e.g., HTST vs. UHT) that preserve nutritional quality.
- **Quality Control:** Establishing Critical Control Points (CCPs) in HACCP systems based on rate-limiting reactions.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Reaction Kinetics (Vitamin C Degradation)

> **Problem Context:** A fruit juice initially contains **50 mg/100 mL** of ascorbic acid. After storage at 25 °C for **30 days**, the concentration drops to **30 mg/100 mL**. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate:
> (a) The rate constant $k_1$
> (b) The half-life $t_{1/2}$
> (c) The time required for the concentration to fall to **10 mg/100 mL** (legal minimum in many countries).

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the kinetic model.**

For a first-order reaction, the rate of change of concentration is proportional to the concentration itself:

$$
-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

Rearranging, all terms with $C$ on the left and all terms with $t$ on the right:

$$
\frac{dC}{C} = -k_1 \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $C_0$ at $t = 0$ to $C$ at $t = t$.**

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k_1 \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -k_1 t
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k_1 t
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k_1$.**

$$
k_1 = -\frac{1}{t} \ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

Given $C_0 = 50$ mg/100 mL, $C = 30$ mg/100 mL, $t = 30$ days:

$$
k_1 = -\frac{1}{30} \ln\!\left(\frac{30}{50}\right)
$$

$$
k_1 = -\frac{1}{30} \ln(0.6)
$$

$$
k_1 = -\frac{1}{30} \times (-0.5108)
$$

$$
k_1 = 0.01703 \, \text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the half-life.**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_1} = \frac{0.693}{0.01703}
$$

$$
t_{1/2} = 40.69 \, \text{days}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the time to reach 10 mg/100 mL.**

$$
t = -\frac{1}{k_1} \ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -\frac{1}{0.01703} \ln\!\left(\frac{10}{50}\right)
$$

$$
t = -\frac{1}{0.01703} \times \ln(0.2)
$$

$$
t = -\frac{1}{0.01703} \times (-1.6094)
$$

$$
t = 94.51 \, \text{days}
$$

**Final Answer:** $k_1 = 0.01703 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $t_{1/2} \approx 40.7$ days, $t \approx 94.5$ days.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Equation — Temperature Effect on Browning

> **Problem Context:** The rate constant for non-enzymatic browning in a dried milk powder is **$2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$** at 25 °C and **$1.2 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{day}^{-1}$** at 45 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$.

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius equation at two temperatures.**

$$
k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}
$$

$$
k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide the two equations to eliminate $A$.**

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}}{e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}} = e^{\,E_a / R \cdot (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$T_1 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$
$T_2 = 45 + 273.15 = 318.15 \, \text{K}$

**Step 5 — Compute the rate ratio.**

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.8
$$

$$
\ln(4.8) = 1.5686
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the temperature term.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{318.15}
$$

$$
= 0.003354 - 0.003143 = 2.113 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $E_a$.**

$$
E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}
$$

$$
E_a = 8.314 \times \frac{1.5686}{2.113 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
E_a = 8.314 \times 7424.4
$$

$$
E_a = 61{,}723 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 61.7 \, \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpret.** A typical $E_a$ for Maillard browning lies in the range **50–150 kJ/mol**, confirming our result is physically realistic.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: $Q_{10}$ Rule Application

> **Problem Context:** A fresh-cut fruit deteriorates with a $Q_{10} = 2.5$. At 20 °C the shelf-life is **6 days**. Estimate the shelf-life at 5 °C (typical refrigeration temperature).

**Step 1 — Recall the $Q_{10}$ definition.**

$$
Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\!\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert the relationship for shelf-life.** Since shelf-life is inversely proportional to reaction rate ($L \propto 1/k$):

$$
\frac{L_1}{L_2} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = Q_{10}^{\,(T_2 - T_1)/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$T_1 = 20$ °C, $L_1 = 6$ days, $T_2 = 5$ °C, $Q_{10} = 2.5$

$$
L_2 = \frac{L_1}{Q_{10}^{\,(T_1 - T_2)/10}} = \frac{6}{2.5^{\,(20 - 5)/10}}
$$

$$
L_2 = \frac{6}{2.5^{1.5}} = \frac{6}{3.952}
$$

$$
L_2 = 1.518 \, \text{days} \;\text{?}
$$

> **Correction (Re-check):** The above gives a *shorter* shelf-life at *lower* temperature, which is physically wrong. The correct application is: the *rate* at the higher temperature is $Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$ times faster; therefore, shelf-life at the lower temperature is **longer**.

**Corrected Solution:**

$$
L_2 = L_1 \times Q_{10}^{\,(T_1 - T_2)/10}
$$

$$
L_2 = 6 \times 2.5^{1.5} = 6 \times 3.952 = 23.71 \, \text{days}
$$

**Final Answer:** At 5 °C, shelf-life extends to approximately **23.7 days**, an almost **4-fold increase**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause, Effect, and Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this matrix — it is a direct favorite of KTU 14-mark questions.**

| **Change Type** | **Cause / Mechanism** | **Effect on Food** | **Control Strategy** | **Unit Operation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Enzymatic browning | PPO + O$_2$ + phenols | Brown color, off-flavor | Blanching, sulphites, vacuum | Blanching |
| Non-enzymatic browning | Maillard / caramelization | Brown color, flavor, ↓lysine | Lower T, lower $a_w$, shorter time | HTST, spray drying |
| Lipid oxidation | Free radicals + O$_2$ | Rancidity, off-flavor | Antioxidants, vacuum, N$_2$ flush | Packaging |
| Vitamin C loss | Oxidation, heat | ↓Nutritional value | Exclusion of O$_2$, low T | Aseptic processing |
| Starch gelatinization | Heat + water | ↑Viscosity, gelation | Required in many products | Cooking, baking |
| Starch retrogradation | Cooling + time | Staling, syneresis | Use modified starches | Refrigeration |
| Pectin hydrolysis | Pectinases | Softening, juice loss | Thermal inactivation | Blanching |
| Protein denaturation | Heat, acid, shear | Texture change, ↑digestibility | Intentional in many foods | Cooking, baking |
| Microbial spoilage | Bacteria, yeasts, molds | Off-odor, slime, toxins | Reduce $a_w$, pH control, heat | Pasteurization |
| Texture softening in storage | Cell wall degradation | Loss of crispness/firmness | Calcium treatment, refrigeration | Blanching + CaCl$_2$ |
