## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between enzymatic and non-enzymatic browning in foods. Give one example of each and one control method."**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Parameter** | **Enzymatic Browning** | **Non-Enzymatic Browning** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Catalyst** | Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) enzyme | Heat alone (no enzyme) |
> | **Substrate** | Phenolic compounds | Reducing sugars + amino acids (Maillard) or sugars alone (caramelization) |
> | **Trigger** | Tissue damage + O$_2$ | Heat, low $a_w$, long storage |
> | **Example** | Cut apple turning brown | Toast browning, bread crust |
> | **Control** | Blanching, sulphites, lemon juice | Reduce T, time, $a_w$; use UHT |
>
> **(1 mark)** for each clear distinction; **(1 mark)** for example + control.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What is the $Q_{10}$ rule? A food spoils in 4 days at 30 °C. If $Q_{10} = 2$, estimate its shelf-life at 10 °C."**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> $Q_{10}$ is the factor by which the rate of a chemical/biological reaction increases for every **10 °C rise** in temperature. (1 mark)
>
> $$
> L_2 = L_1 \times Q_{10}^{\,(T_1 - T_2)/10} = 4 \times 2^{(30-10)/10}
> $$
>
> $$
> L_2 = 4 \times 2^{2} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \, \text{days}
> $$
>
> **(1 mark)** formula; **(1 mark)** substitution and answer = **16 days**.

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify the various changes that occur in foods during processing and storage. Discuss in detail the **physical changes** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The rate constant for vitamin C degradation in a citrus juice is **$5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$** at 4 °C and **$4.0 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{day}^{-1}$** at 25 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol.
   (ii) The half-life of vitamin C at 4 °C.
   (iii) The shelf-life if the legal minimum vitamin C is 20 % of the initial concentration. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution to Question A(b):**
>
> **(i) Activation Energy:**
>
> $$
> \ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
> $$
>
> $T_1 = 277.15 \, \text{K}$, $T_2 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$
>
> $$
> \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-2}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 8
> $$
>
> $$
> \ln(8) = 2.0794
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{1}{277.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003608 - 0.003354 = 2.539 \times 10^{-4}
> $$
>
> $$
> E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 2.0794}{2.539 \times 10^{-4}} = 68{,}082 \, \text{J/mol} \approx 68.1 \, \text{kJ/mol}
> $$
>
> **(3 marks)**
>
> **(ii) Half-life at 4 °C:**
>
> $$
> t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_1} = \frac{0.693}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 138.6 \, \text{days}
> $$
>
> **(2 marks)**
>
> **(iii) Shelf-life at 20 % of initial:**
>
> $$
> t = -\frac{1}{k_1}\ln(0.20) = -\frac{1}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} \times (-1.6094)
> $$
>
> $$
> t = 321.9 \, \text{days}
> $$
>
> **(2 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Maillard reaction** with its three principal stages, chemical mechanism, and significance in food processing. Discuss its impact on the **nutritional quality** of foods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The activation energy for non-enzymatic browning in a tomato paste is **110 kJ/mol**. The rate constant at 25 °C is **$3.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{day}^{-1}$**. Calculate:
   (i) The pre-exponential factor $A$.
   (ii) The rate constant at 50 °C.
   (iii) The expected shelf-life (in days) at 50 °C for the browning index to reach 5 % of its maximum. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution to Question B(b):**
>
> **(i) Pre-exponential factor $A$:**
>
> $$
> A = k_1 \cdot e^{E_a / (R T_1)} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times e^{\frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 298.15}}
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 298.15} = \frac{110{,}000}{2478.8} = 44.38
> $$
>
> $$
> A = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times e^{44.38} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.79 \times 10^{19}
> $$
>
> $$
> A = 5.37 \times 10^{15} \, \text{day}^{-1}
> $$
>
> **(3 marks)**
>
> **(ii) Rate constant at 50 °C:**
>
> $T_2 = 323.15 \, \text{K}$
>
> $$
> k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)} = 5.37 \times 10^{15} \times e^{-\frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 323.15}}
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 323.15} = \frac{110{,}000}{2686.5} = 40.95
> $$
>
> $$
> k_2 = 5.37 \times 10^{15} \times e^{-40.95} = 5.37 \times 10^{15} \times 1.78 \times 10^{-18}
> $$
>
> $$
> k_2 = 9.56 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}
> $$
>
> **(2 marks)**
>
> **(iii) Shelf-life at 50 °C for 5 % browning:**
>
> $$
> t = -\frac{1}{k_2}\ln(0.05) = -\frac{1}{9.56 \times 10^{-3}} \times (-2.996)
> $$
>
> $$
> t = 313.4 \, \text{days}
> $$
>
> **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting temperature unit conversion:** $T$ in Arrhenius must be in **Kelvin**, not °C. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing rate constant units:** Use **day⁻¹** consistently; do not mix minutes and hours.
> 3. **Ignoring the order of reaction:** A first-order assumption must be stated, especially for vitamin and microbial loss problems.
> 4. **Sign errors in logarithms:** When computing $t$ for a fraction remaining, remember $\ln(C/C_0)$ is **negative**.
> 5. **No units in final answers:** Always attach units (days, kJ/mol, s⁻¹).
> 6. **Skipping mechanism steps in Maillard questions:** Examiners expect the **three stages** (Schiff base → Amadori → Melanoidin), not just a single sentence.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Changes in Foods**

- [x] **Five major categories:** Physical, Chemical, Biochemical, Microbiological, Nutritional.
- [x] **Physical changes** include glass transition ($T_g$), retrogradation, gelatinization, color shifts.
- [x] **Enzymatic browning** is catalyzed by PPO; controlled by blanching, SO$_2$, acid, O$_2$ exclusion.
- [x] **Non-enzymatic browning** has two routes: Maillard (sugar + amine) and Caramelization (sugar alone).
- [x] **Maillard reaction** proceeds in 3 stages; it **reduces available lysine** (nutritional loss).
- [x] **Lipid oxidation** follows initiation → propagation → termination; produces off-flavor aldehydes.
- [x] **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** is the most labile vitamin; degraded by O$_2$, heat, light.
- [x] **First-order kinetics** is the *default* assumption for vitamin loss, microbial inactivation, and enzyme inactivation.
- [x] **Zero-order kinetics** is used for **pigment degradation** and browning at high $a_w$.
- [x] **Arrhenius equation** relates $k$ to $T$ via $E_a$; $E_a$ is always in **J/mol or kJ/mol**.
- [x] **$Q_{10}$ rule:** rate doubles (approximately) per 10 °C rise; typical food $Q_{10}$ is **2 to 3**.
- [x] **D-value** = time for 90 % microbial inactivation at constant $T$; **z-value** = °C change for 10-fold change in D.
- [x] **Water activity ($a_w$)** is the *master variable* for microbial and chemical stability; below $a_w$ 0.6, no microbial growth.
- [x] **Pectin methyl esterase (PME)** activity leads to firming (e.g., tomatoes) when followed by Ca$^{2+}$ addition.
- [x] **Respiration rate** is critical in post-harvest storage of fruits and vegetables.
- [x] **Protein denaturation** is *desirable* in baking (gluten network) and egg coagulation, but causes texture loss in surimi.
- [x] **Freezing damage** is caused by intra- vs. extra-cellular ice crystal formation; rapid freezing produces small intracellular crystals.
- [x] **Crystallization** in sugars and lipids causes defects like "bloom" in chocolate and "graining" in honey.
- [x] **Always convert °C to K** before any Arrhenius calculation.
- [x] **Always carry units** through every derivation step.
- [x] **Mention order of reaction** explicitly in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frozen Food Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frozen Food Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Frozen Food Properties?

**Frozen Food Properties** refer to the physical, thermal, chemical, and structural characteristics of food materials that govern their behaviour, stability, and quality during the freezing process and subsequent frozen storage. These properties dictate how water transforms into ice, how heat and mass transfer occur, and how the product's nutritional and sensory attributes are preserved or altered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Framing)**
> Frozen food properties encompass the thermophysical (thermal conductivity, specific heat, density), thermochemical (latent heat, freezing point), structural (ice crystal morphology, glass transition), and quality attributes (texture, colour, water activity) that collectively determine the suitability, efficiency, and shelf-life of a frozen food system.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Frozen Food Property Science

| Pillar | Property Domain | Engineering Significance |
|--------|----------------|-------------------------|
| **Thermophysical** | Thermal conductivity, specific heat, density | Determines freezing rate and energy requirement |
| **Phase-Change** | Freezing point, latent heat, glass transition (Tg) | Governs ice formation kinetics |
| **Quality** | Texture, colour, water activity (aw), nutrients | Controls consumer acceptability & shelf-life |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bubbling Pond" Intuition

Imagine a **frozen lake in winter** versus a **warm tropical pond**. The lake's surface ice acts like a "frozen food matrix," where:
- The **ice crystals** are like tiny, rigid "cages" that lock the dissolved minerals, salts, and organic molecules in place — similar to how freezing immobilises solutes in food.
- The **unfrozen water pockets** between the ice crystals are like the still-liquid zones in a frozen fruit, where sugars and enzymes remain chemically active.
- The **glass transition** is analogous to the moment honey (a viscous liquid) cools into a brittle candy — a sudden shift from "soft and flowing" to "hard and frozen."

> [!NOTE]
> In food freezing, **water** is the protagonist. It comprises **60–95%** of most foods, and the destiny of this water (ice vs. liquid vs. glassy) dictates the product's entire quality trajectory.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Must Memorise for KTU Exams)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Latent heat of fusion of water | **Lf** | **334** | kJ/kg |
| Specific heat of water | Cp,w | **4.18** | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat of ice | Cp,ice | **2.10** | kJ/kg·K |
| Density of water (4°C) | ρw | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Density of ice (0°C) | ρice | **917** | kg/m³ |
| Cryoscopic constant of water | Kf | **1.86** | °C·kg/mol |
| Freezing point of pure water | Tf° | **0.00** | °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Critical Frozen Food Properties

### **Property 1: Freezing Point (Tf)**
- Pure water freezes at **0°C**, but food water freezes at **lower temperatures** because of dissolved solutes.
- The freezing point depression is governed by **Raoult's Law** and the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation**.
- For typical foods, the **initial freezing point** lies between **−0.5°C to −3°C**.

**Why it matters:** Determines the temperature at which ice first nucleates. Sets the lower bound of the "freezing zone."

### **Property 2: Latent Heat of Freezing (L)**
- The amount of heat (in kJ/kg) that must be removed to convert liquid water to ice at the freezing point.
- Pure water: **L = 334 kJ/kg**.
- For foods, the **effective latent heat** is less than 334 kJ/kg because only a fraction of the water freezes at a given temperature.

**Engineering Utility:** Directly used to size refrigeration compressors and design blast freezer capacity.

### **Property 3: Specific Heat Capacity (Cp)**
- The heat required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of food by 1 K.
- **Before freezing:** Cp ≈ 3.5–4.0 kJ/kg·K (water-dominated).
- **After freezing:** Cp ≈ 1.8–2.1 kJ/kg·K (ice-dominated).
- **During freezing:** Cp is **apparently infinite** (because of latent heat release); the **apparent specific heat** concept is used.

### **Property 4: Thermal Conductivity (k)**
- Ability of food to conduct heat.
- **k_frozen > k_unfrozen** because ice conducts heat ~4× better than water.
- Typical values: **0.5–1.5 W/m·K** for frozen foods, **0.4–0.6 W/m·K** for unfrozen.

**Why it matters:** Higher k → faster heat removal → smaller ice crystals → better quality.

### **Property 5: Density (ρ)**
- Mass per unit volume. **Ice is less dense than water** (ρ_ice = 917 kg/m³, ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³), so frozen foods **expand ~9% in volume**.
- Expansion causes **mechanical stress** in packaging and **cellular damage** in tissues.

### **Property 6: Water Activity (aw)**
- Defined as the **ratio of vapour pressure of food to that of pure water** at the same temperature.
- At the freezing point, **aw = 1.0** (liquid water is in equilibrium with ice).
- Below freezing, **aw decreases exponentially** with temperature:

$$a_w = \exp\left(\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right)$$

where **R = 8.314 J/mol·K** is the universal gas constant and **T** is absolute temperature (K).

## 2.2 Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) — The Hidden Property

The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature below which the amorphous (non-crystalline) food matrix transitions from a **rubbery, flexible** state to a **glassy, brittle** state. Below Tg, molecular mobility is so restricted that deteriorative reactions (enzymatic, oxidative) virtually cease.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tg matters in KTU exams:** Frozen foods are stable only if **storage temperature < Tg**. Above Tg, even if the food is "frozen," deteriorative reactions proceed, leading to **recrystallisation, freezer burn, and texture collapse**.

**Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) Equation** (predicts rate of change of properties near Tg):

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta}{\eta_g}\right) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

where:
- η = viscosity at temperature T
- ηg = viscosity at Tg (~10¹² Pa·s)
- **C₁ ≈ 17.44** and **C₂ ≈ 51.6 K** (universal constants for foods)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression | °C |
| 2 | $L_{eff} = L_f \cdot X_w \cdot \frac{T_f - T}{T_f}$ | Effective latent heat (Heldman) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $a_w = \exp\left(\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right)$ | Water activity in frozen state | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $k_{frozen} = k_{ice} \cdot X_{ice} + k_{unfrozen} \cdot (1 - X_{ice})$ | Thermal conductivity (mass-fraction rule) | W/m·K |
| 5 | $\rho_{frozen} = \frac{1}{\frac{X_{ice}}{\rho_{ice}} + \frac{X_{u}}{\rho_{u}}}$ | Density of frozen food (reciprocal rule) | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $t = \frac{\rho L}{T_f - T_\infty}\left(\frac{P D}{h \Delta T} + \frac{R D^2}{k}\right)$ | **Plank's Equation** for freezing time | seconds |
| 7 | $X_{ice} = \frac{C_p(L - L_f)(T - T_f)}{L^2}$ | Ice fraction (Schwartzberg) | Fraction |
| 8 | $T_g \approx T_g^{water} \cdot w_{water} + \sum T_g^i \cdot w_i$ | **Gordon-Taylor** for Tg prediction | °C or K |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vertical bars in `$\vert x \vert$` notation are intentionally replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. The formula table above uses raw math delimiters safely.

## 2.4 Plank's Equation — The Crown Jewel of Freezing Time Prediction

Plank's equation (1913) is the **most cited** equation in frozen food engineering. It predicts freezing time assuming:
- Food is a slab of thickness **2D** (half-thickness D).
- All water freezes at a single temperature (initially at Tf).
- Constant surface heat transfer coefficient **h**.
- Constant thermal conductivity **k**.

$$t_{freezing} = \frac{\rho L}{T_f - T_\infty}\left(\frac{P \cdot D}{h \cdot \Delta T} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{k}\right)$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| ρ | Density | 900–1050 kg/m³ |
| L | Latent heat | 200–335 kJ/kg |
| Tf | Freezing point | −1 to −3°C |
| T∞ | Refrigerant temperature | −30 to −40°C |
| ΔT | Tf − T∞ | 27–40°C |
| D | Half-thickness | 0.01–0.1 m |
| h | Heat transfer coefficient | 10–500 W/m²K |
| k | Thermal conductivity | 0.5–1.5 W/m·K |
| P, R | Plank's geometric constants | P=1/2, R=1/8 (infinite slab) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Plank's equation is the basis of **refrigerated truck loading calculations**, **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) tunnel design**, and **blast freezer capacity planning** in the food industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Freezing Point Depression for a Food Solution

Consider a food containing dissolved sugars, salts, and proteins. The chemical potential of water in solution equals that of pure water at the freezing point. Applying the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** to a dilute ideal solution:

**Starting Equation (Clausius-Clapeyron):**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L_f}{T \cdot \Delta v}$$

For a dilute solution, the vapour pressure of solvent (water) above the solution follows **Raoult's Law**:

$$P_{solution} = X_{water} \cdot P_{water}^\circ$$

**Integration from pure water (Tf°) to solution (Tf):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{solution}}{P_{water}^\circ}\right) = -\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ}\right)$$

Since $X_{water} \approx 1 - X_{solute}$ and $\ln(1 - X_{solute}) \approx -X_{solute}$ for dilute solutions:

$$-X_{solute} = -\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ}\right)$$

**Linearising** around Tf° (≈ 273 K) using $\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ} \approx \frac{T_f^\circ - T_f}{(T_f^\circ)^2}$:

$$X_{solute} = \frac{L_f \cdot (T_f^\circ - T_f)}{R \cdot (T_f^\circ)^2}$$

Solving for **ΔTf = Tf° − Tf**:

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{R \cdot (T_f^\circ)^2}{L_f} \cdot X_{solute}$$

Substituting molality **m** (mol solute per kg solvent) and noting $X_{solute} \approx \frac{m \cdot M_w}{1000}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m}$$

where the **cryoscopic constant** is:

$$K_f = \frac{R \cdot (T_f^\circ)^2 \cdot M_w}{1000 \cdot L_f} = \frac{8.314 \times 273^2 \times 0.018}{1000 \times 334 \times 10^3} \approx 1.86 \; °C \cdot kg/mol$$

**Numerical Verification:**

A fruit juice contains **0.5 mol/kg** of dissolved sugars. Find its freezing point.

**Step 1:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m = 1.86 \times 0.5 = 0.93°C$

**Step 2:** $T_f = T_f^\circ - \Delta T_f = 0 - 0.93 = -0.93°C$

**Result:** The juice freezes at **−0.93°C** instead of 0°C. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Latent Heat of a Food (Heldman, 1974)

Only a portion of water in food freezes as the temperature drops below Tf. Let **Xw** be the initial water mass fraction. The fraction of water frozen at temperature **T** is:

$$X_{ice}(T) = \frac{X_w \cdot C_{p,water} \cdot (T_f - T)}{L_f + C_{p,water}(T_f - T)}$$

This is derived by equating the heat removed from unfrozen water (Cp·ΔT) to the latent heat of the ice formed, plus the sensible cooling of ice.

**The effective latent heat** (used in Plank's equation) is:

$$L_{eff} = L_f \cdot X_w \cdot \frac{(T_f - T)}{(T_f - T) + \frac{L_f}{C_{p,water}}}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Beef contains **Xw = 0.72** and **Cp,water = 4.18 kJ/kg·K**. If stored at **T = −20°C** and initial freezing point **Tf = −1.5°C**:

**Step 1:** Compute $T_f - T = -1.5 - (-20) = 18.5°C$

**Step 2:** Compute $\frac{L_f}{C_{p,water}} = \frac{334}{4.18} = 79.9°C$

**Step 3:** Numerator = $0.72 \times 18.5 = 13.32$
Denominator = $18.5 + 79.9 = 98.4$

**Step 4:** $L_{eff} = 334 \times \frac{13.32}{98.4} = 334 \times 0.1354 = 45.2 \; kJ/kg$

**Interpretation:** Only **~45 kJ/kg** must be removed to freeze the remaining water at −20°C, even though 334 kJ/kg is the theoretical maximum. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Water Activity Below Freezing Point

Starting from thermodynamic equilibrium between pure water ice and unfrozen water in the food:

$$\mu_{ice}(T) = \mu_{water}^{solution}(T)$$

Using the **chemical potential** of the solution:

$$\mu_{water}^{solution} = \mu_{water}^\circ + RT \ln a_w$$

And the **Gibbs-Helmholtz** relation for the ice phase:

$$\mu_{ice}(T) = \mu_{water}^\circ(T) - L_f\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_f}\right)$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$\boxed{a_w = \exp\left(\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right)}$$

**Numerical Example:** 

Orange juice with **Tf = −1.5°C (271.5 K)** stored at **T = −20°C (253 K)**. Find aw.

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{271.5} - \frac{1}{253}$

$$= 0.003683 - 0.003953 = -0.000270 \; K^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $\frac{L_f}{R} = \frac{334000}{8.314} = 40173$

$$40173 \times (-0.000270) = -10.85$$

**Step 3:** $a_w = \exp(-10.85) = 1.92 \times 10^{-5}$

**Interpretation:** At −20°C, aw ≈ **0.00002**, so low that virtually all microbial and enzymatic activity is suppressed. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Freezing Time Calculation Workflow

| Step | Action | Governing Equation | Output |
|------|--------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | Determine initial freezing point (Tf) | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | °C |
| 2 | Calculate ice fraction at storage T | $X_{ice} = \frac{C_p (T_f - T)}{L_f + C_p(T_f - T)}$ | fraction |
| 3 | Compute effective latent heat | $L_{eff} = L_f \cdot X_w \cdot \frac{T_f - T}{(T_f - T) + L_f/C_p}$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Estimate frozen thermal conductivity | $k = k_{ice} X_{ice} + k_u (1 - X_{ice})$ | W/m·K |
| 5 | Apply Plank's equation | $t = \frac{\rho L}{T_f - T_\infty}\left(\frac{P D}{h \Delta T} + \frac{R D^2}{k}\right)$ | seconds |
| 6 | Validate against glass transition Tg | $T_{storage} < T_g - 10°C$ (rule of thumb) | Pass/Fail |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Ice Crystal Formation Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food enters freezer at T above Tf] --> B[Cooling phase: sensible heat removal]
    B --> C{Temperature reaches Tf?}
    C -->|No| D[Continue cooling to Tf]
    D --> C
    C -->|Yes| E[Nucleation phase: ice embryos form]
    E --> F[Supercooling: T below Tf for short time]
    F --> G[Phase change: water to ice releases latent heat]
    G --> H[Crystal growth phase]
    H --> I{Cooling rate?}
    I -->|Fast freezing: 5 to 30 cm/h| J[Many small crystals inside cells]
    I -->|Slow freezing: less than 0.2 cm/h| K[Few large crystals between cells]
    J --> L[Minimal tissue damage, high quality]
    K --> M[Significant tissue damage, low quality]
    L --> N[Storage phase below Tg]
    K --> N
    N --> O[Recrystallisation risk if T fluctuates]
    O --> P[Quality loss: drip loss and texture change]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interrelation of Frozen Food Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TP[Thermophysical Properties]
      A1[Thermal Conductivity k]
      A2[Specific Heat Cp]
      A3[Density rho]
    end
    subgraph PC[Phase Change Properties]
      B1[Freezing Point Tf]
      B2[Latent Heat L]
      B3[Ice Fraction X_ice]
    end
    subgraph ST[Structural Properties]
      C1[Ice Crystal Size]
      C2[Glass Transition Tg]
      C3[Cell Wall Integrity]
    end
    subgraph QL[Quality Properties]
      D1[Water Activity aw]
      D2[Texture Hardness]
      D3[Color Stability]
    end
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    B3 --> D1
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Diagram: Plank's Equation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Inputs: D, h, k, rho, L, Tf, Tinf] --> SPLIT[Split into surface and internal terms]
    SPLIT --> ST1[Surface term: P times D divided by h times deltaT]
    SPLIT --> IT1[Internal term: R times D squared divided by k]
    ST1 --> SUM[Sum both terms]
    IT1 --> SUM
    SUM --> MUL[Multiply by rho times L divided by Tf minus Tinf]
    MUL --> OUT[Output: Freezing time t in seconds]
    OUT --> VAL{Convert to hours}
    VAL -->|Divide by 3600| HR[Output in hours for industrial use]
    VAL -->|Divide by 60| MIN[Output in minutes for batch sizing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `A1`, `B2`) prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid v10 syntax safety. Labels contain **clean uppercase text** only, with no markdown formatting inside quoted strings.

## 4.4 Schematic: Conceptual Map of Freezing Effects on Food Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fresh food at ambient] --> F[Freezing operation]
    F --> F1[Physical changes]
    F --> F2[Chemical changes]
    F --> F3[Microbiological changes]
    F1 --> P1[Ice crystal growth]
    F1 --> P2[Volume expansion 9 percent]
    F1 --> P3[Water migration to extracellular space]
    F2 --> C1[Protein denaturation]
    F2 --> C2[Lipid oxidation]
    F2 --> C3[Vitamin C loss]
    F3 --> M1[Microbial growth inhibited below minus 5C]
    F3 --> M2[Enzyme activity reduced but not stopped]
    P1 --> Q1[Texture softening]
    P3 --> Q2[Drip loss on thawing]
    C2 --> Q3[Rancidity and off flavours]
    M2 --> Q4[Flavour and colour change over time]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define effective latent heat of a food. Why is it less than 334 kJ/kg?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective latent heat (Leff)** of a food is the actual amount of heat that must be removed per kg of food to convert the freezable water fraction into ice at a given storage temperature. It is always **less than 334 kJ/kg** (latent heat of pure water) because:

1. Food contains dissolved solutes (sugars, salts) that depress the freezing point, so **not all water freezes** at the same temperature.
2. As temperature drops below the initial freezing point (Tf), the **unfrozen water fraction** becomes increasingly concentrated with solutes, suppressing further ice formation.
3. Only the water in **unbound form** freezes; bound water (e.g., water bound to proteins) does not freeze even at −40°C.

The governing equation is:

$$L_{eff} = L_f \cdot X_w \cdot \frac{T_f - T}{(T_f - T) + L_f/C_{p,water}}$$

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for three reasons]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] What is the glass transition temperature (Tg)? State its significance in frozen food storage.**

**Model Answer:**

The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature at which the amorphous (non-crystalline) components of food transition from a **rubbery, mobile state** to a **rigid, glassy state**. Below Tg, molecular mobility is so restricted that deteriorative reactions are virtually halted.

**Significance in frozen food storage:**

- **Stability criterion:** A frozen food is **stable only if storage temperature < Tg**. If storage temperature exceeds Tg, the food enters the rubbery state and deteriorative reactions accelerate.
- **Predicts shelf-life:** Foods with high Tg (e.g., sugars, glycerol) can be stored at higher sub-zero temperatures.
- **Prevents recrystallisation:** Below Tg, ice crystal growth and Ostwald ripening are arrested, preserving texture.
- **Rule of thumb:** Maintain storage temperature **at least 10°C below Tg** for optimal quality.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for significance]**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the freezing point depression equation for an aqueous food solution starting from the Clausius-Clapeyron relation. State the values of the cryoscopic constant and initial freezing point of pure water.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fruit juice contains 0.6 mol/kg of dissolved sugars (molecular weight 180 g/mol). Calculate: (i) the freezing point depression, (ii) the freezing point of the juice, and (iii) the water activity at −18°C. Assume Tf° = 0°C and Lf = 334 kJ/kg, R = 8.314 J/mol·K.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Write the Clausius-Clapeyron equation for phase change:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L_f}{T \cdot \Delta v}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Raoult's Law for vapour pressure of solvent above solution: $P_{solution} = X_{water} \cdot P_{water}^\circ$

**Step 3:** Integrate between pure water state (Tf°, 1 atm) and solution state (Tf, 1 atm):

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{solution}}{P_{water}^\circ}\right) = -\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P_{solution}/P_{water}^\circ = X_{water} \approx 1 - X_{solute}$:

$$\ln(1 - X_{solute}) \approx -X_{solute} = -\frac{L_f}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Linearise around Tf° using $\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T_f^\circ} \approx \frac{\Delta T_f}{(T_f^\circ)^2}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_f = \frac{R (T_f^\circ)^2}{L_f} \cdot X_{solute} = K_f \cdot m}$$

**Final values [required for 1 mark]:**
- **Kf = 1.86 °C·kg/mol**
- **Tf° = 273.15 K (0°C)**

**[7 Marks breakdown: 1 for Clausius-Clapeyron + 2 for Raoult's substitution + 2 for linearisation + 1 for Kf derivation + 1 for standard values]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** m = 0.6 mol/kg, Tf° = 273.15 K, T = −18°C = 255.15 K, Lf = 334,000 J/kg, R = 8.314 J/mol·K

**(i) Freezing point depression [2 Marks]:**

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m = 1.86 \times 0.6 = 1.116°C$$

**(ii) Freezing point of juice [2 Marks]:**

$$T_f = T_f^\circ - \Delta T_f = 0 - 1.116 = -1.12°C$$

**(iii) Water activity at −18°C [3 Marks]:**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin: Tf = 273.15 − 1.12 = 272.03 K, T = 255.15 K

Compute $\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T}$:

$$= \frac{1}{272.03} - \frac{1}{255.15} = 0.003676 - 0.003919 = -2.43 \times 10^{-4} \; K^{-1}$$

Compute $\frac{L_f}{R} = \frac{334000}{8.314} = 40173 \; K$

Exponent: $40173 \times (-2.43 \times 10^{-4}) = -9.762$

$$a_w = \exp(-9.762) = 5.78 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Result:** The juice at −18°C has **aw ≈ 5.78 × 10⁻⁵**, ensuring excellent microbial and enzymatic stability. ✓

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 + 2 + 3]**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of the six key thermophysical properties of frozen foods. How do they change when a food transitions from the unfrozen to the frozen state?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4 cm thick slab of fish (Tf = −2°C, ρ = 1050 kg/m³, L = 240 kJ/kg, k = 1.2 W/m·K) is to be frozen in a blast freezer where the air temperature is −35°C and the surface heat transfer coefficient is 30 W/m²K. Using Plank's equation (P = ½, R = ⅛ for infinite slab), calculate the freezing time.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Significance of six thermophysical properties [7 Marks]:**

| # | Property | Unfrozen | Frozen | Change | Significance |
|---|----------|----------|--------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | **Freezing point (Tf)** | 0°C for pure water | −1 to −3°C for foods | Lowered by solutes | Defines onset of ice formation |
| 2 | **Latent heat (L)** | N/A | 200–335 kJ/kg | Effective L < 334 | Energy needed to freeze |
| 3 | **Specific heat (Cp)** | 3.5–4.0 kJ/kg·K | 1.8–2.1 kJ/kg·K | Decreases ~50% | Less heat removal post-freeze |
| 4 | **Thermal conductivity (k)** | 0.4–0.6 W/m·K | 0.5–1.5 W/m·K | Increases 2–3× | Faster heat removal post-freeze |
| 5 | **Density (ρ)** | 1000 kg/m³ | 917 kg/m³ (ice) | Decreases ~9% | Volume expansion, packaging stress |
| 6 | **Water activity (aw)** | 0.95–1.0 | 0.001–0.5 | Decreases exponentially | Microbial stability |

**Why these changes matter:**
- **Increase in k** accelerates subsequent heat removal, enabling faster cooling.
- **Decrease in Cp** means frozen foods require less energy to maintain at sub-zero temperatures.
- **Volume expansion** causes mechanical damage to cell walls and packaging.
- **Drop in aw** makes frozen foods microbiologically shelf-stable.

**[7 Marks: 1 per property + 1 for engineering interpretation]**

---

**(b) Plank's Equation Calculation [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** D = 4/2 = 0.02 m, Tf = −2°C, T∞ = −35°C, h = 30 W/m²K, k = 1.2 W/m·K, ρ = 1050 kg/m³, L = 240,000 J/kg, P = ½, R = ⅛

**Step 1: Compute ΔT = Tf − T∞ = −2 − (−35) = 33°C = 33 K**

**Step 2: Compute Surface Term:**

$$\frac{P \cdot D}{h \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{30 \times 33} = \frac{0.01}{990} = 1.010 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3: Compute Internal Term:**

$$\frac{R \cdot D^2}{k} = \frac{0.125 \times (0.02)^2}{1.2} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.0004}{1.2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2} = 4.167 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4: Sum the Terms:**

$$1.010 \times 10^{-5} + 4.167 \times 10^{-5} = 5.177 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5: Compute Prefactor:**

$$\frac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_\infty} = \frac{1050 \times 240000}{33} = \frac{2.52 \times 10^8}{33} = 7.636 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6: Multiply to get t:**

$$t = 7.636 \times 10^6 \times 5.177 \times 10^{-5} = 395.3 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Step 7: Convert to minutes:**

$$t = \frac{395.3}{60} = 6.59 \; \text{minutes} \approx 6.6 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Result:** The fish slab will take approximately **6.6 minutes** to freeze. ✓

**[7 Marks breakdown: 1 for ΔT + 1 for surface term + 1 for internal term + 1 for sum + 1 for prefactor + 1 for t + 1 for unit conversion]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in ΔT:** Always use **Tf − T∞** (positive value, e.g., 33 K, not −33 K). KTU markers deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for sign errors.
> 2. **Plank's geometric constants (P and R):** For an **infinite slab**, P = ½ and R = ⅛. For an **infinite cylinder**, P = ¼ and R = 1/16. For a **sphere**, P = ⅙ and R = 1/24. **Wrong P, R = −2 marks.**
> 3. **Unit consistency:** Use **SI units throughout** (W, J, kg, m, K). Mixing kJ with kJ/kg·K loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Plank's equation assumes** all water freezes at Tf, which is an approximation. In derivation questions, **state the assumptions explicitly** to fetch full marks.
> 5. **For water activity below freezing:** Always convert **°C to Kelvin** before substitution. **Forgetting Kelvin = −1.5 marks.**
> 6. **Glass transition Tg ≠ Freezing point Tf:** Many students confuse these. Tg applies to **amorphous solutes**, not water. Marks deducted for **conflating** the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Exams**

### **A. Definitions (must memorise verbatim):**
- **Freezing Point (Tf):** Temperature at which ice first begins to form in a food; always **below 0°C** due to dissolved solutes.
- **Latent Heat of Freezing (L):** Energy released when 1 kg of water freezes; pure water value is **334 kJ/kg**.
- **Effective Latent Heat (Leff):** Latent heat actually available in a food, accounting for bound water and freeze-concentrated solutes.
- **Specific Heat (Cp):** Heat needed to raise 1 kg of food by 1 K; **decreases by ~50%** after freezing.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Rate of heat conduction; **increases 2–3×** after freezing because ice conducts better than water.
- **Water Activity (aw):** Ratio of vapour pressure of food water to pure water vapour pressure; **drops exponentially** below freezing.
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Temperature at which amorphous food components become glassy/brittle; **critical for frozen food stability**.
- **Plank's Equation:** Industry-standard formula for predicting freezing time of a slab/cylinder/sphere.

### **B. Key Numerical Values to Memorise:**

| Quantity | Value | Memory Trick |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| Lf (water) | **334 kJ/kg** | "3-3-4 = Freeze" |
| Kf | **1.86 °C·kg/mol** | "1-8-6" |
| Tf° | **273.15 K** | "Quarter of 1092" |
| Cp,ice | **2.10 kJ/kg·K** | "2-1-0" |
| ρ_ice | **917 kg/m³** | "9-1-7: ice is ~92% as dense as water" |

### **C. Conceptual Must-Knows:**

- **Fast freezing → small intracellular ice crystals → better quality.**
- **Slow freezing → large extracellular ice crystals → cellular damage → drip loss.**
- **Volume expansion of 9%** upon freezing → design packaging with headspace.
- **Below −18°C storage is the industry standard** for most frozen foods.
- **Tg is more important than Tf for long-term quality retention** (high-yield point for KTU 2024).
- **Plank's equation is valid for pre-packaged, regular geometry foods only**; irregular shapes require numerical methods (FEM/CFD).

### **D. Common KTU Question Patterns:**

1. **"Define and derive..."** → Use Clausius-Clapeyron → Raoult's Law → ΔTf equation.
2. **"Calculate freezing time for a slab..."** → Apply Plank's equation with P = ½, R = ⅛.
3. **"Compare properties before and after freezing..."** → Use the **Thermophysical Properties Table**.
4. **"Explain glass transition in frozen foods..."** → Define Tg + WLF equation + storage temperature rule.
5. **"Find water activity at sub-zero temperature..."** → Use the exponential aw equation with Kelvin units.

### **E. Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam Hall:**

- ✗ Using °C in the aw equation (always use **Kelvin**).
- ✗ Forgetting to state **assumptions** in Plank's equation derivations.
- ✗ Confusing **latent heat of fusion (334 kJ/kg)** with **latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg)**.
- ✗ Using **T∞ − Tf** instead of **Tf − T∞** (sign error).
- ✗ Forgetting the **−10°C rule** below Tg for stable storage.

### **F. Industrial Application Anchors (for KTU 2024 viva):**

- **Plank's equation** is used by **Daikin, Carrier, and GEA** refrigeration engineers to design IQF tunnels.
- **Tg measurement** is done using **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** in food R&D labs.
- **Water activity meters** (e.g., **Aqualab, Rotronic**) are used to verify frozen food stability.
- **Glass transition theory** is the foundation of **freeze-drying (lyophilisation)** cycle design.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Frozen Food Properties**
> These notes cover the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEFTT415, Module 3. Master the formulas, definitions, and the Plank's equation workflow to score full marks in both Part A and Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freezing Time, Factors Influencing Freezing Time, Freezing/Thawing Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freezing Time, Factors Influencing Freezing Time, Freezing/Thawing Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Definition of Freezing Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freezing Time ($t_f$)** is formally defined as the time required to reduce the temperature of a food product from its initial temperature ($T_i$) to a designated final temperature ($T_f$) at the **thermal center** (the slowest cooling or geometric center) of the product, under specified freezing conditions.

In practical KTU engineering parlance, the freezing time is the period from the moment the product is placed inside the freezer until the **thermal center reaches the storage temperature** (typically $-18^{\circ}C$ for commercial frozen food, or $-5^{\circ}C$ when 80% of the freezable water has crystallized into ice). It encompasses three thermal phases:

1. **Pre-cooling (Sensible Cooling)** – Removal of sensible heat from $T_i$ down to the freezing point ($T_{fp}$).
2. **Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal)** – Crystallization of water into ice at nearly constant temperature $T_{fp}$.
3. **Tempering (Sub-cooling)** – Further cooling of the frozen product from $T_{fp}$ down to the final storage temperature $T_f$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** For board examinations, "Freezing Time" almost always refers to the time elapsed until the **thermal center** of the product reaches the storage temperature, NOT the surface temperature. This is a frequent pitfall where students confuse the two.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Ice-Cube Tray Analogy**
>
> Imagine placing a sealed, water-filled rubber ball (your food) into a freezer. The outer layer of the ball touches the cold air first and turns to ice almost immediately — but the innermost drop of water at the center stays liquid for a long time. Why? Because **heat has to physically travel from the center to the surface** before it can be dumped into the freezer air. The longer the path (thickness) and the harder it is to travel (low thermal conductivity), the longer the freezing time.
>
> Now imagine the same ball *thawing* on a kitchen counter. The outer surface melts to water, and this water actually **acts as an insulating jacket**, slowing heat entry to the icy core. That's why thawing almost always takes longer than freezing — a key concept KTU examiners love to test.

### 1.3 Definition of Thawing Time

**Thawing Time ($t_{th}$)** is the time required to raise the temperature of a frozen product from its initial frozen temperature (typically $-18^{\circ}C$) to a temperature just below its freezing point (usually $0^{\circ}C$ to $-2^{\circ}C$) at the thermal center, under specified warming conditions.

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| Freezing Time ($t_f$) | 0.5 to 24 | hours |
| Initial Temperature ($T_i$) | 0 to 30 | °C |
| Final Storage Temperature ($T_f$) | -18 to -40 | °C |
| Freezing Point of Food ($T_{fp}$) | -0.5 to -2.5 | °C |
| Thawing Time ($t_{th}$) | 1.1 to 1.5 × $t_f$ | hours |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Boards:** Air-blast freezing typically uses an air velocity of **2–5 m/s** and refrigerant temperature of **-30°C to -40°C**. Always state these defaults when solving numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Plank's Equation

The most academically rigorous and KTU-board-recommended method to estimate freezing time is **Plank's Equation** (1913), later modified by **Ede (1949)** to include pre-cooling and post-freezing tempering.

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Plank's Derivation (Why It Works)

1. **Steady-state heat conduction** is assumed through a slab/cylinder/sphere.
2. The food is treated as a **homogeneous isotropic material** with uniform thermophysical properties.
3. The major resistance to heat flow lies in: **(a)** the food's internal conduction and **(b)** the surface convective film.
4. The temperature profile inside the food is assumed to move as a **pseudo-steady front** from the surface inward.
5. Heat is removed both as **latent heat** (at the freezing front) and as **sensible heat** (in pre-cooling and tempering zones).

#### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA REFERENCE — PRINT THIS TABLE FOR EXAMS**

| # | Equation | Application | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f \cdot \Delta H}{T_{fp} - T_{a}} \left[\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h_{c}} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k}\right]$ | Plank's Original (latent only) | $t_f$ [s], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $L_f$ [J/kg], $T_{fp},T_a$ [°C], $D$ [m], $h_c$ [W/m²K], $k$ [W/mK] |
| 2 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{T_{fp}-T_a}\left[\dfrac{P D}{h_c}+\dfrac{R D^2}{k}\right] + \dfrac{C_p \rho \, D^2}{4 k} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{T_{fp}-T_a}{T_f-T_a}\right)$ | Modified Plank (with pre-cooling + tempering) | $T_f$ = final storage temperature |
| 3 | $P_{slab} = \tfrac{1}{2},\ R_{slab} = \tfrac{1}{8}$ | Infinite slab | $D$ = thickness |
| 4 | $P_{cyl} = \tfrac{1}{4},\ R_{cyl} = \tfrac{1}{16}$ | Infinite cylinder | $D$ = diameter |
| 5 | $P_{sph} = \tfrac{1}{6},\ R_{sph} = \tfrac{1}{24}$ | Sphere | $D$ = diameter |
| 6 | $Bi = \dfrac{h_c \cdot D}{k}$ | Biot number (regime indicator) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t}{D^{2}}$ | Fourier number | $\alpha$ = $k/\rho C_p$ [m²/s] |
| 8 | $t_{th} \approx (1.1 \text{ to } 1.5) \times t_f$ | Empirical thawing–freezing ratio | Empirical |
| 9 | $\rho_{ice} = 917$, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ | Densities of pure ice & water | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $L_{fusion} = 334{,}000$ | Latent heat of pure water | J/kg |
| 11 | $C_{p,ice} \approx 2.1$ , $C_{p,water} \approx 4.18$ | Specific heats | kJ/kg·K |
| 12 | $k_{ice} = 2.22$, $k_{water} = 0.56$ | Thermal conductivities | W/m·K |
| 13 | $h_c$ for still air $\approx 5$–$10$ | Surface h-coeff — natural | W/m²·K |
| 14 | $h_c$ for blast air $\approx 20$–$30$ | Surface h-coeff — forced | W/m²·K |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hack — Shape Constants:** The most common examination problem uses an **infinite slab** (e.g., meat patties, fish fillets, packaged flat food). Always verify the shape before applying constants. If the problem says "package dimensions are 10×10×3 cm", the smallest dimension (3 cm) is your characteristic length **D** for an infinite slab.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Freezing time calculations are critical in:

- **Cold-chain logistics:** Estimating dwell time in blast freezers.
- **Energy auditing:** Calculating refrigerant load and compressor sizing.
- **Quality control:** Slow freezing → large ice crystals → cell rupture → drip loss.
- **Process design:** Choosing between air-blast, plate, immersion, or cryogenic (LN₂/CO₂) freezing.
- **Cost engineering:** Faster freezing = higher capital cost, but better product quality.

### 2.4 Why Thawing Time > Freezing Time (The Critical Insight)

| Aspect | Freezing | Thawing |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **Surface medium first to change** | Water → Ice | Ice → Water |
| **Thermal conductivity of surface layer** | High ($k_{ice} \approx 2.22$ W/mK) — heat escapes easily | Low ($k_{water} \approx 0.56$ W/mK) — heat enters slowly |
| **Diffusivity $\alpha = k/\rho C_p$** | $\alpha_{ice} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | $\alpha_{water} \approx 0.13 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| **Consequence** | Fast | Slow |
| **Typical ratio** | $t_{th} / t_f$ | **1.1 to 1.5** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination-Ready Statement:** *The thermal conductivity of ice is approximately 4 times that of liquid water, so when a frozen product is thawed, the melted water on the surface forms a thermal resistance layer, slowing further heat penetration. This is the primary reason why thawing time is consistently longer than freezing time for the same product under symmetric boundary conditions.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Plank's Equation (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Philosophy:** Plank (1913) integrated the heat-transfer equation across a moving freezing front. For KTU exams, you do NOT need to reproduce the full calculus derivation step-by-step. However, you MUST be able to **state the assumptions**, **define every term**, and **substitute values** correctly.

#### Assumptions (Write These First in Every KTU Answer)

1. Food is homogeneous, isotropic, with constant thermophysical properties.
2. Initial temperature is uniform throughout the body.
3. Freezing point $T_{fp}$ is a single sharp value (no freezing plateau range).
4. Pseudo-steady-state heat conduction through the frozen layer.
5. Constant surface heat transfer coefficient $h_c$.
6. Body has a regular geometry: slab, cylinder, or sphere.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1 (Slab)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]:**
> A meat block of dimensions 30 cm × 20 cm × 5 cm is to be frozen in an air-blast freezer. The initial temperature is $20°C$, the freezing point is $-2°C$, and the air/refrigerant temperature is $-30°C$. The surface heat transfer coefficient is $h_c = 25$ W/m²K. The density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, latent heat $L_f = 235{,}000$ J/kg, thermal conductivity $k = 1.4$ W/mK. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation (slab geometry, latent heat only).

#### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry

The smallest dimension is 5 cm. For an infinite slab approximation:
$$D = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

Slab constants:
$$P = \frac{1}{2}, \quad R = \frac{1}{8}$$

#### Step 2 — List All Given Quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= 1050 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \\
L_f &= 235{,}000 \text{ J/kg} \\
T_{fp} &= -2^{\circ}C \\
T_a &= -30^{\circ}C \\
D &= 0.05 \text{ m} \\
h_c &= 25 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K} \\
k &= 1.4 \text{ W/m·K} \\
P &= 0.5 \\
R &= 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Temperature Driving Force

$$
\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_a = (-2) - (-30) = 28^{\circ}C
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Convective Resistance Term

$$
\frac{P \cdot D}{h_c} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.05}{25} = \frac{0.025}{25} = 0.001 \text{ m}^{2}\text{·K/W}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the Conductive Resistance Term

$$
\frac{R \cdot D^{2}}{k} = \frac{0.125 \times (0.05)^{2}}{1.4} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.0025}{1.4} = \frac{0.0003125}{1.4} = 0.0002232 \text{ m}^{2}\text{·K/W}
$$

#### Step 6 — Sum the Resistances

$$
\Sigma R = 0.001 + 0.0002232 = 0.0012232 \text{ m}^{2}\text{·K/W}
$$

#### Step 7 — Compute the Latent Heat Term

$$
\frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{\Delta T} = \frac{1050 \times 235{,}000}{28} = \frac{246{,}750{,}000}{28} = 8{,}812{,}500 \text{ J/m}^{3}\text{·K}
$$

#### Step 8 — Final Substitution into Plank's Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T} \left[\frac{P D}{h_c} + \frac{R D^{2}}{k}\right] \\
t_f &= 8{,}812{,}500 \times 0.0012232 \\
t_f &= 10{,}780 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 9 — Convert to Hours

$$
t_f = \frac{10{,}780}{3600} = 2.99 \approx 3.0 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer:** Freezing time $\approx$ **3.0 hours** (or exactly 10,780 s).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2 (Modified Plank's with Tempering)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Sample pattern]:**
> Using the Modified Plank's equation, recalculate the freezing time for the same meat block if the final storage temperature is $-18°C$, specific heat $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Step 1 — Recall Modified Plank's Equation

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{T_{fp}-T_a}\left[\frac{P D}{h_c}+\frac{R D^2}{k}\right] + \frac{C_p \rho D^2}{4 k} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_{fp}-T_a}{T_f-T_a}\right)
$$

#### Step 2 — Already Computed Term (Plank Part)

$$
t_{1} = 10{,}780 \text{ s}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Tempering Term

$$
C_p \cdot \rho = 3500 \times 1050 = 3{,}675{,}000 \text{ J/m}^{3}\text{·K}
$$

$$
\frac{C_p \rho D^{2}}{4 k} = \frac{3{,}675{,}000 \times (0.05)^{2}}{4 \times 1.4} = \frac{3{,}675{,}000 \times 0.0025}{5.6} = \frac{9187.5}{5.6} = 1640.6 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{T_{fp}-T_a}{T_f-T_a}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{-2-(-30)}{-18-(-30)}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{28}{12}\right) = \ln(2.333) = 0.8473
$$

$$
t_{2} = 1640.6 \times 0.8473 = 1390.1 \text{ s}
$$

#### Step 4 — Total Freezing Time

$$
t_f = t_1 + t_2 = 10{,}780 + 1390.1 = 12{,}170 \text{ s} \approx 3.38 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer:** Modified freezing time (including tempering) $\approx$ **3.38 hours**. The tempering correction adds **~13%** more time — a realistic KTU-level result.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Recommended Equation | Reason |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Slab, only latent heat considered | Plank's original | Standard KTU textbook problem |
| Slab, with pre-cooling + tempering | Modified Plank's | More realistic |
| Irregular shape | Numerical simulation (FEM) | Plank's inaccurate |
| Cryogenic freezing (LN₂) | Not Plank's — use lumped capacitance | Very high $h_c$ |
| Very thin product (Bi < 0.1) | Lumped model: $t = -\frac{\rho C_p D}{h_c} \ln\left(\frac{T_f-T_a}{T_i-T_a}\right)$ | Negligible internal resistance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Freezing Time Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product enters freezer] --> B[Pre-cooling phase: T_i to T_fp]
    B --> C[Phase change: Latent heat removal at T_fp]
    C --> D[Tempering phase: T_fp to T_f]
    D --> E[End: Product at storage temperature T_f]
    
    F[Product properties] --> G[rho, k, Cp, L_f, T_fp]
    G --> C
    
    H[Process parameters] --> I[T_a, h_c, V_air]
    I --> C
    
    J[Geometric factor] --> K{Slab / Cylinder / Sphere?}
    K -->|Slab| L1[P=1/2, R=1/8]
    K -->|Cylinder| L2[P=1/4, R=1/16]
    K -->|Sphere| L3[P=1/6, R=1/24]
    L1 --> C
    L2 --> C
    L3 --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Factors Influencing Freezing Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT[Freezing Time t_f]
    
    P1[Product density rho] --> FT
    P2[Latent heat L_f] --> FT
    P3[Specific heat Cp] --> FT
    P4[Thermal conductivity k] --> FT
    P5[Initial temperature T_i] --> FT
    P6[Freezing point T_fp] --> FT
    P7[Final temperature T_f] --> FT
    P8[Thickness D] --> FT
    
    R1[Air temperature T_a] --> FT
    R2[Air velocity V] --> R3[Heat transfer coeff h_c] --> FT
    R3 --> FT
    R4[Contact medium oil / plate] --> FT
    
    S1[Slab] --> G1[Shape constant P, R] --> FT
    S2[Cylinder] --> G1
    S3[Sphere] --> G1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — Freezing vs Thawing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FREEZING
        F1[Surface water turns to ice first] --> F2[k_ice = 2.22 W/mK HIGH conductivity]
        F2 --> F3[Heat escapes easily]
        F3 --> F4[FASTER: t_f = base]
    end
    
    subgraph THAWING
        T1[Surface ice turns to water first] --> T2[k_water = 0.56 W/mK LOW conductivity]
        T2 --> T3[Water layer insulates core]
        T3 --> T4[SLOWER: t_th = 1.1 to 1.5 x t_f]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cold Chain Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw food material] --> B[Pre-treatment: Blanching / Washing]
    B --> C[Packaging: Pouch / Tray]
    C --> D{TYPE OF FREEZER}
    D -->|Air blast| E1[h_c = 20 to 30 W/m2K]
    D -->|Plate contact| E2[h_c = 50 to 100 W/m2K]
    D -->|Immersion| E3[h_c = 100 to 500 W/m2K]
    D -->|Cryogenic LN2| E4[h_c = 500 to 1500 W/m2K]
    
    E1 --> F[Plank equation solver]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    
    F --> G[Compute t_f]
    G --> H[Compute t_th = 1.3 x t_f]
    H --> I[Cold storage: minus 18 degree C]
    I --> J[Distribution cold chain]
    J --> K[Retail display: minus 18 degree C]
    K --> L[Consumer thawing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Define freezing time. Why is the thermal center chosen as the reference point for measurement?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Freezing time is the time required to reduce the temperature of a food product from its initial temperature to the desired final storage temperature (typically $-18°C$) at the **thermal center** (slowest cooling point) of the product. The thermal center is chosen because it is the **last point** in the food mass to reach the target temperature — once it has frozen, the entire product is guaranteed to be frozen. It is also the point most prone to quality degradation (large ice crystal formation, drip loss) if freezing is too slow, making it the most **critical control point** in process design. *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: why thermal center, 1 mark: practical significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
**Why is the thawing time always greater than the freezing time for the same food product?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> During freezing, the surface layer first converts to **ice**, which has a high thermal conductivity ($k_{ice} = 2.22$ W/m·K), allowing efficient heat removal from the interior. In contrast, during thawing, the surface ice first melts to **liquid water**, which has a much lower thermal conductivity ($k_{water} = 0.56$ W/m·K). This surface water film acts as a **thermal resistance layer**, slowing heat penetration to the still-frozen core. Additionally, the thermal diffusivity of ice is nearly 9 times that of water, compounding the effect. Empirically, $t_{th} \approx 1.1$ to $1.5 \times t_f$. *(1 mark: ice vs water conductivity, 1 mark: insulation effect, 1 mark: empirical ratio)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Enumerate the major factors affecting the freezing time of food products. Classify them into product-related, process-related, and equipment-related categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slab of fish fillet measuring 15 cm × 10 cm × 2 cm is to be frozen in a plate freezer. Initial temperature = 5°C, freezing point = -1.5°C, refrigerant temperature = -35°C, surface heat transfer coefficient $h_c = 80$ W/m²K, density $\rho = 980$ kg/m³, latent heat $L_f = 250{,}000$ J/kg, thermal conductivity $k = 1.5$ W/m·K. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Classification of Factors Influencing Freezing Time (7 marks):**

**A. Product-Related Factors (3 marks):**
1. **Initial temperature ($T_i$):** Higher $T_i$ → longer pre-cooling time.
2. **Final temperature ($T_f$):** Lower $T_f$ → longer tempering time.
3. **Freezing point ($T_{fp}$):** Foods with high salt/sugar content have depressed $T_{fp}$, extending time.
4. **Moisture content:** Higher water content → higher latent heat load → longer time.
5. **Density ($\rho$):** Denser products conduct heat faster (often), but store more total energy.
6. **Thermal conductivity ($k$):** Higher $k$ → faster internal heat conduction → shorter time.
7. **Specific heat ($C_p$):** Higher $C_p$ → more sensible heat to remove.
8. **Latent heat of fusion ($L_f$):** Higher $L_f$ → longer phase-change duration.
9. **Product geometry & size ($D$):** Larger characteristic dimension → longer path → longer time.
10. **Shape:** Slab, cylinder, or sphere — determines $P$ and $R$ constants.

**B. Process-Related Factors (2 marks):**
1. **Refrigerant/Air temperature ($T_a$):** Lower $T_a$ → larger $\Delta T$ → faster freezing.
2. **Air velocity / Contact medium:** Higher velocity → higher $h_c$ → faster freezing.
3. **Surface heat transfer coefficient ($h_c$):** Cryogenic > Immersion > Plate > Air-blast.
4. **Packaging material:** Acts as additional thermal resistance.
5. **Loading density of freezer:** Overloading reduces air flow and increases time.

**C. Equipment-Related Factors (2 marks):**
1. **Type of freezer:** Air-blast, plate, immersion, cryogenic, fluidized bed.
2. **Air distribution uniformity:** Dead zones increase time.
3. **Coil frosting:** Reduces $h_c$ over time.
4. **Defrost cycles:** Interrupt continuous operation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$D = 0.02 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta T = T_{fp} - T_a = -1.5 - (-35) = 33.5^{\circ}C$$
$$P = 0.5, \quad R = 0.125$$

**Step 1 — Convective resistance term:**
$$\frac{P D}{h_c} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{80} = \frac{0.01}{80} = 0.000125 \text{ m}^{2}\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Conductive resistance term:**
$$\frac{R D^{2}}{k} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.0004}{1.5} = \frac{0.00005}{1.5} = 0.0000333 \text{ m}^{2}\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance:**
$$\Sigma R = 0.000125 + 0.0000333 = 0.0001583 \text{ m}^{2}\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat factor:**
$$\frac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T} = \frac{980 \times 250{,}000}{33.5} = \frac{245{,}000{,}000}{33.5} = 7{,}313{,}433 \text{ J/m}^{3}\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Plank's equation:**
$$t_f = 7{,}313{,}433 \times 0.0001583 = 1157.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to minutes:**
$$t_f = \frac{1157.5}{60} = 19.3 \text{ minutes} \approx 0.32 \text{ hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** $t_f \approx$ **1158 seconds (≈ 19.3 min)**. Note: the small thickness and high $h_c$ (plate freezer) give a realistic commercial freezing time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample pattern, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** Derive the shape factor constants (P and R) for an infinite slab geometry used in Plank's equation. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare freezing time and thawing time for the same product. Explain the structural and physical reasons for the difference. Provide a numerical estimate of thawing time if the freezing time is 2.5 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Derivation of P and R for Infinite Slab (7 marks):**

For an infinite slab of thickness $D$ (half-thickness $a = D/2$ on each side of the center), the heat flow is one-dimensional through the frozen layer of thickness $x$ at time $t$.

**Governing equation (pseudo-steady state):**
$$q = \frac{k A (T_{fp} - T_a)}{x} = h_c A (T_s - T_a)$$

**Equating surface and conduction temperatures** at the ice front, after algebraic manipulation, the freezing front position $x$ is found by Plank's integral solution.

**At the end of freezing,** the front meets at the center, so $x = a = D/2$. The integral solution yields the characteristic constants:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{2}, \quad R = \frac{1}{8}}$$

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Homogeneous isotropic food body.
2. Uniform initial temperature and sharp freezing point.
3. Constant thermophysical properties during freezing.
4. One-dimensional heat flow.
5. Pseudo-steady-state conduction through the frozen layer.
6. Surface heat transfer coefficient is constant.
7. Latent heat is removed entirely at the freezing front.

---

**(b) Freezing vs Thawing Time (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Freezing | Thawing |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Surface layer first to change | Water → Ice | Ice → Water |
| Surface thermal conductivity | 2.22 W/mK (ice) — high | 0.56 W/mK (water) — low |
| Thermal diffusivity | $1.15 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | $0.13 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| Heat transfer path | Easy (ice conducts well) | Blocked (water insulates) |
| Mass transfer | Water migrates inward | Drip loss outward |
| Time | Shorter (baseline) | **Longer** ($1.1$–$1.5 \times t_f$) |

**Numerical estimate (3 marks):**

Given $t_f = 2.5$ hours:

**Lower bound (factor 1.1):**
$$t_{th,min} = 1.1 \times 2.5 = 2.75 \text{ hours}$$

**Upper bound (factor 1.5):**
$$t_{th,max} = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ hours}$$

**Best estimate (factor 1.3, most common):**
$$t_{th} = 1.3 \times 2.5 = 3.25 \text{ hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** $t_{th} \approx$ **3.25 hours** (using average factor of 1.3).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using wrong characteristic length $D$:** For a 30×20×5 cm block, students often take 30 cm. The correct $D$ for a slab is the **smallest dimension** = 5 cm.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $\rho$ in g/cm³ vs kg/m³ changes answer by 1000×. Always convert to SI.
> 3. **Wrong shape constants:** Cylinder vs slab is a 2× difference in $P$ and 2× in $R$.
> 4. **Missing latent heat factor $\rho L_f$:** Many students write only the bracketed term and forget to multiply.
> 5. **Confusing $\Delta T$:** It is $T_{fp} - T_a$, NOT $T_i - T_a$.
> 6. **Not converting seconds to hours:** Examiner may deduct 0.5 mark if units are inconsistent.
> 7. **State assumptions first** in derivations — KTU marks are explicitly allocated for "stating assumptions" in 7-mark derivation sub-questions.
> 8. **Thawing ratio must be cited with reasoning** — don't just state the number; explain the physics of water vs ice conductivity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Freezing Time Module**

- **Freezing time** is measured at the **thermal center**, not the surface.
- **Plank's equation** is the workhorse formula: $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{PD}{h_c} + \dfrac{RD^2}{k}\right]$.
- **Shape constants:** Slab ($P=1/2, R=1/8$), Cylinder ($P=1/4, R=1/16$), Sphere ($P=1/6, R=1/24$).
- **Use smallest dimension** as $D$ for slab approximation.
- **Modified Plank's equation** adds a tempering term using $\ln[(T_{fp}-T_a)/(T_f-T_a)]$.
- **Thawing > Freezing** because $k_{ice} > k_{water}$ and $\alpha_{ice} > \alpha_{water}$.
- **Empirical ratio:** $t_{th} = 1.1$ to $1.5 \times t_f$ (typically 1.3).
- **Factors:** Product (size, $T_i$, $T_f$, $k$, $C_p$, $L_f$, $\rho$, $T_{fp}$), Process ($T_a$, $h_c$, air velocity), Equipment (freezer type).
- **Biot number** $Bi = h_c D / k$ indicates whether internal conduction or surface convection dominates.
- **Cryogenic freezing** has very high $h_c$ → freezing times are minutes, not hours.
- **Always state assumptions** before any derivation in KTU answers.
- **Conversion check:** $1 \text{ hr} = 3600 \text{ s}$, $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$.
- **Standard freezer air velocity:** 2–5 m/s; standard refrigerant temperature: $-30°C$ to $-40°C$.
- **Quality link:** Fast freezing → small intracellular ice crystals → less drip loss → better texture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeze Concentration: Principles, Process, Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Freeze Concentration: Principles, Process, Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Freeze Concentration (Cryoconcentration)** is a physical separation process in which water is selectively removed from an aqueous food solution by means of **partial crystallization (ice formation)** followed by **mechanical separation** of the ice crystals from the resulting concentrated liquid phase. It operates on the principle that when an aqueous solution is cooled below its freezing point, the solvent (water) crystallizes as essentially pure ice, leaving the dissolved solids (sugars, acids, salts, aroma compounds) in the remaining unfrozen liquid, thereby increasing its concentration. The process is governed by the **solid-liquid phase equilibrium** of the food system and is characterized by operation at **sub-zero temperatures (typically 0 °C to −40 °C)**, well below the boiling point used in evaporative concentration. This makes it a non-thermal concentration technique that preserves heat-sensitive components. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Ocean's Natural Purifier"** Imagine standing on a frozen lake in winter. The clear, glass-like ice surface contains almost no salt, while the brine (salty water) is trapped underneath, far more concentrated than the original seawater. Nature performs *freeze concentration* every winter in polar seas. In your kitchen, you do the same thing when you make **homemade frozen fruit pops** or **ice candy** — the sugary syrup concentrates around the ice crystals, and biting into the sweet liquid between ice crystals is essentially tasting freeze-concentrated juice! In food industry terms: **juice → freeze → separate pure ice crystals → keep the rich, aromatic concentrate** — simple as making ice cubes from coffee, but in reverse and on an industrial scale. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following standard constants are essential for KTU numerical problems on Freeze Concentration: | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Latent heat of fusion of water | $L_f$ | **333.55** | kJ/kg | | Latent heat of fusion (older) | $L_f$ | **80** | cal/g | | Specific heat of water | $C_{pw}$ | **4.18** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat of ice | $C_{pi}$ | **2.09** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Cryoscopic constant of water | $K_f$ | **1.86** | °C·kg/mol | | Freezing point of pure water | $T_f^0$ | **0** | °C | ### 1.4 Why Freeze Concentration? — Significance in Food Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > Freeze concentration is preferred over evaporation when the product is **heat-sensitive**, **aroma-retentive**, and when **flavor quality is paramount**. It is the only concentration method that retains volatile aroma compounds (which are often stripped off in evaporation along with water vapor). - Preserves **vitamins, pigments, and thermos-labile bioactives**. - Low operating temperatures inhibit **microbial growth and enzymatic browning**. - Produces concentrate with **fresh-like sensory quality** — used for fruit juices, coffee extract, beer, vinegar, wine, and dairy products. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Principle — Freezing Point Depression When a solute (sugar, salt, acid) is dissolved in water, the freezing point of the solution **drops below 0 °C**. This phenomenon is called **Freezing Point Depression (FPD)** and is the fundamental thermodynamic driver of freeze concentration. The general FPD equation (for dilute, non-electrolyte solutions) is: $$\Delta T_f = T_f^0 - T_f = K_f \cdot m$$ Where: - $\Delta T_f$ = Depression of freezing point (°C) - $T_f^0$ = Freezing point of pure solvent (water) = 0 °C - $T_f$ = Freezing point of solution (°C) - $K_f$ = Cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol** - $m$ = Molality of solution (mol solute / kg solvent) > [!NOTE] > As freeze concentration proceeds, the **solute concentration rises**, freezing point **decreases further**, meaning progressively lower temperatures are needed. This is the operational limit of any freeze concentration system. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Freeze Concentration Process The freeze concentration process consists of **four sequential unit operations**: #### **Step 1 — Pre-cooling / Sensible Heat Removal** - Feed liquid is cooled from ambient temperature to just above its freezing point. - Heat removed = sensible heat ($Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$). #### **Step 2 — Crystallization (Ice Nucleation & Growth)** - The pre-cooled liquid is further cooled below its freezing point. - **Nucleation**: Tiny ice nuclei form (homogeneous or heterogeneous). - **Crystal Growth**: Water molecules migrate to nuclei and crystallize into ice. - Heat removed = latent heat ($Q_L = m \cdot L_f$). - Controlled, slow crystallization → **large, separable ice crystals**. - Fast crystallization → small, hard-to-separate crystals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crystal size is the most critical design parameter.** Large crystals = easy separation, less entrained solute, higher purity concentrate. #### **Step 3 — Ice-Liquid Separation** - The ice slurry (mixture of ice crystals + concentrated liquid) is sent to a **separator** (centrifuge, filter press, wash column, or gravity settler). - Pure ice crystals are physically separated from the concentrate. - Sometimes, the ice is **washed** with cold water to recover adhering concentrate. #### **Step 4 — Maturation / Ripening (Optional)** - In suspension crystallization, the ice crystals are held in the concentrated liquid to allow **Ostwald Ripening** — small crystals dissolve and re-deposit on larger crystals. - This increases average crystal size, improving separation efficiency. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > High-Yield Equations for Freeze Concentration (Mandatory for Numericals): | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression (°C) | | 2 | $F = C + I$ | Overall mass balance (kg/h) | | 3 | $F \cdot X_F = C \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$ | Solute (solids) balance | | 4 | $Y = \dfrac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F}$ | Solute recovery yield (decimal) | | 5 | $Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (pre-cooling) | | 6 | $Q_{latent} = m_{ice} \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat (crystallization) | | 7 | $Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}$ | Total refrigeration load | | 8 | $X_C = \dfrac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{C}$ | Final concentration calculation | | 9 | $\eta_{sep} = 1 - \dfrac{X_I}{X_C}$ | Separation efficiency | | 10 | $\dot{W}_{ref} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{COP}$ | Refrigeration power required | Where: - $F$ = Feed mass flow rate - $C$ = Concentrate mass flow rate - $I$ = Ice mass flow rate - $X_F, X_C, X_I$ = Solids fraction in feed, concentrate, and ice respectively - $COP$ = Coefficient of Performance of refrigeration system (typical: **2.0 to 3.5** for industrial systems) - $L_f$ = Latent heat of fusion = **333.55 kJ/kg** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fruit Juice Industry** — Orange, apple, grape, pineapple juices (premium "from concentrate" products). - **Brewing** — Production of high-gravity beer wort and ice-beer (removes water as ice to boost alcohol). - **Coffee & Tea** — Freeze-concentrated coffee/tea retains volatile aroma oils lost in evaporation. - **Vinegar & Wine** — Cold concentration preserves delicate flavor profiles. - **Dairy** — Concentration of whey and milk for specialty cheese and infant formula. - **Pharmaceutical & Biotechnology** — Concentration of heat-sensitive enzymes, antibodies, and probiotics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Balance & Final Concentration Equation **Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the concentration of solids in the concentrate ($X_C$) in terms of feed flow ($F$), feed concentration ($X_F$), ice flow ($I$), and ice purity ($X_I$). **Step 1: Write the overall mass balance.** The total mass entering must equal the total mass leaving. $$F = C + I$$ **Step 2: Write the solute (solids) balance.** The solids entering with the feed leave either in the concentrate or entrained in the ice. $$F \cdot X_F = C \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$$ **Step 3: Solve the mass balance for $C$ (concentrate flow).** $$C = F - I$$ **Step 4: Substitute $C = F - I$ into the solute balance.** $$F \cdot X_F = (F - I) \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$$ **Step 5: Expand and isolate $X_C$.** $$F \cdot X_F = F \cdot X_C - I \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$$ $$F \cdot X_C - I \cdot X_C = F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I$$ $$X_C \cdot (F - I) = F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I$$ **Step 6: Final expression for concentrate concentration.** $$X_C = \frac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F - I}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **master concentration equation** for freeze concentration. It assumes a **single-stage equilibrium separation**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Yield (Solute Recovery) The yield $Y$ is the fraction of feed solids that ends up in the concentrate (not lost with the ice). **Step 1: Define yield as a ratio.** $$Y = \frac{\text{Solids in concentrate}}{\text{Solids in feed}} = \frac{C \cdot X_C}{F \cdot X_F}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the solute balance.** From $F \cdot X_F = C \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$, we have $C \cdot X_C = F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I$. $$Y = \frac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F}$$ **Step 3: Simplify.** $$Y = 1 - \frac{I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy (Refrigeration) Load **Step 1: Pre-cooling sensible heat (feed $F$ cooled from $T_1$ to freezing point $T_f$).** $$Q_1 = F \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_1 - T_f)$$ **Step 2: Latent heat to freeze a fraction of water (mass $I$ into ice).** $$Q_2 = I \cdot L_f$$ **Step 3: Optional sub-cooling of ice.** $$Q_3 = I \cdot C_{pi} \cdot (T_f - T_2)$$ **Step 4: Total refrigeration load.** $$Q_{total} = F \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_1 - T_f) + I \cdot L_f + I \cdot C_{pi} \cdot (T_f - T_2)$$ **Step 5: Refrigeration power.** $$\dot{W}_{ref} = \frac{Q_{total}}{COP}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Numerical Pattern (typical 7-mark problem):** **Problem:** 1000 kg/h of orange juice containing 12% total soluble solids (TSS) is to be freeze-concentrated. The process produces 400 kg/h of ice containing 0.5% TSS (entrained solids). Calculate: (a) Mass flow rate of concentrate, (b) Concentration of TSS in concentrate, (c) Yield (solute recovery), and (d) Refrigeration load if feed enters at 25 °C, juice freezing point is −2.5 °C, ice leaves at −5 °C. (Take $COP = 2.5$). #### **Solution:** **Given:** - $F = 1000$ kg/h - $X_F = 0.12$ - $I = 400$ kg/h - $X_I = 0.005$ **Part (a) — Concentrate flow rate:** $$C = F - I = 1000 - 400 = 600 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Part (b) — Concentration of TSS in concentrate:** $$X_C = \frac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F - I} = \frac{(1000)(0.12) - (400)(0.005)}{1000 - 400}$$ $$X_C = \frac{120 - 2}{600} = \frac{118}{600} = 0.1967 \approx 19.67\% \text{ TSS}$$ **Part (c) — Yield (solute recovery):** $$Y = 1 - \frac{I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F} = 1 - \frac{(400)(0.005)}{(1000)(0.12)} = 1 - \frac{2}{120} = 1 - 0.0167$$ $$Y = 0.9833 = 98.33\%$$ **Part (d) — Refrigeration load:** Sensible cooling: $$Q_1 = 1000 \times 4.18 \times (25 - (-2.5)) = 1000 \times 4.18 \times 27.5 = 114{,}950 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Latent heat: $$Q_2 = 400 \times 333.55 = 133{,}420 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Sub-cooling of ice: $$Q_3 = 400 \times 2.09 \times (-2.5 - (-5)) = 400 \times 2.09 \times 2.5 = 2090 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Total: $$Q_{total} = 114{,}950 + 133{,}420 + 2090 = 250{,}460 \text{ kJ/h} = 69.57 \text{ kW}$$ Refrigeration power: $$\dot{W}_{ref} = \frac{69.57}{2.5} = 27.83 \text{ kW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Insight:** Always show units in every step. Carry forward at least 4 decimal places to avoid rounding errors in sub-parts. ### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Methods of Freeze Concentration | Parameter | Suspension Crystallization | Layer (Block) Crystallization | Progressive Freeze Concentration | |---|---|---|---| | Crystal form | Discrete crystals in slurry | Frozen layer on cooled surface | Directional solidification | | Crystal size | Large (controllable) | Small to medium | Variable | | Separation | Centrifuge / wash column | Melting + fractionation | Natural drainage | | Capacity | High (continuous) | Medium | Low (batch) | | Energy efficiency | High | Medium | Low | | Equipment complexity | High | Low | Lowest | | Product quality | Excellent | Good | Variable | | Industrial examples | Grenco, Niro, FreezeCon | Zibo Zhenhua | Research/lab-scale | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Overall Freeze Concentration Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Feed Solution
Fruit Juice/Wort] --> B[Pre-cooler
Sensible Heat Removal] B --> C[Ice Crystallizer
Scraped Surface / Suspension] C --> D[Ice-Concentrate Slurry
Mixed Phase] D --> E[Separator Unit
Centrifuge / Wash Column] E --> F1[Pure Ice Crystals
Removed] E --> F2[Concentrated Product
High TSS Stream] F2 --> G[Optional Maturation
Ostwald Ripening Tank] G --> H[Final Concentrate
Storage / Packaging] F1 --> I[Optional Wash
Cold Water Recovery] I --> J[Wash Liquid Recycle
Back to Feed] style A fill:#e1f5ff style C fill:#ffe1e1 style E fill:#fff4e1 style H fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Methods of Freeze Concentration ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Freeze Concentration
Methods] --> M1[Suspension
Crystallization] M --> M2[Layer Crystallization
Block Method] M --> M3[Progressive Freeze
Concentration PFC] M1 --> M1a[Crystals grow
freely in slurry] M1 --> M1b[Separation via
centrifuge/wash column] M1 --> M1c[Best for large-scale
industrial production] M2 --> M2a[Solution freezes on
cooled surface] M2 --> M2b[Layer scraped/melted
in fractions] M2 --> M2c[Used for viscous
and pulpy liquids] M3 --> M3a[Directional freezing
from one side] M3 --> M3b[Ice front moves slowly
through solution] M3 --> M3c[Solute rejected ahead
