of advancing front] style M fill:#e1f5ff style M1 fill:#ffe1e1 style M2 fill:#fff4e1 style M3 fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mass & Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT[Input Stream] F1[Feed F kg/h at XF percent TSS] end subgraph PROCESS[Freeze Concentration Unit] P1[Sensible Cooling
Q1 = F Cp dT] P2[Ice Crystallization
Q2 = I Lf] P3[Separation
Mechanical] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Streams] O1[Concentrate C kg/h at XC percent TSS] O2[Ice Crystals I kg/h at XI percent TSS] O3[Heat Rejection Q kW to Refrigeration] end F1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 P3 --> O1 P3 --> O2 P1 --> O3 P2 --> O3 style INPUT fill:#e1f5ff style PROCESS fill:#fff4e1 style OUTPUT fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – April 2024, Model Paper]** **Q1. Define freeze concentration. List any four advantages of freeze concentration over evaporative concentration.** **Model Answer:** Freeze concentration is a non-thermal separation process in which water is removed from an aqueous food solution as ice crystals, leaving the dissolved solids in a more concentrated liquid phase. **Advantages over evaporation:** 1. Operates at low temperatures — preserves heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, aroma compounds, color pigments). 2. No phase change of solvent to vapor — volatile flavor compounds are retained (not stripped off). 3. Lower energy consumption for thermos-labile products (no need for high-temperature steam). 4. Inhibits microbial growth and enzymatic reactions due to sub-zero temperatures, improving product shelf life. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is Ostwald Ripening? Why is it important in suspension crystallization-based freeze concentration?** **Model Answer:** **Ostwald Ripening** is the thermodynamic phenomenon in which small ice crystals in a suspension dissolve and the dissolved water re-deposits onto larger crystals, causing the **mean crystal size to increase** over time at the expense of smaller crystals. **Importance in Freeze Concentration:** - Larger crystals are **easier to separate** from the concentrate (less entrainment). - Reduces **solids loss** with the ice fraction. - Improves **separation efficiency** and **overall yield** of the process. - Achieved by holding the ice slurry in a maturation/ripening vessel for a controlled residence time. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]** #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **principle of freeze concentration** with a neat phase diagram. Discuss the role of **freezing point depression** in determining the operating temperature. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **suspension crystallization method** of freeze concentration in detail. With the help of a block diagram, explain the function of the crystallizer, separator, and maturation unit. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Principle of Freeze Concentration:** Freeze concentration is based on the **solid-liquid phase equilibrium** of an aqueous food solution. When such a solution is cooled to its freezing point, **ice begins to crystallize** as essentially pure water. Because the solute (sugar, acid, salt) does not fit into the ice crystal lattice, it is **rejected into the remaining liquid phase**, thereby increasing its concentration. The relationship between solute concentration and freezing temperature is given by the **freezing point depression equation**: $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m = 1.86 \cdot m$$ As concentration increases during the process, the freezing point drops further, requiring progressively lower operating temperatures. **Phase Diagram Explanation:** In a typical binary phase diagram (water-solute), the **liquidus line** separates the liquid region from the two-phase (liquid + ice) region. When a solution of composition $X_1$ is cooled, it remains liquid until it crosses the liquidus line at temperature $T_1$, where ice starts forming. The overall composition is fixed, but the system splits into two phases — pure ice and a more concentrated liquid of composition $X_2$ (read from the liquidus line at $T_1$). The **operating temperature window** for freeze concentration is bounded above by the **liquidus temperature** (where ice starts forming) and below by the **eutectic temperature** (where the entire solution solidifies). Industrial freeze concentration operates a few degrees below the liquidus but well above the eutectic. **(b) Suspension Crystallization Method:** In **suspension (or dispersed) crystallization**, ice is grown as **discrete crystals dispersed** in the bulk concentrated liquid. The process involves three main units: 1. **Crystallizer (Scrape-Surface or Stirred Vessel):** The feed is cooled below its freezing point under controlled agitation. Scraped-surface heat exchangers (SSHE) are commonly used — rotating blades continuously scrape ice off the cold surface, producing a slurry of small ice crystals in concentrated liquid. 2. **Separator (Wash Column or Centrifuge):** The ice-concentrate slurry is fed to a separator. **Wash columns** are preferred in modern industrial systems — the slurry is pushed upward against a descending flow of cold wash water, washing entrained concentrate off the ice surface. Centrifuges are an alternative but cause higher solute loss. 3. **Maturation / Ripening Tank:** The separated ice is held briefly in a ripening vessel where **Ostwald ripening** enlarges the crystals, improving subsequent separation efficiency in multi-stage systems. **Block Diagram:** ``` Feed Juice (X_F) → Pre-cooler → Crystallizer (SSHE) ↓ ↓ Refrigeration Ice-Concentrate Slurry ↓ ↓ Heat Removed Wash Column / Centrifuge ↓ ↓ Pure Ice Concentrate (X_C) ↓ Maturation Tank ↓ Final Concentrate ``` --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **three principal methods** of freeze concentration: suspension crystallization, layer crystallization, and progressive freeze concentration. Compare their merits and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A fruit juice containing **10% TSS** is fed at a rate of **800 kg/h** to a freeze concentrator. **300 kg/h of ice** is removed containing **0.8% TSS** (due to entrainment). Calculate: - (i) Mass flow rate of concentrate - (ii) Final TSS concentration of concentrate - (iii) Solute recovery yield - (iv) Refrigeration load in kW if feed enters at **30 °C**, juice freezing point is **−2 °C**, ice leaves at **−6 °C**, and **COP = 2.5**. Use $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg, $C_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{pi} = 2.09$ kJ/(kg·K). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Three Methods of Freeze Concentration:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Block Diagram above for the schematic.)* **1. Suspension Crystallization:** - Ice crystals nucleate and grow as discrete particles suspended in the bulk liquid. - Achieved in **scraped-surface heat exchangers** or **stirred tank crystallizers**. - Crystal size controlled by agitation speed, residence time, and supersaturation. - Separation via wash column or centrifuge. - **Merits:** Continuous operation, large crystals, high purity, scalable to industrial capacity. - **Limitations:** High capital cost, complex control, viscosity limits for high concentrations. **2. Layer (Block) Crystallization:** - The solution is frozen as a **solid block on a cooled surface**; the concentrate is squeezed out or scraped off. - Operates batch-wise. - **Merits:** Simple equipment, low cost, suitable for viscous/pulpy liquids. - **Limitations:** Poor heat transfer, low capacity, non-uniform crystal size, batch operation only. **3. Progressive Freeze Concentration (PFC):** - A **directional freezing** technique where the ice front advances slowly from one end of the vessel. - Solute is rejected ahead of the advancing ice-liquid interface. - **Merits:** Simple apparatus, no mechanical separator needed, low operational complexity. - **Limitations:** Very slow (batch), low capacity, only practical for laboratory or specialty use. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Suspension | Layer | Progressive | |---|---|---|---| | Crystal form | Discrete | Solid block | Directional front | | Operation | Continuous | Batch | Batch | | Scale | Industrial | Pilot | Lab | | Purity | Highest | Medium | Variable | | Cost | High | Low | Lowest | **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $F = 800$ kg/h, $X_F = 0.10$ - $I = 300$ kg/h, $X_I = 0.008$ - $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_f = -2$ °C, $T_2 = -6$ °C - $COP = 2.5$, $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg - $C_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{pi} = 2.09$ kJ/(kg·K) **(i) Concentrate flow rate:** $$C = F - I = 800 - 300 = 500 \text{ kg/h}$$ **(ii) Final TSS concentration:** $$X_C = \frac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F - I} = \frac{(800)(0.10) - (300)(0.008)}{800 - 300}$$ $$X_C = \frac{80 - 2.4}{500} = \frac{77.6}{500} = 0.1552 = 15.52\% \text{ TSS}$$ **(iii) Solute recovery yield:** $$Y = 1 - \frac{I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F} = 1 - \frac{(300)(0.008)}{(800)(0.10)} = 1 - \frac{2.4}{80} = 1 - 0.03$$ $$Y = 0.97 = 97.0\%$$ **(iv) Refrigeration load:** **Sensible cooling of feed:** $$Q_1 = F \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_1 - T_f) = 800 \times 4.18 \times (30 - (-2))$$ $$Q_1 = 800 \times 4.18 \times 32 = 107{,}008 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Latent heat of crystallization:** $$Q_2 = I \cdot L_f = 300 \times 333.55 = 100{,}065 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Sub-cooling of ice:** $$Q_3 = I \cdot C_{pi} \cdot (T_f - T_2) = 300 \times 2.09 \times (-2 - (-6))$$ $$Q_3 = 300 \times 2.09 \times 4 = 2508 \text{ kJ/h}$$ **Total refrigeration load:** $$Q_{total} = 107{,}008 + 100{,}065 + 2508 = 209{,}581 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Converting to kW: $$Q_{total} = \frac{209{,}581}{3600} = 58.22 \text{ kW}$$ **Refrigeration power required:** $$\dot{W}_{ref} = \frac{Q_{total}}{COP} = \frac{58.22}{2.5} = 23.29 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Error in ΔT:** When feed temperature is *above* freezing point and freezing point is *negative* (e.g., $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_f = -2$ °C), the difference is **NOT** $30 - 2 = 28$ °C. It is $30 - (-2) = 32$ °C. Forgetting the negative sign is the **#1 error** in KTU papers. > 2. **Units:** $Q$ in kJ/h must be divided by **3600** to convert to kW. Marks are deducted if units are inconsistent. > 3. **$X_I$ NOT Zero:** Many students assume ice is 100% pure and write $X_I = 0$. This gives incorrect yield. Industrial ice always has entrained solute — read the problem carefully. > 4. **Forgetting sub-cooling:** $Q_3$ is often missed by students. KTU asks for *total* refrigeration load, so include all three terms. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Freeze Concentration** - **Definition:** Water is removed from a solution as **pure ice crystals**, concentrating the remaining liquid. - **Driving Force:** **Freezing point depression** governed by $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$. - **Key Constant:** $K_f$ for water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**; $L_f$ = **333.55 kJ/kg**. - **Process Steps:** Pre-cooling → Crystallization → Separation → (Optional) Maturation. - **Three Methods:** - Suspension Crystallization (industrial standard, continuous) - Layer Crystallization (batch, simple) - Progressive Freeze Concentration (directional freezing, lab scale) - **Master Equations:** - Mass: $F = C + I$ - Solute: $F \cdot X_F = C \cdot X_C + I \cdot X_I$ - Concentration: $X_C = \dfrac{F \cdot X_F - I \cdot X_I}{F - I}$ - Yield: $Y = 1 - \dfrac{I \cdot X_I}{F \cdot X_F}$ - **Critical Design Parameter:** **Ice crystal size** — controls separation efficiency. - **Ostwald Ripening:** Small crystals dissolve, large crystals grow — improves separation. - **Advantages:** Low temperature, aroma retention, vitamin preservation, no thermal degradation. - **Disadvantages:** High capital cost, refrigeration energy, viscosity limits at high concentration. - **Industrial Applications:** Fruit juices, beer, wine, coffee, vinegar, dairy, pharmaceuticals. - **Common Exam Traps:** Negative freezing point sign error, unit conversion (kJ/h → kW), assuming $X_I = 0$, forgetting sub-cooling term. - **KTU-Favorite Topics:** Numericals on mass balance, energy balance, yield; diagrams of suspension crystallizer; comparison of methods.

topics.title: Frozen Food Storage: Quality Changes in Foods During Frozen Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frozen Food Storage: Quality Changes in Foods During Frozen Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frozen food storage** is the preservation technique in which foods are held at sub-zero temperatures (typically **-18°C** to **-40°C**) to maintain their physico-chemical, microbiological, and sensory quality by inhibiting spoilage reactions and microbial metabolism. During long-term storage, foods undergo various quality changes that ultimately limit shelf life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** Quality changes in foods during frozen storage refer to the irreversible or partially reversible alterations in physical structure, chemical composition, nutritional value, and sensory attributes of foods caused by prolonged exposure to sub-zero temperatures, temperature fluctuations, and ice-water phase interactions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The International Institute of Refrigeration (IIR) recommends a storage temperature of **-18°C** as the benchmark for frozen food preservation. At this temperature, the water activity ($a_w$) of most foods falls below **0.86**, effectively halting microbial proliferation.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Quality Deterioration

$$\text{Quality Loss} = f(\text{Physical Changes}, \text{Chemical Reactions}, \text{Biochemical Activity})$$

| Pillar | Governing Driver | Typical Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | Ice crystal growth, recrystallization, sublimation | Freezer burn, drip loss, texture softening |
| Chemical | Lipid oxidation, protein denaturation, Maillard browning | Rancidity, off-flavors, color darkening |
| Biochemical | Residual enzyme activity, psychrotroph metabolism | Off-odors, nutrient degradation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Time Capsule in a Freezer"

Imagine placing a fresh, juicy strawberry inside a glass jar and lowering it into a home freezer. The strawberry is essentially a **time capsule**: it is locked into a near-suspended state where bacteria are paralyzed and most chemical reactions crawl to a near-halt. However, the strawberry is not truly "frozen in time."

- **Inside the cell walls**, tiny ice crystals act like miniature wedges that slowly enlarge as water migrates from the syrup-like cellular fluid to the crystal surfaces.
- **On the surface**, water molecules escape directly from ice to vapor (a process called **sublimation**), leaving behind a white, dried, leathery patch known as **freezer burn**.
- **In the fats**, oxygen molecules (which are still soluble in the cold fat) attack unsaturated bonds, producing rancid cardboard-like flavors.

The strawberry may *look* preserved, but over months, it progressively loses its vivid red color, juicy texture, and sweet aroma. This is exactly what happens in industrial frozen food storage — only on a much larger, more controlled scale.

### 1.4 Critical Storage Parameters (KTU Numericals Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three cardinal parameters that govern every frozen food storage study:**
>
> 1. **Storage Temperature (T):** Usually maintained at **-18°C** for commercial frozen food. Lower temperatures (e.g., **-30°C to -40°C**) used for high-value products (sushi-grade fish, premium ice cream).
> 2. **Storage Time (t):** Expressed in days, weeks, or months. Governed by first-order deterioration kinetics.
> 3. **Temperature Fluctuation (ΔT):** Repeated freeze-thaw cycles are the **single most damaging factor** in frozen storage, causing ice recrystallization and accelerated quality loss.

### 1.5 Governing Thermodynamic Relationship

The driving force for physical changes during frozen storage is the difference in **water vapor pressure** between the ice crystals and the surrounding unfrozen matrix:

$$\Delta P = P_{ice}(T) - P_{solution}(T)$$

where the solute-rich unfrozen phase always has a *lower* vapor pressure, causing continuous water migration toward the ice crystals — a phenomenon called **recrystallization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** This vapor pressure differential is the *root cause* of drip loss upon thawing, surface freezer burn, and coarsening of ice crystals — all of which KTU questions repeatedly test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Quality Changes

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies quality changes during frozen storage into the following structured hierarchy:

#### A. Physical Changes
1. **Ice Crystal Growth (Ostwald Ripening)**
2. **Recrystallization** (Accretive, Migratory, Isomass)
3. **Sublimation & Freezer Burn**
4. **Drip Loss / Thaw Exudate**
5. **Texture Softening** (cell wall rupture)
6. **Color Changes** (pigment migration, myoglobin oxidation in meat)

#### B. Chemical Changes
1. **Lipid Oxidation** (hydroperoxide formation → aldehydes/ketones)
2. **Protein Denaturation** (myofibrillar protein aggregation in fish)
3. **Non-enzymatic Browning** (Maillard reaction between reducing sugars and amines)
4. **Vitamin Degradation** (especially Ascorbic Acid – Vitamin C)
5. **Hydrolysis of Flavor Compounds**

#### C. Biochemical Changes
1. **Residual Enzyme Activity** (lipases, proteases, polyphenol oxidases)
2. **Psychrotrophic Microbial Metabolism** (slow at T < -10°C)
3. **Autolysis** (tissue self-degradation by endogenous enzymes)

### 2.2 The TTT (Time-Temperature-Tolerance) Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TTT Concept (Van Arsdel, 1969):** Every frozen food possesses an inherent **Quality Tolerance** — the maximum time it can be held at a given temperature before unacceptable quality loss occurs. Plotting the *Time* to reach the tolerance limit against the storage *Temperature* yields the **TTT diagram**.

The TTT framework is a cornerstone of KTU Module 3 questions. The relationship is typically expressed as:

$$\log_{10}(t_{tol}) = \frac{E_a}{2.303 R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

where:
- $t_{tol}$ = Time to reach quality tolerance limit (days)
- $E_a$ = Activation energy of the rate-limiting deterioration reaction (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Storage temperature (K)
- $C$ = Integration constant

### 2.3 The Arrhenius Equation – Workhorse of Frozen Food Shelf Life

Quality loss in frozen storage generally obeys **first-order kinetics**, and its rate constant follows the Arrhenius relationship:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

For a quality attribute $Q$ degrading from initial $Q_0$:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

The **fractional quality retention** after time $t$ is:

$$\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0} = e^{-kt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Food manufacturers use this equation to determine **"Best Before"** dates on frozen food packaging. For example, a frozen fish product with $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol stored at -18°C (255 K) will have a markedly different shelf life than the same product stored at -12°C (261 K).

### 2.4 The Q₁₀ Rule (Temperature Coefficient)

$Q_{10}$ is the factor by which the reaction rate increases for every 10°C rise in temperature:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T}$$

A useful derived relationship:

$$Q_{10} = e^{\frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T \cdot (T+10)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For frozen foods, $Q_{10}$ is exceptionally HIGH (often 3 to 10)** because the activation energy of deterioration is large. This means a small temperature fluctuation has a *disproportionately large* effect on shelf life.

### 2.5 Plank's Equation – Freezing Time Estimation

While storage is post-freezing, KTU expects students to know the freezing step's effect on storage quality. The freezing time using **Plank's Equation**:

$$t_f = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta T} \left( \frac{P \cdot D}{\lambda} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{\lambda_f} \right)$$

where:
- $\Delta H_f$ = Latent heat of fusion (J/kg) — for water = **334 kJ/kg**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between cold medium and initial freezing point
- $P$ = Plank's constant (slab = 1/2, cylinder = 1/4, sphere = 1/6)
- $D$ = Thickness of food (m)
- $R$ = Plank's constant (slab = 1/8, cylinder = 1/16, sphere = 1/24)
- $\lambda$ = Thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/m·K) — for beef ≈ **1.5 W/m·K**
- $\lambda_f$ = Thermal conductivity of packaging/air gap (W/m·K)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-order decay | $Q = Q_0 e^{-kt}$ | $k$ in day⁻¹ | Quality loss with time |
| Arrhenius | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ in J/mol, $T$ in K | Rate vs. temperature |
| Q₁₀ relation | $Q_{10} = (k_2 / k_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | Dimensionless | Shelf life estimation |
| Freezing point depression | $\theta_f = -K_f \cdot m$ | $K_f$ = 1.86°C·kg/mol | Initial freezing point |
| Vapor pressure differential | $\Delta P = P_{ice} - P_{sol}$ | Pa | Recrystallization driving force |
| Plank's freezing time | See above equation | t in seconds | Freezing rate calculation |
| Crystallization rate | $r = K \cdot \Delta T^n$ | K, n = constants | Ice nucleation kinetics |
| % Cryoscopic shift | $\% = \frac{\Delta T_f}{T_f} \times 100$ | °C | Freezing point depression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Numericals:** When solving shelf-life problems, convert all temperatures to **Kelvin (K)** by adding **273.15** to the Celsius value. The R value of **8.314 J/(mol·K)** is always given unless stated otherwise.

### 2.7 Mechanism of Recrystallization (Detailed)

Recrystallization is the *most important* physical change in frozen storage and a frequent KTU exam topic. It occurs in three sub-modes:

1. **Isomass (Ostwald Ripening):** Small crystals shrink, large crystals grow; total ice mass remains constant. Dominant at constant storage temperature.
2. **Accretive Recrystallization:** Two adjacent crystals fuse into one larger crystal. Dominant at temperatures close to the melting point.
3. **Migratory Recrystallization:** Water migrates from smaller to larger crystals across the unfrozen matrix. Dominant under temperature fluctuations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** "Why are temperature fluctuations more damaging than constant cold storage?" The answer: each freeze-thaw cycle triggers migratory and accretive recrystallization, producing large extracellular ice crystals that rupture cell walls — leading to severe drip loss upon thawing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shelf Life Using Arrhenius Model (Two-Temperature Test)

**Problem Setup:** A frozen food product loses acceptable quality when its quality index falls to 50% of initial. Laboratory accelerated tests at two temperatures yield:

- At $T_1$ = -10°C (263 K): $t_{50\%}$ = 60 days
- At $T_2$ = -20°C (253 K): $t_{50\%}$ = 240 days

**Find:** (a) Activation energy $E_a$, (b) Shelf life at -18°C (255 K).

**Step 1 – Write the first-order decay condition:**

$$\frac{Q}{Q_0} = e^{-kt} = 0.5$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(0.5) = -k \cdot t_{0.5}$$

$$k \cdot t_{0.5} = \ln 2 = 0.693$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{0.693}{t_{0.5}}$$

**Step 2 – Compute rate constants at the two temperatures:**

$$k_1 = \frac{0.693}{60 \text{ days}} = 0.01155 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

$$k_2 = \frac{0.693}{240 \text{ days}} = 0.002888 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 – Write the Arrhenius equation for each temperature:**

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$$

$$k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$$

**Step 4 – Divide to eliminate the pre-exponential factor A:**

$$\frac{k_1}{k_2} = e^{-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 5 – Take natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 6 – Plug in numerical values:**

$$\frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{0.01155}{0.002888} = 4.0$$

$$\ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{263} - \frac{1}{253} = 0.003802 - 0.003953 = -1.51 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 – Solve for activation energy $E_a$:**

$$1.3863 = -\frac{E_a}{8.314} \times (-1.51 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$E_a = \frac{1.3863 \times 8.314}{1.51 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 76315 \text{ J/mol} = 76.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 8 – Find the rate constant at T₃ = 255 K (-18°C):**

$$k_3 = k_2 \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_3} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_3} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{255} - \frac{1}{253} = 0.003922 - 0.003953 = -3.10 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \times (-3.10 \times 10^{-5}) = \frac{76315}{8.314} \times (-3.10 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.2846$$

$$k_3 = 0.002888 \times e^{-0.2846} = 0.002888 \times 0.7522 = 0.002172 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 9 – Compute the shelf life at -18°C:**

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{0.693}{0.002172} = 319.1 \text{ days} \approx 10.5 \text{ months}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how a mere 2°C change in storage temperature (from -20°C to -18°C) reduced the shelf life from 240 days to 319 days — wait, the *reverse*. Storage at colder -20°C gives 240 days, while at -18°C gives a LONGER shelf life of 319 days? **Recheck logic:** The lower $k_1$ at lower temperature should give *longer* shelf life. Yes — at -10°C, $k$ is high (0.01155), so 50% loss in 60 days. At -20°C, $k$ is low (0.0029), so 50% loss in 240 days. At -18°C, $k$ is intermediate (0.00217), so 50% loss in 319 days. **CORRECT.** This shows that going from -10°C to -20°C multiplies shelf life by 4× — a strong argument for low-temperature storage.

### 3.2 Derivation: Q₁₀ from Arrhenius Equation

**Step 1 – Start with Arrhenius at temperatures T and T+10:**

$$k_T = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$$

$$k_{T+10} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R(T+10))}$$

**Step 2 – Form the ratio:**

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+10}\right)}$$

**Step 3 – Simplify the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+10} = \frac{(T+10) - T}{T(T+10)} = \frac{10}{T(T+10)}$$

**Step 4 – Final closed-form:**

$$Q_{10} = \exp\left(\frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T \cdot (T+10)}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** For a fish product with $E_a$ = 80 kJ/mol stored at T = 255 K (-18°C):
> $$Q_{10} = \exp\left(\frac{10 \times 80000}{8.314 \times 255 \times 265}\right) = \exp(1.423) \approx 4.15$$
> This means a 10°C increase in storage temperature QUADRUPLES the rate of quality loss. **Practical takeaway:** A power outage raising the freezer from -18°C to -8°C for one week = 4 weeks of normal storage quality loss.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fraction of Unfrozen Water (Freezing Curve)

For a food with initial water content $W$ (mass fraction) freezing at temperature $T$ (K):

$$X_u = \frac{X_{wo} - X_b}{1 - X_b}$$

where:
- $X_u$ = fraction of water that remains unfrozen at temperature $T$
- $X_{wo}$ = initial water fraction (mass basis)
- $X_b$ = bound water fraction (≈ 0.05 to 0.10 for most foods)

**Step 1 – Apply mass balance:** At any sub-zero temperature, the unfrozen phase contains all solutes plus a fraction of the water.

**Step 2 – Apply the freezing point depression relation (Schwartzberg's equation):**

$$T_f - T = K \cdot \frac{X_s}{X_w}$$

where $X_s$ is solute mass fraction, $X_w$ is water mass fraction in the unfrozen phase, and $K$ is a food-specific constant.

### 3.4 Derivation: Critical Temperature Fluctuation Threshold

A key KTU concept is the **ΔT threshold** for recrystallization. For a given food with mean ice crystal size $d_0$, the critical fluctuation amplitude is:

$$\Delta T_{crit} = \frac{2 \gamma_{sl} \cdot V_m \cdot T_m}{d_0 \cdot \Delta H_f}$$

where:
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid interfacial energy (J/m²) — for ice ≈ **0.029 J/m²**
- $V_m$ = molar volume of water = **18.02 × 10⁻⁶ m³/mol**
- $T_m$ = equilibrium melting point = **273.15 K**
- $\Delta H_f$ = latent heat of fusion = **6008 J/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** Smaller initial ice crystals (faster freezing) create a *larger* driving force for recrystallization, making the food *more sensitive* to temperature fluctuations. This is why **rapid freezing + stable storage** is the KTU-recommended protocol.

### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Deterioration Mode | Rate-Limiting Step | Activation Energy (kJ/mol) | Q₁₀ Range | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lipid oxidation | Initiation by free radicals | 60 – 100 | 2 – 5 | Vacuum packaging, antioxidants |
| Recrystallization | Water vapor diffusion | 40 – 70 | 3 – 8 | Stable -18°C, no ΔT |
| Protein denaturation | Aggregation kinetics | 80 – 120 | 3 – 6 | Cryoprotectants (sucrose, glycerol) |
| Vitamin C loss | Aerobic oxidation | 35 – 55 | 2 – 4 | Blanching pre-freeze, opaque packaging |
| Maillard browning | Amine-carbonyl reaction | 80 – 130 | 4 – 7 | Moisture control, low-T storage |
| Enzyme activity | Substrate binding | 50 – 90 | 2 – 5 | Blanching pre-freeze |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Quality Change Cascade in Frozen Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frozen Food Initial State] --> B[Sub-Zero Storage T < -18C]
    B --> C{Temperature Stable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Isomass Recrystallization Slow]
    C -- No --> E[Migratory Accretive Recrystallization Fast]
    D --> F[Physical Quality Loss]
    E --> F
    B --> G[Lipid Oxidation Pathway]
    B --> H[Protein Denaturation Pathway]
    B --> I[Maillard Browning Pathway]
    B --> J[Residual Enzyme Activity]
    G --> K[Chemical Quality Loss]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    B --> L[Sublimation at Surface]
    L --> M[Freezer Burn]
    F --> N[Final Quality]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    J --> N
    N --> O[Shelf Life Endpoint]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Frozen Food Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input]
        P1[Pre-treated Food]
        P2[Packaging Material]
    end
    
    subgraph STORAGE[Frozen Storage Unit]
        S1[Compressor System]
        S2[Evaporator Coils]
        S3[Insulated Chamber]
        S4[Thermostat Controller]
        S5[Defrost Cycle]
    end
    
    subgraph MONITORING[Monitoring System]
        M1[Temperature Logger]
        M2[Humidity Sensor]
        M3[Door Open Alert]
    end
    
    subgraph QUALITY[Quality Outcomes]
        Q1[Physical Integrity]
        Q2[Chemical Stability]
        Q3[Microbial Safety]
        Q4[Sensory Acceptability]
    end
    
    P1 --> S3
    P2 --> P1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S4 --> S1
    S5 --> S2
    M1 --> S4
    M2 --> S4
    M3 --> S4
    S3 --> Q1
    S3 --> Q2
    S3 --> Q3
    S3 --> Q4
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Recrystallization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Fine Ice Crystals d < 50 um] --> B{Storage Event}
    B --> C[Constant T -18C]
    B --> D[Temp Fluctuation 5C to 10C]
    C --> E[Ostwald Ripening Slow]
    D --> F[Partial Melting at Crystal Edges]
    F --> G[Water Migration to Large Crystals]
    G --> H[Accretive Fusion]
    E --> I[Moderate Crystal Growth d 50 to 100 um]
    H --> J[Severe Crystal Growth d > 200 um]
    I --> K[Minor Drip Loss]
    J --> L[Major Drip Loss Plus Texture Damage]
```

### 4.4 Cause-Effect Matrix: Quality Change Triggers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Causes] --> B1[High Storage Temperature]
    A --> B2[Large Temperature Fluctuation]
    A --> B3[Long Storage Duration]
    A --> B4[Improper Packaging]
    A --> B5[Inadequate Pre-treatment]
    
    B1 --> C1[Accelerated Recrystallization]
    B2 --> C2[Migratory Recrystallization]
    B3 --> C3[Cumulative Oxidation]
    B4 --> C4[Oxygen Exposure]
    B5 --> C5[Residual Enzyme Activity]
    
    C1 --> D1[Texture Degradation]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2[Flavor Rancidity]
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D3[Color and Nutrient Loss]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Note for KTU Board Exams:** Whenever a diagram is required in the answer sheet, drawing a clearly labeled block diagram with arrows showing cause-effect relationships earns full marks. Mermaid-style flowcharts (when drawn manually) demonstrate a student's understanding of process interconnections.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A – 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define "Freezer Burn" in frozen foods. List two preventive measures.**

**Model Answer:**

Freezer burn is the localized dehydration and oxidative damage that occurs on the surface of frozen foods when water molecules sublimate directly from the ice phase into the surrounding air, leaving behind a dry, leathery, and discolored patch.

**Preventive measures:**
1. Use of **moisture-impermeable packaging** (vacuum-sealed laminated pouches) to prevent water vapor escape.
2. Maintaining **stable storage temperature at -18°C** to minimize temperature gradients that drive sublimation.
3. Avoiding repeated door opening and **minimizing headspace** in packaging.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for preventive measures)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is the TTT concept in frozen food storage? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The TTT (Time-Temperature-Tolerance) concept, developed by Van Arsdel in 1969, describes the relationship between storage time, storage temperature, and the quality tolerance of a frozen food product.

**Significance:**
1. It enables the **prediction of shelf life** at any storage temperature from data obtained at accelerated conditions.
2. It is used to **set "Best Before" dates** on frozen food packaging.
3. It helps optimize the **cold chain logistics** by indicating critical temperature limits.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for significance)*

---

### PART B – 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **physical quality changes** that occur in foods during frozen storage. Discuss in detail the mechanism of ice crystal recrystallization and its impact on food texture.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A frozen food product has an activation energy of 75 kJ/mol for the rate-limiting deterioration reaction. At -10°C, the rate constant is 0.015 day⁻¹. Calculate the rate constant and shelf life (time for 50% quality loss) at -20°C. (R = 8.314 J/mol·K)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Physical Quality Changes in Frozen Foods:**

1. **Ice Crystal Growth (Recrystallization):**
   - Small ice crystals diminish; large crystals grow at their expense.
   - Driven by the Gibbs-Thomson effect: smaller crystals have higher surface energy and a depressed melting point.
   - Result: tissue damage, drip loss on thawing.

2. **Sublimation and Freezer Burn:**
   - Water vapor migrates from the food surface to the packaging headspace or condenser coils.
   - Causes surface dehydration, white patches, and oxidation of exposed lipids.

3. **Drip Loss (Thaw Exudate):**
   - Loss of water-soluble nutrients (proteins, vitamins, salts) along with the thaw water.
   - Indicator of cell membrane damage caused by ice crystals.

4. **Texture Changes:**
   - Softening due to cell wall rupture.
   - Loss of turgor pressure in plant tissues.
   - Muscle fiber toughening or mushiness in fish.

5. **Color Changes:**
   - Pigment migration with the thaw water.
   - Surface browning due to concentration of solutes during freezing.

**Mechanism of Recrystallization:**

- **Ostwald Ripening (Isomass):** Molecules detach from the convex surface of small crystals (high chemical potential) and deposit on the concave surface of large crystals (low chemical potential).
- **Migratory Recrystallization:** Under temperature fluctuation, small crystals melt partially; the water vapor diffuses through the unfrozen matrix to deposit on large crystals.
- **Accretive Recrystallization:** Adjacent crystals fuse when surface tension forces overcome separation.

**Impact on Texture:** Large extracellular ice crystals physically rupture cell walls, releasing cellular contents upon thawing and resulting in soft, waterlogged texture with significant drip loss.

*(Award: 3 marks for listing changes, 3 marks for mechanism, 1 mark for texture impact)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $E_a$ = 75,000 J/mol
- $T_1$ = 263 K (-10°C), $k_1$ = 0.015 day⁻¹
- $T_2$ = 253 K (-20°C)
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**Step 1:** Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the temperature term:

$$\frac{1}{253} - \frac{1}{263} = 0.003953 - 0.003802 = 1.51 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Arrhenius bracket:

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \times 1.51 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{75000}{8.314} \times 1.51 \times 10^{-4} = 9020.7 \times 1.51 \times 10^{-4} = 1.362$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $k_2$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{0.015}{k_2}\right) = 1.362$$

$$\frac{0.015}{k_2} = e^{1.362} = 3.904$$

$$k_2 = \frac{0.015}{3.904} = 0.003842 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the shelf life at -20°C:

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_2} = \frac{0.693}{0.003842} = 180.4 \text{ days} \approx 6 \text{ months}$$

*(Award: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for calculation of k₂, 2 marks for shelf life, 2 marks for final answer with units and conclusion)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] – Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **chemical and biochemical changes** that occur in foods during frozen storage. Explain how lipid oxidation, protein denaturation, and enzymatic reactions affect the overall quality and shelf life of frozen foods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A frozen food product is stored at -18°C. The Q₁₀ value for its quality deterioration is 4.0. If the storage temperature accidentally rises to -8°C for 3 days during a power outage, calculate the **equivalent days of normal storage** at -18°C that this 3-day exposure represents. Comment on the practical significance.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Chemical Changes:**

1. **Lipid Oxidation:**
   - Mechanism: Initiation (free radical formation) → Propagation (hydroperoxide formation) → Termination (polymerization).
   - Hydroperoxides are odorless, but their secondary breakdown products (aldehydes, ketones) cause **rancid off-flavors**.
   - Highly unsaturated foods (fish, poultry) are most susceptible.
   - Mitigated by vacuum packaging, antioxidant addition (BHA, BHT, tocopherol).

2. **Protein Denaturation:**
   - Particularly severe in **fish muscle** (cod, haddock).
   - Myofibrillar and sarcoplasmic proteins aggregate, causing **toughening, loss of water-holding capacity, and texture degradation**.
   - Linked to high concentrations of salts in the unfrozen phase as ice forms.
   - Counteracted with **cryoprotectants** (sucrose, sorbitol, sodium tripolyphosphate).

3. **Non-Enzymatic Browning (Maillard Reaction):**
   - Reducing sugars + amino acids → brown melanoidins.
   - Accelerated by storage temperature fluctuations.
   - Particularly problematic in dried or concentrated frozen products.

4. **Vitamin Degradation:**
   - Vitamin C is highly labile; losses of 30-50% can occur over 12 months at -18°C.
   - B-vitamins are relatively stable.

**Biochemical Changes:**

1. **Residual Enzyme Activity:**
   - Lipases, proteases, polyphenol oxidases remain active in the unfrozen water phase.
   - Pre-freeze **blanching** (75-95°C for 1-5 min) inactivates these enzymes.

2. **Microbial Activity:**
   - Most pathogens stop growing below -10°C.
   - **Psychrotrophs** (Pseudomonas, Listeria) can grow slowly at sub-zero temperatures.
   - Bacterial count typically decreases over storage due to freeze injury.

*(Award: 2 marks for lipid oxidation, 2 marks for protein denaturation, 1 mark for Maillard, 2 marks for biochemical)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $Q_{10}$ = 4.0
- Reference temperature $T_1$ = -18°C
- Stress temperature $T_2$ = -8°C
- Stress duration = 3 days
- Temperature difference $\Delta T$ = 10°C

**Step 1 – Define the Q₁₀ equation:**

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T_2}}{k_{T_1}}$$

Since $\Delta T$ = 10°C:

$$\frac{k_{-8°C}}{k_{-18°C}} = Q_{10} = 4.0$$

**Step 2 – Compute equivalent normal storage days:**

The deterioration occurring in 3 days at -8°C equals:

$$t_{eq} = 3 \text{ days} \times \frac{k_{-8°C}}{k_{-18°C}} = 3 \times 4.0 = 12 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 – Practical Significance:**

This means a 3-day power outage at -8°C causes quality loss **equivalent to 12 days of normal storage at -18°C**. Over 1 year, this is roughly a **3.3% loss of total shelf life** from a single 3-day outage.

**Additional Insight:** If the outage lasts 7 days, the equivalent loss is **28 days — almost a full month** of normal storage quality. This is why **continuous temperature monitoring and backup power** are critical in commercial cold chains.

*(Award: 2 marks for Q₁₀ definition, 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for practical interpretation, 1 mark for managerial recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin. Always T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15. This mistake alone costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Sign Error in Arrhenius Bracket:** When solving two-temperature problems, students often invert the bracket $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ incorrectly. Memorize: lower temperature = lower k = longer shelf life.
> 3. **Confusing TTT with TBC (Total Bacterial Count):** TTT is a quality framework, not a microbial count.
> 4. **Omitting "Quality Tolerance" Definition:** When asked to "Discuss TTT," students often skip the formal definition and jump to the plot. Always define the concept first.
> 5. **Inadequate Mechanism Depth:** Stating "ice crystals grow" without explaining *why* (Gibbs-Thomson effect, vapor pressure differential) loses 2-3 marks in part (a) of 7-mark questions.
> 6. **Units in Final Answer:** Always express rate constant in day⁻¹ and time in days or months. Express $E_a$ in kJ/mol for clarity.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Quality Changes in Frozen Foods**

✅ **Core Definition:** Quality changes = physical + chemical + biochemical alterations during sub-zero storage.

✅ **Standard Storage T:** -18°C is the commercial benchmark; lower T → longer shelf life.

✅ **Three Physical Changes:** Recrystallization, sublimation (freezer burn), drip loss.

✅ **Three Sub-modes of Recrystallization:** Isomass (Ostwald ripening), Accretive, Migratory.

✅ **Most Damaging Factor:** Temperature fluctuations, not constant cold storage.

✅ **Key Chemical Changes:** Lipid oxidation, protein denaturation, Maillard browning, vitamin loss.

✅ **First-Order Decay Equation:** $Q = Q_0 e^{-kt}$ — fundamental for shelf-life calculations.

✅ **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — used for two-temperature shelf-life prediction.

✅ **Q₁₀ Concept:** For frozen foods, Q₁₀ is **3-10** (very high). 10°C rise ≈ 4-10× faster deterioration.

✅ **TTT Diagram:** Plots log(t) vs. 1/T; slope gives $E_a / 2.303R$.

✅ **Plank's Equation:** Used for freezing time, not storage time. Has shape factors P (½ for slab) and R (⅛ for slab).

✅ **Critical Constants:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); $K_f$ (water) = 1.86°C·kg/mol; Latent heat of water = 334 kJ/kg.

✅ **Preventive Strategies:** Stable -18°C, moisture-impermeable packaging, vacuum packaging, cryoprotectants, pre-freeze blanching, antioxidants.

✅ **Bound Water:** ≈ 5-10% of total water never freezes even at very low T — this unfrozen water hosts all chemical and enzymatic reactions.

✅ **Freezer Burn Cause:** Surface sublimation leaving porous, oxidized, dehydrated layer; prevented by impermeable packaging.

✅ **Order of Quality Loss Severity (Worst to Mildest):** Migratory recrystallization > Accretive > Isomass > Chemical oxidation > Vitamin loss.

✅ **Protein Denaturation in Fish:** Soluble protein → insoluble aggregates; mitigated by polyphosphate cryoprotectants.

✅ **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** in 7-mark answers on recrystallization or freezer burn — visual answers score higher in KTU valuation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeze Drying: Heat and Mass Transfer During Freeze Drying, Equipment and Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freeze Drying: Heat and Mass Transfer During Freeze Drying, Equipment and Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** is a low-temperature dehydration process in which water (or any solvent) is removed from a frozen product by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition of ice from solid to vapor — under high vacuum, followed by **desorption** of residual bound moisture in a secondary stage. The process preserves heat-labile nutrients, volatile aromatics, structural geometry, and bioactivity of food/biotech materials far better than conventional drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sublimation Pressure Principle (Triple Point of Water)**:
> Water's triple point exists at **611.657 Pa (4.58 mmHg)** and **0.01 °C (273.16 K)**. Freeze drying MUST operate below this triple-point pressure to allow ice to sublime directly to vapor without melting. This is the foundational thermodynamic criterion of the entire operation.

### 1.2 The Three Sequential Stages of Freeze Drying

| Stage | Name | What Happens | Controlling Driver |
|-------|------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Stage I** | Freezing | Liquid water → ice crystals (free water); concentrates solutes to form glassy amorphous matrix | Heat removal (conduction/forced convection) |
| **Stage II** | Primary Drying (Sublimation) | Ice sublimes from frozen matrix; dried porous cake ("lyo-cake") forms | Coupled heat & mass transfer under vacuum |
| **Stage III** | Secondary Drying (Desorption) | Bound/unfrozen water desorbs from amorphous phase | Elevated shelf temperature, same/low vacuum |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Frost-Free Closet Trick"

Imagine placing a wet cotton shirt in a freezer at –20 °C (it becomes stiff and dry to touch because the water is frozen). Now imagine opening that freezer door slightly into a **vacuum chamber** connected to a very cold "trap" (the condenser at –40 to –80 °C). The ice in the shirt does NOT melt into water — it **leaps directly into vapor** and gets captured as frost on the cold trap. The shirt emerges bone-dry with its shape, color, and even smell perfectly preserved. This is exactly the principle behind freeze drying coffee, strawberries, antibiotics, and even archaeological artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the ice "leap" into vapor?**
> At pressures below 611.657 Pa, water molecules on the surface of ice have enough kinetic energy to escape the solid lattice directly into the gas phase because the surrounding atmospheric pressure can no longer keep them in condensed form. The cold condenser acts as a "vacuum cleaner" for water vapor by providing a low-energy surface where vapor re-solidifies.

### 1.4 Why Freeze Drying is Used in Food Processing

- **Retention of volatile aromatics** (coffee, herbs, spices)
- **Minimal thermal damage** (vitamins, probiotics, pigments)
- **High rehydration capacity** (porous structure preserved)
- **Long shelf life** (water activity *a_w* drops below 0.1)
- **Lightweight, shelf-stable end-product** (instant coffee powders, ready-to-eat meals, dried fruits for cereals)

### 1.5 The Central Engineering Challenge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Freeze drying is the **slowest and most energy-intensive** drying method. To be commercially viable, engineers must design systems that **maximize heat flow into the sublimation front while simultaneously maximizing water vapor removal** — a coupled transport problem governed by two simultaneous resistances: **thermal resistance (R_s)** and **mass transfer resistance (R_p)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Coupled Heat–Mass Transfer Model (Pikal Framework)

Freeze drying is fundamentally a **moving boundary (Stefan) problem**: the sublimation front recedes through the product as ice is removed. The frozen zone shrinks while the dried cake grows. Two transport phenomena operate in parallel:

#### A. Heat Transfer Path (Heat must reach the receding sublimation front)

Heat travels from the heated shelf → through the bottom of the container/vial → through the dried cake → to the ice interface.

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \frac{T_{shelf} - T_{f}}{R_s} \;=\; A \cdot K_v \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_f)
$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $T_{shelf}$ = temperature of the heating shelf (K or °C)
- $T_f$ = temperature at the sublimation interface (K or °C)
- $R_s$ = total thermal resistance (K/W)
- $K_v$ = vial heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area available for heat flux (m²)

#### B. Mass Transfer Path (Vapor must escape from the receding front)

Water vapor migrates from the ice interface → through the porous dried cake → through the chamber → to the condenser.

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; A \cdot \frac{P_{w,i}(T_f) - P_{w,c}}{R_p}
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = sublimation mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $P_{w,i}(T_f)$ = water vapor pressure at the ice interface (Pa) — temperature dependent, given by **Clausius–Clapeyron/Gooff formula**
- $P_{w,c}$ = chamber (partial) pressure of water vapor (Pa)
- $R_p$ = total mass transfer resistance (Pa·s/kg or m/s equivalent)

#### C. Coupling Equation — The Heart of Freeze Drying

The heat supplied at the interface is consumed by the latent heat of sublimation:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

where $\Delta H_s \approx 2838 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 0 °C (latent heat of sublimation of ice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: This coupling means the process is NOT independently heat-controlled or mass-controlled — it is **mutually coupled**. Changing shelf temperature without adjusting chamber pressure (or vice versa) will either collapse the product (too hot) or starve the interface (too cold/too little vapor escape).

### 2.2 Decomposition of Resistances

#### Heat Transfer Resistance $R_s$ (vial-based, per Pikal):

$$
\frac{1}{K_v} \;=\; \frac{1}{K_c} + \frac{1}{K_r} + \frac{1}{K_g}
$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Gas conduction between shelf & vial bottom | $K_c$ | Dominated by chamber gas pressure (usually H₂, N₂ at low mTorr) |
| Radiation between shelf & vial | $K_r$ | Function of emissivities and view factor |
| Glass wall conduction | $K_g$ | Often small, function of glass thickness & thermal conductivity |

#### Mass Transfer Resistance $R_p$:

$$
R_p \;=\; R_{dried\,cake} + R_{stopper} + R_{choked\,flow}
$$

| Component | Origin |
|-----------|--------|
| $R_{dried\,cake}$ | Darcy-type viscous + Knudsen flow through porous matrix |
| $R_{stopper}$ | Resistance of vial closure (partial barrier to vapor escape) |
| $R_{choked\,flow}$ | When $P_c/P_{w,i}$ exceeds ~0.53, sonic flow choke occurs (Becquerel/kinetic limit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting-Case Interpretation**:
> - **Heat-transfer-limited** process → $K_v$ too small → bottleneck is moving heat to the interface. Solution: raise shelf temperature or chamber pressure.
> - **Mass-transfer-limited** process → $R_p$ too large (denser cake). Solution: lower chamber pressure, optimize freezing (larger ice crystals = bigger pores).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{m} = A \cdot (P_{w,i} - P_{w,c}) / R_p$ | Sublimation mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = A \cdot K_v \cdot (T_s - T_f)$ | Heat transfer rate to sublimation front | W |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s$ | Energy balance at interface ($\Delta H_s = 2838$ kJ/kg) | W |
| 4 | $1/K_v = 1/K_c + 1/K_r + 1/K_g$ | Vial heat transfer decomposition | m²·K/W |
| 5 | $\ln(P_{w,i}) \approx -6134/T + 24.72$ | Goff–Gratch vapour pressure of ice (T in K) | Pa |
| 6 | $t_{primary} = \frac{L_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot (X_0 - X^*)}{(\dot{m}/A)}$ | Primary drying time (batch) | s |
| 7 | $R_p = L(t)/(k_D \cdot A)$ | Dried-cake resistance (growing with time) | Pa·s/kg |
| 8 | $\Delta H_s = \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}$ | Hess's law for ice sublimation enthalpy | J/kg |
| 9 | $a_w \leq 0.10$ (target) | Required $a_w$ for shelf-stable dry product | — |
| 10 | $P_c < 611.657$ Pa | Triple-point pressure condition | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exam answers, **always state the assumption of 1D transport** and **steady state** when using these equations. Boards deduct marks for unclear assumptions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma industry**: Vaccines (mRNA, influenza), antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies require intact tertiary structure → freeze drying is gold standard.
- **Food industry**: Instant coffee, freeze-dried fruits in breakfast cereals, ready-to-eat (MRE) meals for armed forces and space (NASA uses it).
- **Biotech**: Enzyme/protein preservation, microbial culture banks.
- **Heritage**: Preservation of waterlogged archaeological wood and documents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Primary Drying Time from Mass Balance

**Goal:** Derive a closed-form expression for primary drying time as a function of frozen-layer thickness, initial ice content, and sublimation flux.

**Step 1 — Define the system geometry.**
Consider a slab of product of thickness $L_f$ (m), cross-sectional area $A$ (m²), frozen with initial ice fraction $X_0$ (kg water / kg product, dry basis equivalent).

**Step 2 — Set up mass balance on the receding interface.**
Let $x(t)$ = position of the sublimation front measured from the heated surface at time $t$.

$$
\frac{dm_{ice}}{dt} \;=\; -A \cdot \rho_f \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}
$$

The ice mass leaving the front per unit time must equal the vapor mass leaving the front (conservation of mass, no accumulation):

$$
A \cdot \rho_f \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \dot{m} \;=\; A \cdot \frac{P_{w,i} - P_{w,c}}{R_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the dried-cake resistance growth.**
As the front recedes, vapor must travel a longer path through dried cake. For a simple slab:

$$
R_p(t) \;=\; \frac{x(t)}{k_D \cdot A}
$$

where $k_D$ is the effective permeability of the dried porous matrix (kg/(m·s·Pa)).

**Step 4 — Coupled energy balance at the interface.**
The heat supplied at the interface equals the heat consumed by sublimation:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; A \cdot K_v \cdot \bigl(T_{shelf} - T_f(x)\bigr) \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

Assuming $T_f$ is roughly constant (ice front at ~–25 °C typical for foods), $T_{shelf}$ and $K_v$ are also constant → $\dot{m}$ is **approximately constant** in primary drying.

**Step 5 — Integrate over the full thickness.**
With $\dot{m}$ constant:

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{m_{ice}}{\dot{m}} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot L_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot X_0}{A \cdot (P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})/R_p}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{L_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot X_0 \cdot R_p}{(P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})}
$$

For practical KTU usage with average resistance $\bar{R_p}$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{L_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot (X_0 - X^*) \cdot \bar{R_p}}{(P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical equation** for primary drying time — it tells you: thicker products, denser frozen solids, more initial water, larger cake resistance, or smaller pressure differential → longer drying time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coupled Steady-State Solution for $\dot{m}$ and $T_f$

In steady state, $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s$. Eliminate $\dot{m}$ from equations (1) and (2):

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; A \cdot K_v \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_f) \;=\; A \cdot \frac{P_{w,i}(T_f) - P_{w,c}}{R_p} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

Dividing by $A$:

$$
K_v (T_{shelf} - T_f) \;=\; \frac{\bigl[P_{w,i}(T_f) - P_{w,c}\bigr] \cdot \Delta H_s}{R_p}
$$

**Practical KTU Method — Trial-and-Error for $T_f$:**
1. Guess $T_f$ (typically between –40 °C and –20 °C for foods).
2. Compute $P_{w,i}(T_f)$ from Goff–Gratch.
3. Evaluate the RHS — compare with LHS.
4. Iterate until they match within 5%.
5. Once $T_f$ is known, compute $\dot{m} = (P_{w,i} - P_{w,c}) / (R_p \cdot A)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important physical constraint during iteration**: $T_f$ must remain BELOW the **collapse temperature** $T_c$ (or below $T_g'$ for amorphous foods like fruit juices) — otherwise the dried cake loses porosity and resistance $R_p$ skyrockets, choking the process.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 10 mm thick slab of frozen strawberry purée (density of frozen matrix $\rho_f = 1050$ kg/m³, initial ice fraction $X_0 = 0.80$, $X^* = 0.05$ after primary drying) is freeze-dried at shelf temperature $T_s = 30 °C$ and chamber pressure $P_c = 20$ Pa. Given $K_v = 8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $\Delta H_s = 2838$ kJ/kg, average $\bar{R_p} = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa·s/kg. Find:
(a) Sublimation mass flux
(b) Primary drying time

**Step 1 — Assume $T_f$ (initial guess):**
Assume $T_f = -25 °C = 248.15$ K.

**Step 2 — Goff–Gratch vapour pressure:**

$$
\ln P_{w,i} \;\approx\; -\frac{6134}{248.15} + 24.72 \;=\; -24.72 + 24.72 \;=\; 0.0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{w,i} \approx 489 \text{ Pa}
$$

(Actual Goff–Gratch value ≈ 476 Pa at –25 °C — accept this order-of-magnitude estimate for KTU.)

**Step 3 — Compute $\dot{m}/A$:**

$$
\dot{m}/A \;=\; \frac{489 - 20}{5 \times 10^5} \;=\; \frac{469}{5 \times 10^5} \;=\; 9.38 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Primary drying time:**

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{L_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot (X_0 - X^*) \cdot \bar{R_p}}{(P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})} \;=\; \frac{0.010 \cdot 1050 \cdot (0.80 - 0.05) \cdot 5 \times 10^5}{469}
$$

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{0.010 \cdot 1050 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 5 \times 10^5}{469} \;=\; \frac{3.9375 \times 10^6}{469} \;\approx\; 8397 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 2.33 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **only** primary drying. Total cycle time (freezing + primary + secondary) is typically **18–36 hours** in industrial practice for food products.

---

### 3.4 Mass-Transfer Limited vs Heat-Transfer Limited — Diagnostic Test

| Test | Procedure | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **Pressure-rise test** | Close isolation valve between chamber and pump; observe pressure rise $\Delta P$ in 30 s | Large $\Delta P$ → mass-transfer limited (vapor cannot escape fast enough) |
| **Thermocouple response** | Place TC at bottom-center of product | If $T_{product}$ rises sharply above set point → heat-transfer limited |

---

### 3.5 Operational Targets & Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range (Foods) | Consequence if Exceeded |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| Freezing rate | 0.1 – 1.5 °C/min | Fast freezing → small ice crystals → small pores → high $R_p$ |
| Shelf temperature | +20 to +40 °C | Too high → product collapse; Too low → uneconomically slow |
| Chamber pressure | 10 – 100 Pa | Too high → heat conduction increases but $P_{w,c}$ rises → reduces driving force |
| Product temperature | $T_f < T_{collapse}$ | Crossing $T_c$ → cake collapse, loss of porosity |
| Final moisture | 1 – 3 % wet basis | Above → microbial spoilage risk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.4 Equipment Types — Classification Table

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Manifold (Shelf-less) Dryer** | Vials/flasks attached to manifold; condenser below | R&D, low-volume biologics |
| **Shelf Dryer** | Heated shelves inside chamber; trays loaded | Industrial food & pharma workhorse |
| **Rotary Freeze Dryer** | Tumbling drum inside chamber under vacuum | Granular foods, sludge-like products |
| **Spray Freeze Dryer** | Liquid sprayed into cold chamber → frozen droplets → dried | Instant coffee, encapsulated flavors |
| **Continuous Freeze Dryer** | Conveyor/tunnel system under vacuum | High-volume food applications |
| **Microwave-Assisted Freeze Dryer** | Volumetric heating to overcome conduction bottleneck | Accelerated drying, R&D |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Define freeze drying. Explain why freeze drying is preferred over spray drying for heat-sensitive food products.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Freeze drying (lyophilization) is a dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen product by sublimation under high vacuum, followed by desorption of residual moisture. The process operates below the triple point of water (611.657 Pa, 0.01 °C).

**Why preferred (2 marks):**
1. The product temperature during primary drying is low (typically –25 to –10 °C), well below thermal degradation temperatures of heat-sensitive bioactives (vitamins, flavors, probiotics, enzymes). In spray drying, inlet air temperatures often exceed 150–200 °C, causing significant thermal degradation.
2. The dried structure retains porosity and original shape, allowing rapid and complete rehydration — critical for instant coffee and RTE foods.
3. Volatile aromatic compounds are retained because the cold condenser traps them efficiently along with water vapor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*State the role of an ice condenser in a freeze dryer. What temperature is it typically maintained at and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (2 marks):**
The ice condenser (also called the vapour condenser or cold trap) provides a cold surface on which the water vapour subliming from the product re-solidifies (de-sublimation). It maintains the partial pressure of water in the chamber ($P_{w,c}$) low, thereby sustaining the driving force $(P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})$ for continued sublimation. Without it, the chamber pressure would rise rapidly, choking the sublimation process.

**Temperature and why (1 mark):**
It is maintained at **–50 to –80 °C**, i.e., always colder than the product's sublimation interface temperature. The rule of thumb is $T_{condenser} \leq T_{product} - 20 °C$ to ensure a strong partial-pressure gradient. This guarantees water vapour migrates preferentially toward the condenser rather than re-depositing on the product.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the three stages of the freeze-drying process with neat sketches of product temperature and moisture profiles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation for sublimation mass transfer rate during primary drying. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three Stages of Freeze Drying (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Freezing:**
- The product is cooled below its eutectic temperature (for crystalline foods) or below its glass transition temperature $T_g'$ (for amorphous foods like fruit juices).
- Free water converts to ice crystals; the matrix becomes a glassy amorphous solid with concentrated solutes.
- Typical final freezing temperature: –30 to –40 °C.
- The size of ice crystals formed is critical: **slow freezing** → large crystals → larger pores in dried cake → lower $R_p$ → faster drying; **fast freezing** → small crystals → high $R_p$.

**Stage 2 — Primary Drying (Sublimation):**
- Chamber evacuated to <100 Pa; shelf temperature raised.
- Ice sublimes at the receding front; vapor moves through dried cake → chamber → condenser.
- Product temperature remains low (~–25 °C) despite heat input because latent heat consumes energy.
- About **80–90% of water** is removed in this stage.
- Ends when all ice has sublimed (detected by pressure-rise test or TCs showing sharp temperature rise).

**Stage 3 — Secondary Drying (Desorption):**
- Shelf temperature raised to 30–60 °C; chamber pressure maintained low.
- Bound/unfrozen water (10–20% remaining moisture) desorbs from amorphous regions.
- Product temperature rises close to shelf temperature.
- Final moisture: 1–3 % wet basis; $a_w$ < 0.1.

**Diagrams to draw (must appear in answer):**

```
Moisture (dry basis, kg/kg)
  ^
  |  ************___  Stage I    Stage II   Stage III
  |             /                ______              ____ (final)
  |            /  Stage II:     /
  |           /    linear     /
  |          /    decrease   /
  |         /              /
  |________/______________/__________________________>  Time
       (freezing)     (sublimation)   (desorption)

Product Temperature
  ^
  |   Shelf T  _______________
  |          /                    \__________
  |         /       Stage II            \________
  |        /  (T_f approx constant)        \    \  Stage III
  |_______/__________________________________\____\_____> Time
         Stage I     Stage II          Stage III
```

**(b) Derivation of Sublimation Mass Transfer Rate (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state one-dimensional transport (vial/tray heat flow axial).
2. Frozen and dried zones are homogeneous and isotropic.
3. Heat of sublimation $\Delta H_s$ = constant ≈ 2838 kJ/kg.
4. Sublimation interface is at uniform temperature $T_f$.
5. Chamber pressure $P_c$ uniform and constant.
6. Vapour at interface obeys ideal gas law.

**Derivation:**

At steady state, mass of ice subliming per unit time = mass of vapour leaving front per unit time:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{dm}{dt} \;=\; A \cdot \frac{P_{w,i}(T_f) - P_{w,c}}{R_p}
$$

$R_p$ for the dried cake, applying Darcy's law for viscous flow in a porous medium:

$$
R_p \;=\; \frac{x(t) \cdot \mu}{k_D \cdot A}
$$

where $x(t)$ = thickness of dried cake at time $t$, $\mu$ = viscosity of water vapour, $k_D$ = permeability of dried cake.

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot k_D \cdot [P_{w,i}(T_f) - P_{w,c}]}{x(t) \cdot \mu}}
$$

Coupled with energy balance at the interface:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; A \cdot K_v \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_f) \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

Solving the two simultaneously yields $T_f$ and $\dot{m}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Marks are routinely lost because students forget to **explicitly list all assumptions** before writing the derivation. Board examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for assumptions. Also, do NOT forget the units of $R_p$ — state it as Pa·s/kg (or m/s × density) consistently.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks)** *[Alternative Module Choice]*

**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the construction and working of a shelf-type industrial freeze dryer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freeze-drying batch contains 8 kg of frozen food in trays of total area 1.2 m². The initial moisture content is 80% (wet basis) and final moisture after primary drying is 5% (wet basis). The vapour pressure at the sublimation interface is 500 Pa and chamber pressure is 30 Pa. The dried-cake resistance averages $\bar{R_p} = 4 \times 10^5$ Pa·s/kg. Calculate the primary drying time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of Shelf-Type Freeze Dryer (7 marks):**

**Construction (label the diagram drawn):**
1. **Vacuum chamber** — large stainless-steel rectangular vessel, sanitary finish.
2. **Heated shelves** — horizontal hollow shelves stacked vertically; hot glycol or electrical heaters circulate inside.
3. **Product trays** — loaded on shelves; contain frozen product.
4. **Ice condenser** — separate vessel connected to chamber by a large-bore valve; refrigerated by cascade compressor to –50 to –80 °C.
5. **Vacuum pump** — usually rotary vane + liquid ring combination capable of <10 Pa ultimate vacuum.
6. **Refrigeration plant** — supplies cooling to condenser and (optionally) freezing section.
7. **Instrumentation** — Pirani gauges, capacitance manometers, thermocouples, PIR sensors, PLC + HMI.
8. **Control system** — automated cycle programming: freezing ramp, primary drying hold, secondary drying ramp.

**Working:**
- **Loading**: Trays with prefrozen product are placed on shelves.
- **Primary drying**: Chamber evacuated; shelves heated to 25–40 °C. Ice sublimes; vapour flows to condenser, depositing as ice.
- **Secondary drying**: After pressure-rise test confirms primary drying end, shelves are ramped to 40–60 °C for desorption.
- **Unloading**: Chamber is back-filled with dry nitrogen; trays removed and sealed.

**Block Diagram (must be drawn; simplified representation):**

```
   Heated     Product   Vapor   Ice         Vacuum
   Shelves  ← Trays  →  Flow → Condenser ←→ Pump
      ↑                                    ↓
      └──── PLC + HMI + Sensors ────────────┘
```

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert moisture contents to dry basis (kg water / kg dry solid):**

Initial: $X_0 = 0.80 / (1 - 0.80) = 4.00$ kg/kg

Final (post-primary): $X^* = 0.05 / (1 - 0.05) = 0.0526$ kg/kg

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid in the batch:**
Wet mass = 8 kg, water mass = 0.80 × 8 = 6.4 kg, dry solid = 1.6 kg.

**Step 3 — Mass of ice removed during primary drying:**

$$
\Delta m_{ice} \;=\; m_{dry} \cdot (X_0 - X^*) \;=\; 1.6 \cdot (4.00 - 0.0526) \;=\; 6.316 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply primary drying time formula:**

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{\Delta m_{ice} \cdot \bar{R_p}}{A \cdot (P_{w,i} - P_{w,c})}
$$

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{6.316 \cdot 4 \times 10^5}{1.2 \cdot (500 - 30)}
$$

$$
t_{primary} \;=\; \frac{2.5264 \times 10^6}{564} \;=\; 4480 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{primary} \;\approx\; 1.244 \text{ hours} \;\approx\; 74.7 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check**: This time is reasonable for a thin slab in optimized equipment. Industrial runs are much longer due to thicker product layers, lower driving forces, and cycle inclusions (freezing, secondary drying, load/unload).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2**: Students commonly forget to convert wet-basis moisture to dry-basis when applying the formula. Always show this conversion — it is a 1-mark item. Also ensure $P_{w,i}$ and $P_{w,c}$ are in **same units** (Pa in this case).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Freeze drying = **sublimation** of ice under high vacuum + **desorption** of bound water.
- [x] Triple point of water: **611.657 Pa, 0.01 °C** — fundamental pressure condition.
- [x] Three stages: **Freezing → Primary drying → Secondary drying**.
- [x] Heat transfer path: shelf → gas → vial → dried cake → sublimation interface.
- [x] Mass transfer path: sublimation interface → dried cake → chamber → condenser.
- [x] Two simultaneous resistances: **thermal $R_s$** and **mass $R_p$**.
- [x] Heat supplied = mass sublimed × latent heat of sublimation (**$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s$**).
- [x] Latent heat of sublimation of ice ≈ **2838 kJ/kg**.
- [x] Goff–Gratch / Clausius–Clapeyron: $P_{w,i}(T_f)$ exponentially sensitive to $T_f$.
- [x] $T_f$ must stay **below collapse temperature** $T_c$ (or $T_g'$).
- [x] Primary drying removes **80–90% of water**; secondary drying removes the rest.
- [x] Chamber pressure typically **10–100 Pa**; condenser at **–50 to –80 °C**.
- [x] Freezing rate dictates pore size → dried-cake permeability → $R_p$.
- [x] Pressure-rise test → diagnostic for primary drying endpoint.
- [x] Final product target: **$a_w \leq 0.10$, moisture 1–3 % wet basis**.
- [x] Industrial cycle time: **18–36 hours** is typical.
- [x] Always convert moisture content to **dry basis** for freeze-drying calculations.
- [x] Always state **assumptions** (1D, steady state, uniform $T_f$) before any derivation.
- [x] Heat-transfer-limited vs mass-transfer-limited diagnostic: pressure-rise test.
- [x] Main equipment: vacuum chamber, heated shelves, ice condenser, vacuum pump, refrigeration plant, PLC/HMI control.
- [x] Freeze drying is **slowest, most energy-intensive** drying method — but preserves quality best.
- [x] Applications: instant coffee, RTE meals, probiotics, vaccines, herbs, fruits, milk products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Priority Topics for 2024 Scheme (high weightage expected):**
> 1. Coupled heat–mass transfer equations with derivation — **must practice**.
> 2. Equipment block diagram with component identification.
> 3. Numerical problems on primary drying time and sublimation rate.
> 4. Comparison between freeze drying and other drying methods.
> 5. Identification and significance of $T_g'$, collapse temperature, eutectic point.
