> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dehydration: Thermodynamics of Moist Air, Convective Drying, Drying Curve - Constant Rate, Falling Rate, Total Drying Time, Conductive Drying, Dryers in the Food Processing Industry - Cabinet, Tunnel, Belt, Belt-Trough, Rotary, Spray Dryers, Fluidized Bed Dryer, Pneumatic Dryer, Drum Dryer, Sun, Solar Drying, Issues in Food Drying Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Dehydration in Food Processing

**Dehydration** is defined as the simultaneous application of heat and removal of moisture from a food material to achieve a level of water activity ($a_w$) low enough to inhibit microbial growth and enzymatic activity, thereby extending shelf life. The KTU 2024 syllabus formally recognizes dehydration as a critical **Unit Operation** that combines heat transfer (energy supply) and mass transfer (moisture removal) into a single thermodynamic process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Drying is the removal of moisture from a material by the application of heat, causing vaporization of the water held within the product, and the subsequent removal of that vapor.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine hanging a wet cotton towel on a clothesline on a hot, breezy day. Three things happen simultaneously:
1. **The sun supplies energy (heat transfer)** to the water in the towel, raising the water molecules' energy.
2. **The molecules at the surface escape into the air (mass transfer)** — this is the "constant rate" phase where water flows freely to the surface.
3. As drying continues, the surface starts to dry, and the water has to travel from **inside the towel to the surface (internal diffusion)** — this is the "falling rate" phase, which is much slower.

In a food dryer, we engineer this natural process — controlling temperature, humidity, and airflow to achieve predictable, safe, and uniform drying. **Air is the "clothesline carrier"** in convective dryers; in conductive dryers, a **hot metal surface is the carrier** of heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Drying* refers to the removal of relatively small amounts of water (from a solid), while *Evaporation* usually refers to concentrating a solution (e.g., milk to milk powder). Both involve vaporization, but the engineering equipment differs.

## 1.2 Thermodynamics of Moist Air

**Moist Air** is a binary mixture of **dry air** (treated as a single non-condensable gas) and **water vapor** (the condensable component). The thermodynamic state of moist air is fully described by three independent properties — typically **dry-bulb temperature ($T_{db}$)**, **wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$)**, and **pressure ($P$)**.

### Fundamental Psychrometric Parameters

| Property | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulb Temperature | $T_{db}$ | °C | True air temperature measured by a standard thermometer |
| Wet Bulb Temperature | $T_{wb}$ | °C | Temperature reached when air is adiabatically saturated |
| Humidity Ratio (Absolute Humidity) | $Y$ or $\omega$ | kg water / kg dry air | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ or $RH$ | % (dimensionless ratio) | Ratio of partial pressure of vapor to saturation pressure at $T_{db}$ |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | m³/kg dry air | Volume occupied per unit mass of dry air |
| Enthalpy | $H$ or $h$ | kJ/kg dry air | Total heat content per unit mass of dry air |
| Dew Point Temperature | $T_{dp}$ | °C | Temperature at which vapor begins to condense on cooling |

### Standard Physical Constants Used in Drying (Bolded for Memory)

- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water at 100 °C:** $H_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water at 0 °C:** $H_{fg} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Molecular Weight of Water:** $M_w = 18.015 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Molecular Weight of Air:** $M_a = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

## 1.3 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer in Drying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the **convective** and **conductive** modes. Recognition of the third mode (radiation) is also expected.

1. **Convective Drying** — Heat is supplied by **hot air flowing over the food surface**. Used in cabinet, tunnel, belt, spray, fluidized bed, and rotary dryers. **Most common mode in food industry.**
2. **Conductive Drying (Contact Drying)** — Heat is supplied through a **hot metal surface** in direct contact with the food. Used in drum dryers and belt-trough dryers.
3. **Radiative Drying** — Heat is supplied by **infrared or microwave radiation**. Used in specialty applications (infrared finish dryers).

## 1.4 Classification of Food Dryers

A clear taxonomy is essential for KTU exams:

**Based on Mode of Heat Transfer:**
- **Convection-type:** Cabinet, Tunnel, Belt, Belt-Trough, Rotary, Spray, Fluidized Bed, Pneumatic, Solar
- **Conduction-type:** Drum Dryer
- **Radiation-type:** Infrared, Microwave (advanced topic)

**Based on Operating Pressure:**
- **Atmospheric (Most food dryers)**
- **Vacuum (heat-sensitive foods, expensive)**

**Based on Batch vs. Continuous Operation:**
- **Batch:** Cabinet, Sun, Solar cabinet
- **Continuous:** Tunnel, Belt, Spray, Fluidized Bed, Pneumatic, Rotary, Drum

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme places strong emphasis on the *principle, construction, working, applications, advantages, and disadvantages* of each named dryer. Memorize the table form for the 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Thermodynamics of Moist Air — The Saturation Pressure Law

The cornerstone property is the **saturation vapor pressure of water** ($P_{sat}$ or $P_g$). At any given temperature, the air can hold only a finite amount of moisture. The Antoine equation (or steam tables) provides $P_{sat}$ as a function of temperature:

$$\log_{10} P_{sat} = A - \frac{B}{C + T_{db}}$$

where $P_{sat}$ is in mmHg and $T_{db}$ is in °C. For water between 1 °C and 100 °C, $A = 8.10765$, $B = 1750.286$, $C = 235.0$.

### Relative Humidity — Formal Definition

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_{sat}(T_{db})}$$

where $P_w$ is the **partial pressure of water vapor** in the moist air.

### The Humidity Ratio (Absolute Moisture Content)

The humidity ratio $Y$ is the most useful property for drying calculations. It represents the kilograms of water vapor carried per kilogram of **bone-dry** air:

$$Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm} - P_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** The factor **0.622** comes from the ratio of molecular weights $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97$. This appears in EVERY psychrometric equation — never forget it.

Substituting $P_w = \phi \cdot P_{sat}$:

$$Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot P_{sat}}{P_{atm} - \phi \cdot P_{sat}}$$

### Saturated Humidity Ratio

When $\phi = 1$ (100% relative humidity), the air is saturated:

$$Y_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{sat}}{P_{atm} - P_{sat}}$$

## 2.2 Wet-Bulb Temperature and Adiabatic Saturation

The **wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$)** is reached when a wetted thermometer bulb is placed in an air stream. At steady state, the heat transferred from the air to the bulb equals the latent heat carried away by evaporation:

$$h_g (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_y (Y_{swb} - Y) \cdot H_{fg,wb}$$

where:
- $h_g$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $h_y$ = convective mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s)
- $Y_{swb}$ = saturated humidity ratio at $T_{wb}$
- $H_{fg,wb}$ = latent heat of vaporization at $T_{wb}$

This equation is the **governing relation for the psychrometric chart** — every point on the chart satisfies it.

## 2.3 Specific Volume of Moist Air

$$v = \frac{R_{a} \cdot T_{db}}{P_{atm} - P_w}$$

where $R_a = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ is the specific gas constant for dry air. Units of $v$ are m³/kg dry air.

## 2.4 Enthalpy of Moist Air (per kg of dry air)

The enthalpy is the sum of the dry air enthalpy and the water vapor enthalpy, referenced to 0 °C:

$$H = c_{pa} (T_{db} - 0) + Y \cdot [H_{fg,0} + c_{pv} (T_{db} - 0)]$$

With $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $c_{pv} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $H_{fg,0} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$H = 1.005 \, T_{db} + Y \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \, T_{db})$$

## 2.5 Dew Point Temperature

The dew point is the saturation temperature corresponding to the actual partial pressure $P_w$:

$$P_w = P_{sat}(T_{dp}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{dp} = T_{sat}(P_w)$$

## 2.6 Convective Drying — Governing Heat & Mass Transfer

In a convective dryer, hot air flows over a wet food surface. Three resistances govern the rate:

1. **External resistance** — convective heat and mass transfer from air to surface (controlled by air velocity).
2. **Internal resistance** — moisture migration within the food (controlled by food structure, porosity, bound water).
3. **Equilibrium resistance** — bound water at very low moisture levels (controlled by $a_w$ and isotherm).

### Rate of Drying Equation

The instantaneous drying rate $N$ (kg water / m²·hr) is defined as:

$$N = -\frac{m_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

where:
- $m_s$ = mass of bone-dry solid (kg)
- $A$ = drying surface area (m²)
- $X$ = moisture content on a dry basis (kg water / kg dry solid)
- $t$ = time (hr)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marker:** The minus sign is present because $X$ *decreases* with time, but the drying rate $N$ is a *positive* quantity.

## 2.7 Moisture Content Definitions — A Frequent Confusing Topic

**Wet basis ($M_{wb}$):**
$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{total mass}}$$

**Dry basis ($M_{db}$ or $X$):**
$$X = M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{mass of dry solid}}$$

**Conversion:**
$$X = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} \quad \text{and} \quad M_{wb} = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** All drying-rate equations (constant rate, falling rate, total time) use the **DRY-BASIS** moisture content. The wet basis is used only for product specifications (e.g., "dried to 5% moisture wet basis"). Many students lose marks by mixing the two.

## 2.8 Drying Curve Analysis

A drying curve plots **moisture content $X$ vs. time $t$** and its derivative plot (drying rate $N$ vs. $X$) is the **drying rate curve**. The complete curve has these regions:

| Region | Description | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **A–B** | Initial adjustment (transient) | Surface warming up; brief unsteady state |
| **B–C** | Constant Rate Period (CRP) | Free water at surface; rate controlled by external convection |
| **C** | Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$) | Surface becomes dry; internal diffusion takes over |
| **C–D** | First Falling Rate Period (FFRP) | Partially saturated surface; wet islands shrink |
| **D–E** | Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP) | Internal moisture movement is fully rate-controlling |

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this entire table for the 14-mark problems.**

| Formula | Expression | Use / When to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| Humidity Ratio | $Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm} - P_w}$ | Given partial pressure of vapor |
| Saturation Ratio | $Y_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{sat}}{P_{atm} - P_{sat}}$ | At 100% RH at given $T_{db}$ |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_{sat}(T_{db})}$ | Conversions between $P_w$ and $\phi$ |
| Specific Volume | $v = \frac{R_a T_{db}}{P_{atm} - P_w}$ | Air flow rate calculations in m³/s |
| Enthalpy | $H = 1.005 T_{db} + Y (2501 + 1.88 T_{db})$ | Heat balance across dryer |
| Wet-bulb Equation | $h_g (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_y (Y_{swb} - Y) H_{fg,wb}$ | Relating $T_{db}$, $T_{wb}$, $Y$ |
| Drying Rate | $N = -\frac{m_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt}$ | Definition — derived from mass balance |
| Constant Rate Time | $t_c = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$ | When $X_1 > X_c$ (free water present) |
| Falling Rate Time | $t_f = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{f(X)}$ | General integral form for falling rate |
| Linear FFRP | $t_{f1} = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln \left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | When falling rate is exponential |
| Total Drying Time | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | Sum of constant + falling rate periods |
| Equilibrium Moisture | $X^*$ | Bound water — not removable by air drying |
| Conductive Drying (Fourier) | $\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{k (T_s - T_c)}{L}$ | Heat flux through drum wall |
| Drum Surface Area | $A = \pi D L$ | Single drum; $D$ = diameter, $L$ = length |
| Spray Droplet Drying | $\tau = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 H_{fg}}{12 k_g \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Approximate drying time for a droplet |
| Log Mean Temp Diff | $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Heat transfer in dryers |
| Mass of Water Removed | $m_w = m_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | Material balance |
| Energy for Vaporization | $Q_{evap} = m_w \cdot H_{fg}$ | Minimum thermal energy needed |
| Air Flow Rate (psychrometric) | $\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{m_w}{Y_{out} - Y_{in}}$ | Required air mass flow rate |
| Air Flow Rate (volumetric) | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot v$ | Convert mass to volume using $v$ |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- In **milk powder production** (spray drying): $N_c$ and the droplet drying time $\tau$ determine the tower height.
- In **fruit drying** (cabinet, tunnel): $t_{total}$ sets the dryer residence time and thus the conveyor length.
- In **grain drying** (solar, belt): $X^*$ determines the lowest achievable moisture without artificial desiccants.
- In **drum drying of potato flakes**: Fourier's law sets the steam pressure and drum rotational speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Humidity Ratio from Dalton's Law

**Starting premise:** Moist air is an ideal gas mixture at total pressure $P_{atm} = P_a + P_w$ (sum of partial pressures).

**Step 1:** By the ideal gas law, the partial volume of water vapor per unit total volume is:
$$y_w = \frac{P_w}{P_{atm}}$$

**Step 2:** The mass of water vapor in a unit total volume of moist air:
$$m_w = \frac{P_w \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T_{db}}$$

**Step 3:** The mass of dry air in the same unit total volume:
$$m_a = \frac{P_a \cdot M_a}{R \cdot T_{db}} = \frac{(P_{atm} - P_w) \cdot M_a}{R \cdot T_{db}}$$

**Step 4:** Form the mass ratio (water vapor to dry air):
$$Y = \frac{m_w}{m_a} = \frac{P_w \cdot M_w}{(P_{atm} - P_w) \cdot M_a}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the molecular weight ratio $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm} - P_w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is one of the most frequently asked 7-mark sub-questions in KTU exams. Always state the ideal gas assumption, Dalton's law, and the molecular weight ratio at the end.

## 3.2 Derivation: Constant Rate Drying Time

**Setup:** During the constant rate period, the surface of the food is saturated with free water, and the drying rate equals the **rate of evaporation from a free water surface** under the same air conditions. Let $N_c$ = constant drying rate (kg/m²·hr).

**Step 1:** The mass of water evaporated in time $t_c$ from area $A$:
$$\Delta m = N_c \cdot A \cdot t_c$$

**Step 2:** The same mass expressed in terms of moisture content change:
$$\Delta m = m_s (X_1 - X_c)$$

where $X_1$ = initial moisture content, $X_c$ = critical moisture content, $m_s$ = bone-dry mass.

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions:
$$N_c \cdot A \cdot t_c = m_s (X_1 - X_c)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t_c$:

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}}$$

**Step 5:** The constant rate $N_c$ is determined experimentally or by:
$$N_c = h_y (Y_{sw} - Y)$$

where $h_y$ is the mass transfer coefficient, $Y_{sw}$ is the humidity ratio at the wet-bulb temperature, and $Y$ is the bulk air humidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A typical problem gives $m_s$, $X_1$, $X_c$, $A$, and air conditions (from which $N_c$ is calculated). You then substitute and compute $t_c$ in hours.

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Drying Time (Constant + Falling Rate)

**General form:** The instantaneous drying rate $N$ during the falling rate period is some function of the current moisture content:
$$N = N_c \cdot f(X)$$

where $f(X)$ decreases from 1 at $X = X_c$ to 0 at $X = X^*$.

**Step 1:** From the definition of drying rate:
$$N = -\frac{m_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N = N_c f(X)$:
$$N_c f(X) = -\frac{m_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:
$$dt = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \frac{dX}{f(X)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $X_1$ to $X_2$ (initial to final moisture):
$$t_{total} = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \left[ \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{f(X)} + (X_1 - X_c) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Recognizing that $\int_{X_c}^{X_1} dX = X_1 - X_c$ (the constant rate integral trivially), the **general form** is:

$$\boxed{t_{total} = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \left[ (X_1 - X_c) + \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{f(X)} \right]}$$

### Case 1: Linear Falling Rate (sometimes used for the entire falling period)

If $N$ vs. $X$ is a straight line from $(X_c, N_c)$ to $(X^*, 0)$:

$$f(X) = \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Substituting and integrating:

$$t_f = \frac{m_s}{A N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{X_c - X^*}{X - X^*} \, dX$$

$$t_f = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A N_c} \left[ \ln(X - X^*) \right]_{X_2}^{X_c}$$

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A N_c} \ln \left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the formula uses **dry basis** moisture and **natural logarithm (ln)**. Some texts use log base 10 — always check the textbook's convention. KTU 2024 uses natural logarithm.

### Case 2: All Drying in Falling Rate (when $X_1 \leq X_c$)

In foods with high initial moisture but high $X_c$ (e.g., some sugar-rich foods), the constant rate period may be negligible:

$$\boxed{t_{total} \approx \frac{m_s (X_1 - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \text{(factor depending on } f(X) \text{)}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Conductive Drying (Fourier's Law on Drum Surface)

**Setup:** A drum dryer has a thin metal wall of thickness $L_w$, thermal conductivity $k_w$, with inner surface at $T_{si}$ (steam side) and outer surface at $T_{so}$ (food side).

**Step 1:** Heat flux through the wall (steady state, 1-D conduction):
$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{k_w (T_{si} - T_{so})}{L_w}$$

**Step 2:** Energy balance at the food surface (no accumulation):
$$\frac{Q}{A} = m_s H_{fg} \frac{dX}{dA_{drum}}$$

**Step 3:** Drum surface area per unit time (one revolution):
$$A_{drum} = \pi D L$$

where $D$ = drum diameter, $L$ = drum length.

**Step 4:** Drum rotation time per revolution:
$$t_{rev} = \frac{60}{N_{rpm}} \text{ (in seconds)}$$

**Step 5:** Production rate:
$$\dot{m}_{product} = \frac{A_{drum} \cdot N_{rpm} \cdot \rho_{film}}{60}$$

where $\rho_{film}$ is the dried film thickness density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Drum dryers are characterized by very **short residence times** (3–30 seconds) and operate at **high temperatures** (120–160 °C steam). This makes them suitable for pastes and purees (tomato, potato, milk) but NOT for products requiring long moisture removal.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Drying Period Comparisons

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | First Falling Rate | Second Falling Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface condition** | Fully wetted | Partially dry | Dry |
| **Rate-controlling step** | External convection | Mixed (convection + diffusion) | Internal diffusion |
| **Heat transfer** | All convective, by air | Mixed (air + conduction) | Predominantly by conduction |
| **Mass transfer** | Liquid diffusion to surface | Liquid + vapor diffusion | Vapor diffusion |
| **Product temperature** | $\approx T_{wb}$ (wet-bulb) | Above $T_{wb}$, below $T_{db}$ | Approaches $T_{db}$ |
| **Rate equation** | $N = N_c =$ constant | $N = aX + b$ (linear) | $N \propto (X - X^*)$ (diffusion) |
| **Air humidity effect** | Strong | Moderate | Weak |
| **Air velocity effect** | Strong | Moderate | Weak |
| **Effect of shrinkage** | Negligible | Significant | Major (case hardening possible) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A food material with initial moisture content $X_1 = 3.0$ kg water/kg dry solid is dried to $X_2 = 0.1$ kg/kg in a tray dryer. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 1.5$ kg/kg, and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.05$ kg/kg. The bone-dry mass is $m_s = 50$ kg, the drying area is $A = 5$ m², and the constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.2$ kg/m²·hr. The falling rate is linear. Calculate the **total drying time**.

**Solution — Step 1:** Compute constant rate time:
$$t_c = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{50 \times (3.0 - 1.5)}{5 \times 1.2} = \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2:** Compute falling rate time using the linear falling rate formula:
$$t_f = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln \left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{50 \times (1.5 - 0.05)}{5 \times 1.2} \ln \left( \frac{1.5 - 0.05}{0.1 - 0.05} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{50 \times 1.45}{6} \ln \left( \frac{1.45}{0.05} \right) = 12.083 \times \ln(29) = 12.083 \times 3.367 = 40.68 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3:** Total drying time:
$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 12.5 + 40.68 = 53.18 \text{ hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** Always show the substitution with units in the **numerator** and **denominator** separately. The final answer must include the unit (hr). Marks are deducted for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Drying Curve — Moisture Content vs. Time and Drying Rate vs. Moisture Content

The following Mermaid diagram shows the relationship between the moisture-vs-time curve and the rate-vs-moisture curve, both of which are exam essentials.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Curve1["Drying Curve: X vs t"]
        direction LR
        axisX["X axis: Moisture Content (kg/kg db)"]
        axisT["t axis: Time (hr)"]
        pA(["A: Initial Point, X=X1"])
        pB(["B: Start of CRP, t=0"])
        pC(["C: End of CRP at Xc"])
        pD(["D: End of FFRP"])
        pE(["E: End of SFRP at X*"])
        pA --> pB
        pB --> pC
        pC --> pD
        pD --> pE
    end

    subgraph Curve2["Drying Rate Curve: N vs X"]
        direction LR
        axisN["N axis: Drying Rate (kg/m2·hr)"]
        axisX2["X axis: Moisture Content"]
        pB2(["B: N rises to Nc"])
        pC2(["C: Plateau of CRP, N=Nc"])
        pD2(["D: FFRP, N falls"])
        pE2(["E: N approaches 0 at X*"])
        pB2 --> pC2
        pC2 --> pD2
        pD2 --> pE2
    end

    Curve1 --- Curve2
```

## 4.2 Psychrometric Chart — Schematic Layout

The psychrometric chart is a graphical representation of the equations derived earlier. KTU exams expect a labeled sketch of the chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Chart["Psychrometric Chart Schematic"]
        direction TB
        yAxis["Y axis: Humidity Ratio Y (kg/kg da) — curving upward to the right"]
        xAxis["X axis: Dry Bulb Temperature Tdb (°C)"]
        curveRH100["100% RH Saturation Curve — left boundary, Tdb = Twb = Tdp here"]
        curveRH50["50% RH Curve — middle, φ = 0.5"]
        curveRH0["0% RH — right boundary, dry air line"]
        linesTdb["Constant Tdb: vertical lines"]
        linesTwb["Constant Twb: slanted lines upward to the right"]
        linesWetVol["Constant Specific Volume v: nearly vertical, slight tilt"]
        linesEnth["Constant Enthalpy H: slanted lines, parallel to Twb lines (adiabatic saturation lines)"]
        yAxis --- xAxis
        curveRH100 --- yAxis
        curveRH50 --- curveRH100
        curveRH0 --- curveRH50
        linesTdb --- xAxis
        linesTwb --- yAxis
        linesWetVol --- yAxis
        linesEnth --- yAxis
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of Psychrometric Chart:** Along any **wet-bulb line (adiabatic saturation line)**, the enthalpy $H$ is constant. This is the principle behind evaporative cooling and is the basis for finding $Y$ from $T_{db}$ and $T_{wb}$.

## 4.3 Cabinet (Tray) Dryer — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CabinetDryer["Cabinet Tray Dryer - Block Architecture"]
        direction TB
        heater["Heater: Steam coils or Electric elements — supplies sensible heat to air"]
        blower["Blower / Fan: Circulates hot air through the cabinet"]
        ductIn["Inlet Duct: Hot dry air enters from top or side"]
        trays["Trays: Perforated metal trays loaded with food in thin layers — 1 to 5 cm bed depth"]
        product["Food Product: Spreads as a static bed; moisture migrates to surface"]
        airFlow["Air Flow Path: Air passes over and through tray perforations"]
        exhaust["Exhaust Duct: Humid air leaves carrying evaporated moisture"]
        control["Temperature and Humidity Controller: Maintains Tdb and RH setpoints"]

        heater --> blower
        blower --> ductIn
        ductIn --> trays
        trays --> product
        product --> airFlow
        airFlow --> exhaust
        control -. regulates .-> heater
        control -. regulates .-> blower
    end
```

## 4.4 Tunnel Dryer — Long Duct with Trucks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TunnelDryer["Tunnel Dryer Architecture"]
        direction LR
        inlet["Air Inlet: Hot air at Tdb1 = 80 to 120 °C, Y1 = 0.010"]
        truck1["Truck 1: Freshly loaded product, X = X1 high — encounters hottest driest air — Constant Rate Period active"]
        truck2["Truck 2: Partially dried product — intermediate state — Falling Rate begins"]
        truck3["Truck 3: Nearly dry product, X close to X2 — meets cooler more humid air — Second Falling Rate"]
        exhaust2["Air Outlet: Cooled and humidified air, Tdb2 = 40 to 60 °C, Y2 = 0.040"]
        rails["Rails: Trucks move through tunnel in countercurrent flow to air"]

        inlet --> truck1
        truck1 --> truck2
        truck2 --> truck3
        truck3 --> exhaust2
        rails --- truck1
        rails --- truck2
        rails --- truck3
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Question Pattern:** "Explain the countercurrent operation of a tunnel dryer and justify why the wettest product meets the driest air." The answer involves: maximum driving force for heat and mass transfer at the wet end; protection from case-hardening at the dry end; reduced energy wasted on already-dry material.

## 4.5 Spray Dryer — Atomization and Droplet Drying

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SprayDryer["Spray Dryer Functional Architecture"]
        direction TB
        feed["Liquid Feed Tank: Concentrated solution, slurry, or emulsion, e.g., milk, coffee, eggs"]
        pump["Feed Pump: High-pressure pump delivers feed to atomizer"]
        atomizer["Atomizer: Centrifugal disc or Pressure Nozzle — creates 20 to 200 micron droplets"]
        airIn["Hot Air Inlet: Tdb = 150 to 220 °C, Y1 low — supplied by indirect heater"]
        chamber["Drying Chamber: Large vertical cylinder 3 to 10 m diameter, 10 to 30 m tall — droplets dry in 2 to 5 seconds"]
        droplets["Droplet Stages: 1 Initial heating to wet-bulb; 2 Constant rate evaporation at Twb; 3 Falling rate as crust forms; 4 Particles cool to Tamb"]
        cyclone["Cyclone Separator: Recovers fines from exhaust air"]
        exhaust3["Exhaust Air: Cooled to 80 to 100 °C, high Y — discharged"]
        productOut["Powder Collection: From cone bottom and cyclone"]

        feed --> pump
        pump --> atomizer
        airIn --> chamber
        atomizer --> chamber
        chamber --> droplets
        droplets --> cyclone
        cyclone --> exhaust3
        chamber --> productOut
    end
```

## 4.6 Fluidized Bed Dryer — Particulate Suspension

```mermaid
        plenum["Air Plenum: Distributes hot air uniformly through perforated plate"]
        hotAir2["Hot Air: 60 to 150 °C from below — fluidizes the bed at velocity umf to ut"]
        bed["Fluidized Bed: Particles suspended and vigorously mixed — Tdb around 60 to 90 °C; excellent heat and mass transfer"]
        expanded["Expanded Bed Height: 1.5 to 2 times static bed height; intense turbulence"]
        discharge2["Discharge: Dried particles overflow weir or underflow at outlet"]
        exhaust4["Exhaust Air: Top of bed, carries fines — passes through bag filter or cyclone"]
        filter["Bag Filter: Recovers entrained fines, returns to product stream"]

        feed2 --> bed
        hotAir2 --> plenum
        plenum --> bed
        bed --> expanded
        expanded --> discharge2
        expanded --> exhaust4
        exhaust4 --> filter
        filter -. returns fines .-> bed
    end
```

```mermaid

        direction LR
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the terms wet-bulb temperature and dew point temperature as applied to moist air. How are they related to the humidity ratio?

**Model Answer:**

**Wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$):** It is the dynamic equilibrium temperature a parcel of air attains when it flows past a wetted thermometer bulb under adiabatic conditions. At steady state, the heat transferred from the air to the bulb (sensible heat) equals the latent heat carried away by the evaporated water.

**Dew point temperature ($T_{dp}$):** It is the temperature at which the water vapor in the air becomes saturated (begins to condense) when the air is cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. Mathematically, $T_{dp}$ is the saturation temperature corresponding to the actual partial pressure of water vapor: $P_{sat}(T_{dp}) = P_w$.

**Relation to humidity ratio ($Y$):**
- $Y$ is a direct function of $P_w$. At dew point, $P_w = P_{sat}(T_{dp})$, so $Y$ is determined entirely by $T_{dp}$.
- $T_{wb}$ is found on the psychrometric chart as the intersection of the constant wet-bulb line (adiabatic saturation line) passing through the state point, and the saturation curve. It is **lower** than $T_{db}$ and **higher** than $T_{dp}$ for unsaturated air.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List any six different types of industrial dryers used in the food processing industry, classifying each as either batch or continuous.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Dryer Type | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cabinet (Tray) Dryer | **Batch** |
| 2 | Tunnel Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 3 | Belt Dryer (single or multi-tier) | **Continuous** |
| 4 | Belt-Trough Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 5 | Rotary Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 6 | Spray Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 7 | Fluidized Bed Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 8 | Pneumatic (Flash) Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 9 | Drum Dryer | **Continuous** |
| 10 | Solar Cabinet Dryer | **Batch** |
| 11 | Sun Drying (open yard) | **Batch** |

Any six with correct classification fetch full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below are TWO complete alternative questions with sub-parts and full solutions.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] Derive the expression for the humidity ratio of moist air. A food dryer receives air at 30 °C dry-bulb and 22 °C wet-bulb temperature. The atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. The saturation pressure of water at 30 °C is 4.246 kPa and at 22 °C is 2.645 kPa. Determine the humidity ratio and relative humidity of the inlet air.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**
1. Moist air follows Dalton's law: $P_{atm} = P_a + P_w$
2. From ideal gas law, mass of vapor = $\dfrac{P_w M_w}{R T_{db}}$, mass of dry air = $\dfrac{(P_{atm}-P_w) M_a}{R T_{db}}$
3. $Y = m_w / m_a = \dfrac{P_w M_w}{(P_{atm}-P_w) M_a} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_w}{P_{atm}-P_w}$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** First find the humidity ratio at the wet-bulb (saturated at 22 °C):
$$Y_{sw} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{sat}(22°C)}{P_{atm} - P_{sat}(22°C)} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.645}{101.325 - 2.645}$$

$$Y_{sw} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.645}{98.680} = 0.01667 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 2:** Use the wet-bulb relation assuming $h_y \approx h_g / H_{fg,wb}$ with $H_{fg,wb} \approx 2450$ kJ/kg at 22 °C, $c_s \approx 1.005 + 1.88 Y \approx 1.05$ kJ/kg·K:

$$Y = Y_{sw} - \frac{c_s (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{H_{fg,wb}} = 0.01667 - \frac{1.05 \times (30 - 22)}{2450}$$

$$Y = 0.01667 - \frac{8.4}{2450} = 0.01667 - 0.00343 = 0.01324 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3:** Find partial pressure of vapor:
$$P_w = \frac{Y \cdot P_{atm}}{0.622 + Y} = \frac{0.01324 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.01324} = \frac{1.3415}{0.6352} = 2.112 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Find relative humidity:
$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_{sat}(30°C)} = \frac{2.112}{4.246} = 0.4974 = 49.74\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- Humidity Ratio: $Y = 0.0132$ kg water/kg dry air
- Relative Humidity: $\phi \approx 49.7\%$

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle, construction, and working of a Spray Dryer with a neat labeled diagram. List four advantages and three disadvantages.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A spray dryer atomizes a liquid feed (solution, slurry, or emulsion) into a hot air stream, creating fine droplets (20–200 μm diameter). The enormous surface area per unit volume of the droplets results in extremely rapid moisture removal (2–5 seconds). The droplets first reach the wet-bulb temperature (constant rate period) and then undergo falling rate drying as a dried crust forms. The dried particles are separated from the moist exhaust air by a cyclone separator.

**Construction (2 marks):**
1. **Feed tank** with stirrer
2. **High-pressure pump** or feed pump
3. **Atomizer** — centrifugal disc atomizer OR pressure nozzle (fountain nozzle)
4. **Drying chamber** — large vertical stainless steel cylinder (3–10 m diameter, 10–30 m height) with conical bottom
5. **Air heater** — indirect-fired (steam coil or gas burner) to avoid contamination
6. **Hot air distributor** at the top (co-current) or side
7. **Cyclone separator** for fines recovery
8. **Exhaust fan and stack**
9. **Product collection** at chamber cone and cyclone bottom

**Working (2 marks):**
- Liquid feed (e.g., milk, coffee extract, egg) is pumped at high pressure (for nozzle) or fed to a spinning disc (for centrifugal atomizer) rotating at 5000–25000 rpm.
- The feed is atomized into a fine spray entering the top of the chamber.
- Hot filtered air (150–220 °C) enters at the top (co-current) and contacts the spray.
- Each droplet undergoes: (i) heating to wet-bulb temperature, (ii) constant rate evaporation, (iii) falling rate, (iv) particle cooling.
- Dried powder falls to the conical bottom; fines are recovered by cyclone; exhaust air leaves at 80–100 °C with high humidity.

**Advantages (0.5 marks each):**
1. Very short drying time (2–5 s) preserves heat-sensitive nutrients, color, and flavor.
2. Continuous operation with high production rates.
3. Produces free-flowing, uniform, spherical powder directly from liquid feed.
4. Single-step process — no need for intermediate grinding.

**Disadvantages (0.5 marks each):**
1. High capital cost and high energy consumption.
2. Product can deposit on chamber walls, requiring periodic cleaning.
3. Not suitable for very viscous or sticky feeds (e.g., high-fat foods may require wall-scraper design).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023] With the help of a drying rate curve, explain the constant rate and falling rate periods of drying. A wet solid is dried from initial moisture content 80% (wet basis) to 5% (wet basis) on a tray dryer. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents (dry basis) are 1.5 and 0.02 kg/kg respectively. The bone-dry mass is 30 kg, the tray area is 3 m², and the constant drying rate is 1.5 kg/m²·hr. The falling rate is linear. Calculate the total drying time.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Drying Rate Curve (3 marks):**

The drying rate curve plots drying rate $N$ (kg water/m²·hr) on the y-axis against moisture content $X$ (kg/kg db) on the x-axis. It has the following periods:

1. **A-B (Initial adjustment):** The product warms from initial temperature to wet-bulb temperature; rate rises sharply. Brief, often ignored.

2. **B-C (Constant Rate Period, CRP):** The surface is fully saturated with free water, and the rate of evaporation equals the rate of evaporation from a free water surface. The rate is determined by external conditions (air velocity, $T_{db}$, $Y$) and is **constant** regardless of $X$. The product temperature equals $T_{wb}$.

3. **C (Critical Point):** The surface first becomes dry. The corresponding moisture content $X_c$ is the **critical moisture content**.

4. **C-D (First Falling Rate Period):** The wetted area on the surface shrinks; rate decreases linearly (assumed) with $X$. Both external and internal resistances matter.

5. **D-E (Second Falling Rate Period):** The surface is fully dry; rate is controlled by internal moisture diffusion. The product temperature rises from $T_{wb}$ toward $T_{db}$. The rate approaches zero at the **equilibrium moisture content $X^*$**, beyond which no drying is possible at the given air conditions.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert wet-basis moistures to dry-basis:
$$X_1 = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0 \text{ kg/kg db}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg db}$$

**Step 2:** Check that $X_1 > X_c$ (CRP exists):
$$X_1 = 4.0 > X_c = 1.5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Compute constant rate time:
$$t_c = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{30 \times (4.0 - 1.5)}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{30 \times 2.5}{4.5} = \frac{75}{4.5} = 16.67 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 4:** Compute falling rate time (linear falling rate):
$$t_f = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln \left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{30 \times (1.5 - 0.02)}{3 \times 1.5} \ln \left( \frac{1.5 - 0.02}{0.0526 - 0.02} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{30 \times 1.48}{4.5} \ln \left( \frac{1.48}{0.0326} \right) = 9.867 \times \ln(45.40)$$

$$t_f = 9.867 \times 3.815 = 37.64 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 5:** Total drying time:
$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 16.67 + 37.64 = 54.31 \text{ hr}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{total} \approx 54.3$ hours.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle and working of a Fluidized Bed Dryer. State its applications, advantages, and disadvantages in food processing.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
When hot air is passed upward through a bed of wet granular food particles at a velocity equal to or greater than the **minimum fluidization velocity ($u_{mf}$)** but less than the **terminal velocity ($u_t$)** of the particles, the bed behaves like a boiling liquid. The particles become suspended and vigorously mixed, exposing enormous surface area to the hot air. This results in extremely high rates of heat and mass transfer — typically 5 to 10 times faster than static tray drying.

**Construction (2 marks):**
1. **Air plenum chamber** below the distributor plate
2. **Perforated distributor plate** (or bubble cap plate) to ensure uniform air distribution
3. **Drying chamber** with vertical walls, expanded section to allow for bed expansion
4. **Feed inlet** at top or side
5. **Discharge weir or rotary valve** at the side
6. **Blower and air heater**
7. **Cyclone or bag filter** for fines recovery
8. **Control panel** for $T_{db}$, bed temperature, humidity

**Working (1.5 marks):**
- Wet particles (1–5 mm size, e.g., diced vegetables, sugar crystals, grains) are fed into the bed.
- Hot air (60–150 °C) is blown upward through the distributor plate.
- At $u_{mf}$, the bed fluidizes; particles move in a turbulent, mixing pattern.
- Moisture evaporates from particle surfaces, carried away by the air.
- Bed temperature remains at $T_{wb}$ during constant rate period (~50–60 °C).
- Dried particles discharge over the weir or through the rotary valve.
- Typical residence time: 5 to 30 minutes.

**Applications (0.5 marks each, list 4):**
1. Drying of granulated sugar, salt, and spices
2. Drying of diced vegetables (peas, carrots, beans)
3. Drying of cereals and grains
4. Drying of pharmaceutical granules and seeds
5. Pre-drying of pasta, noodles
6. Cooling of roasted coffee and nuts (using cold air)

**Advantages (0.4 marks each, list 3):**
1. Very high heat and mass transfer coefficients — short drying time.
2. Uniform product quality due to excellent mixing.
3. Continuous operation with easy control of residence time.
4. Compact equipment — small floor area for high throughput.

**Disadvantages (0.4 marks each, list 2):**
1. Limited to free-flowing granular materials; not suitable for pastes or large pieces.
2. High air velocity causes attrition and dust generation — needs dust collection.
3. High power consumption for blower and heater.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Dehydration Questions:**
>
> 1. **Moisture Basis Confusion (4–5 marks lost per paper):** Always convert wet basis percentages to dry basis BEFORE substituting into drying-time equations. Writing $X_1 = 80\%$ is a guaranteed error — the formula needs $X_1 = 4.0$ kg/kg db.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Equilibrium Moisture Content $X^*$ (2–3 marks):** The falling rate equation has $\ln((X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*))$, NOT $\ln(X_c / X_2)$. Omitting $X^*$ gives a numerically wrong (smaller) drying time.
>
> 3. **Molar Mass Constant (0.622) Mistake:** The 0.622 factor in the humidity ratio is non-negotiable. Showing it as 0.66 or 0.6 is a calculation error.
>
> 4. **Differentiating Constant Rate vs Falling Rate (3 marks):** Some students assume all drying is constant rate. The problem must explicitly check $X_1 > X_c$ before using $t_c$ formula.
>
> 5. **Confusing Drum Dryer with Spray Dryer (2 marks):** Drum dryer uses **conductive** heat through a metal wall; spray dryer uses **convective** heat from hot air. Don't interchange.
>
> 6. **Missing Diagram Labels:** In the 14-mark dryer questions, the labeled diagram fetches 2 marks by itself. Use the Mermaid architecture / neat block diagram with all parts labeled.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 30 Minutes Before Exam):**

- **Moist Air Thermodynamics:**
  - The 0.622 factor = $M_w / M_a$ — appears in humidity ratio formulas.
  - Saturation curve on psychrometric chart is the locus of $T_{db} = T_{wb} = T_{dp}$.
  - Along constant wet-bulb lines, the **enthalpy is constant** (adiabatic saturation).
  - Specific enthalpy: $H = 1.005 T_{db} + Y(2501 + 1.88 T_{db})$ in kJ/kg da.

- **Drying Rate Concepts:**
  - $N$ vs $X$ curve has two distinct regions: constant rate (B-C) and falling rate (C-D and D-E).
  - Constant rate is controlled by **external** air conditions; falling rate by **internal** moisture movement.
  - Product temperature during CRP $\approx T_{wb}$; during SFRP it approaches $T_{db}$.
  - Critical moisture content $X_c$ depends on product thickness — thicker bed gives higher $X_c$.

- **Total Drying Time Formulas (Know Both Forms):**
  - $t_c = m_s(X_1 - X_c)/(A \cdot N_c)$
  - $t_f = m_s(X_c - X^*)/(A \cdot N_c) \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$ (linear)
  - $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ — use **dry basis** for ALL $X$ values.

- **Conductive vs Convective Drying:**
  - **Convective:** Heat via hot air (most food dryers).
  - **Conductive:** Heat via hot metal surface (drum dryers).
  - Radiative: Heat via infrared (specialty).

- **Dryer Classification (Memorize This Table):**

| Dryer | Mode | Operation | Typical Food Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cabinet (Tray) | Convective | Batch | Fruits, herbs, vegetables, small lots |
| Tunnel | Convective | Continuous | Fruits, pasta, crackers |
| Belt | Convective | Continuous | Fruits, vegetables, cereals |
| Belt-Trough | Convective | Continuous | Sticky foods, pastes |
| Rotary | Convective | Continuous | Grains, sugar, salt, powders |
| Spray | Convective | Continuous | Milk, coffee, eggs, flavors |
| Fluidized Bed | Convective | Continuous | Granules, diced vegetables |
| Pneumatic | Convective | Continuous | Powders, starch, filter cakes |
| Drum | Conductive | Continuous | Potato flakes, milk, tomato paste |
| Sun | Convective (natural) | Batch | Grapes, figs, fish, grains |
| Solar | Convective (assisted) | Batch/Semi-continuous | Fruits, herbs, fish |

- **Issues in Food Drying Technology (Key Points for Short Notes):**
  - **Case hardening:** Surface dries too fast, forming a hard crust that traps moisture inside. Reduce air temperature or increase humidity early.
  - **Shrinkage and collapse:** Loss of cellular structure; reduces rehydration capacity.
  - **Nutrient degradation:** Heat-sensitive vitamins (Vitamin C, thiamine) and pigments (chlorophyll) degrade at high temperatures.
  - **Maillard browning:** Reducing sugars + amino acids at high $a_w$ and temperature; controlled by lowering $T$ or using sulfur dioxide pretreatment.
  - **Stickiness in spray drying:** High sugar/low molecular weight foods (e.g., honey) become sticky; use carrier agents (maltodextrin).
  - **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$):** Foods should be dried below $T_g$ to maintain crispness; use low $a_w$ storage.
  - **Energy consumption:** Drying accounts for 10–25% of total food industry energy use — opportunity for heat recovery and solar drying.
  - **Microbial safety:** Sun drying risks dust, insect, and rodent contamination; solar dryers offer better protection.
  - **Color and flavor loss:** Volatile compounds evaporate with water; encapsulation or low-temperature drying is preferred.
  - **Uneven drying:** Air channeling and bed depth variation — solved by tray rotation or fluidization.

- **Numericals — Always Carry Out:**
  1. Wet basis to dry basis conversion.
  2. Identify $X_1$, $X_c$, $X^*$, $X_2$.
  3. Use $N_c$ directly or compute from mass transfer coefficient.
  4. Apply $t_c$ if $X_1 > X_c$; else straight falling rate.
  5. Add $t_c$ and $t_f$ to get $t_{total}$.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Extraction — The Master Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extraction** is a mass-transfer operation in which a soluble constituent (the *solute* or *solute A*) is separated from a mixture by using a liquid or supercritical fluid (the *solvent*) that preferentially dissolves it. When applied to food materials, it is one of the most economically important unit operations, underpinning the production of vegetable oils, sugar, coffee, tea, pectin, and natural colourants.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, extraction is classified by the **physical state of the feed** and the **physical state of the solvent**:
- **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching)** — solid feed + liquid solvent
- **Liquid–Liquid Extraction (LLE)** — liquid feed + immiscible liquid solvent
- **Super Critical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)** — solid (or liquid) feed + supercritical fluid solvent

### 1.2 Solid–Liquid Extraction — Leaching

> [!NOTE]
> **Leaching** is defined as the preferential separation of one or more soluble constituents (solute) from a solid matrix by dissolution into a liquid solvent. The solid residue left behind is called the **extracted solids** or **marc**, and the solvent leaving the stage is called the **extract** or **overflow**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Tea Making 🍵):**
Imagine placing a tea bag (soluble tea solids locked inside dried leaves) into a cup of hot water. The hot water (solvent) penetrates the leaf, dissolves the flavour compounds, colour, and caffeine (solute), and diffuses them outward, producing a coloured liquor (extract). The spent tea leaves (marc/underflow) are discarded. *Leaching is exactly this process, only on an industrial scale, optimised for time, temperature, particle size, and solvent-to-solid ratio.*

**Industrial Examples of Leaching in Food Processing:**
- **Sugar** from sugar beet or sugar cane (water is the solvent).
- **Soybean oil** (hexane is the solvent, though increasingly replaced by SCFE).
- **Coffee** from roasted and ground beans (water).
- **Pectin** from citrus peels (acidified water).
- **Tea solubles** for instant tea (water).
- **Vanilla** and **colourants** from plant tissues (ethanol/water).

### 1.3 Liquid–Liquid Extraction (LLE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** is the separation of one or more solutes from a liquid mixture by contacting it with a second liquid (the *extractant*) that is **immiscible** (or only partially miscible) with the first. The solute distributes itself between the two phases according to its **partition coefficient** *K*.

**Conceptual Analogy (Oil & Vinegar Salad Dressing 🫒):**
When you shake olive oil and vinegar, they form a temporary emulsion. Vinegar (aqueous phase) carries water-soluble flavour acids; oil carries fat-soluble compounds. On standing, the two layers separate, each now enriched with the solute it prefers. Industrial LLE works on the same principle, in continuous contactors (mixer-settlers, centrifugal extractors, columns).

### 1.4 Super Critical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **super critical fluid (SCF)** is a substance held above its **critical temperature (T_c)** and **critical pressure (P_c)**. In this region, the fluid has gas-like diffusivity and viscosity but liquid-like density and solvating power — a unique combination that makes it a *tunable solvent*.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Sponge" 🧽):**
Imagine a sponge whose absorbency you can switch on and off by simply tweaking temperature or pressure. A supercritical fluid behaves the same way: by reducing pressure at the end of the process, the solvent "lets go" of the dissolved solute and reverts to a gas, leaving a pure extract **and** an easily recoverable solvent — no residue, no distillation energy penalty.

**The Industry Standard Solvent — Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):**
- **Critical Temperature (T_c) = 31.1 °C**
- **Critical Pressure (P_c) = 73.8 bar (≈ 7.38 MPa)**
- Non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS (Generally Recognised As Safe) for food use.
- Leaves **zero solvent residue** in the product — the holy grail for natural flavours, decaffeinated coffee, and nutraceuticals.

### 1.5 Quick-Reference Comparative Matrix

| Operation | Feed Phase | Solvent | Solute Leaves In | Driving Force | Mark Drivers in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Leaching** | Solid | Liquid (water, hexane, ethanol) | Overflow (extract) | Concentration gradient | 3 / 14 marks |
| **LLE** | Liquid | Immiscible liquid | Extract phase | Partition coefficient *K* | 3 / 14 marks |
| **SCFE** | Solid / Liquid | Supercritical fluid (CO₂) | Extract after depressurisation | Solubility in SCF | 3 / 14 marks |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners love the phrase: *"Leaching is a diffusion-controlled, solid–liquid mass transfer operation"* — memorise it verbatim for a guaranteed 1 mark in Part A.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Leaching — The Physical Picture

Leaching proceeds in **three sequential steps**, each with its own kinetics:

1. **Penetration of the solvent** into the porous solid matrix (wetting).
2. **Dissolution of the solute** into the solvent.
3. **Diffusion of the solute** from the interior of the particle to the bulk solvent.

> [!NOTE]
> The rate-limiting step is almost always **internal diffusion** (step 3). Hence, KTU questions frequently test the influence of **particle size, temperature, agitation, and solvent viscosity** on leaching rate.

### 2.2 Essential Nomenclature for Leaching Calculations

To master the KTU 14-mark numerical problems, you must internalise the following symbols. Using the wrong basis (e.g., total mass instead of insoluble-solid mass) is the single biggest error.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| *L₀* | Mass flow of solvent-free (insoluble) solid in feed | kg inert/h |
| *V_{N+1}* | Mass flow of fresh solvent entering the last stage | kg/h |
| *x₀* | Mass of solute per kg insoluble solid in feed (underflow basis) | kg A/kg inert |
| *x_N* | Mass of solute per kg insoluble solid in final underflow | kg A/kg inert |
| *y_{N+1}* | Solute concentration in fresh solvent (usually 0) | kg A/kg solvent |
| *y_N* | Solute concentration in final overflow (extract) | kg A/kg solvent |
| *N* | Mass of solvent retained per kg insoluble solid in underflow | kg solvent/kg inert |
| *N′* | Stages required | dimensionless |
| *K* | Partition / distribution coefficient | kg A/kg solvent per kg A/kg inert |

> [!WARNING]
> The *x* axis in leaching is always **per kg insoluble solid** (NOT per kg of underflow). The *y* axis is **per kg of pure solvent** (NOT per kg of overflow). Mixing up the bases is the most common mark-loser.

### 2.3 Material Balance — Single Stage

Let a feed of *L₀* kg insoluble solid containing *L₀·x₀* kg solute be contacted with *V_{N+1}* kg of solvent. The exit streams are:

- **Overflow (extract):** *V_N* kg of solution containing *V_N·y_N* kg solute.
- **Underflow (marc):** *L_N* kg containing *L_N·x_N* kg solute and *L_N·N* kg solvent.

**Total Mass Balance:**

$$L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_N$$

**Insoluble (Inert) Solid Balance:**

$$L_0 = L_N \quad \text{(insoluble solid passes through unchanged)}$$

**Solute Balance:**

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1} = L_N \cdot x_N + V_N \cdot y_N$$

### 2.4 Equilibrium in Leaching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At equilibrium, the concentration of the solute in the solution wetting the solid (*x*/N on a solvent basis) equals the concentration in the bulk overflow (*y*). Hence, the **equilibrium line** is:

$$y^* = \frac{x}{N}$$

Where *N* is the mass of solvent retained per kg of inert solid in the underflow. *N* is approximately **constant** for a given solid when solvent viscosity and surface tension are unchanged — this is the *constant-underflow* assumption used in most KTU problems.

### 2.5 Operating Line for Countercurrent Multistage Leaching

For an *N′-stage* countercurrent cascade (numbered 1 → N from feed to solvent inlet), the operating line passing through the terminal points (*x₀, y₁*) and (*x_N, y_{N+1}*) is:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}} \cdot x_n + \left( y_{N+1} - \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}} \cdot x_N \right)$$

This is a **straight line** on an *x–y* diagram, of slope *L₀/V_{N+1}*, between the equilibrium line and the diagonal. Construction by stepping off stages graphically (McCabe–Thiele style) is the KTU-expected graphical method.

### 2.6 Stage Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage efficiency (η)** is the ratio of the change in composition achieved in an actual stage to the change that would occur if the stage operated at thermodynamic equilibrium.

$$\eta = \frac{x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{actual}}}{x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{equilibrium}}}$$

In leaching, *Murphree efficiency* is used, since the underflow and overflow do not usually reach equilibrium.

### 2.7 Kremser-Type Equation for Number of Stages

When the equilibrium line and operating line are both straight (constant underflow, linear equilibrium), the number of ideal stages *N′* is:

$$N' = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln K}$$

where *K = L₀/(N · V_{N+1})* is the effective equilibrium slope ratio. *This equation is a 7-mark guaranteed question in every KTU cycle — memorise it.*

### 2.8 Super Critical Fluid Extraction (SCFE) — Principles

A substance becomes a **supercritical fluid** when both temperature AND pressure exceed their critical values. The SCF region on a *P–T* diagram lies beyond the **critical point (CP)** — it is *neither* a true gas *nor* a true liquid.

**Tunable Solvating Power:**
- **Pressure ↑** at constant *T* → density ↑ → solvating power ↑ (more solute dissolves).
- **Temperature ↑** at constant *P* → density ↓ → solvating power ↓ BUT vapour pressure of solute ↑.
- Net effect: a 2-D "tuning window" that lets the engineer pick the optimum (*P*, *T*) for each solute.

### 2.9 Why CO₂ Dominates as a SCFE Solvent in Food

| Property | Value | Food-Processing Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **T_c** | **31.1 °C** | Operate near room temperature → thermo-labile bioactives preserved |
| **P_c** | **73.8 bar** | Achievable with commercial pumps |
| Toxicity | None (GRAS) | Safe in food |
| Flammability | None | No explosion hazard |
| Cost | Cheap, abundant | By-product of fermentation/brewing |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Depressurise → CO₂ gas → recompress |
| Residue | Zero | "Natural" / "organic" label friendly |

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_N$ | Total mass balance | Every leaching problem |
| 2 | $L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1} = L_N \cdot x_N + V_N \cdot y_N$ | Solute balance | Single + multi stage |
| 3 | $y^* = x/N$ | Equilibrium line | Constant underflow |
| 4 | $\text{slope} = L_0 / V_{N+1}$ | Operating-line slope | Countercurrent |
| 5 | $N' = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln K}$ | Kremser equation for stages | Linear eqm + linear op |
| 6 | $\eta = (x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{act}})/(x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{eq}})$ | Murphree stage efficiency | Real vs ideal stage |
| 7 | $\rho_{SCF} = f(T, P)$ | SCF density = solvating power | SCFE design |
| 8 | $K_{D} = C_{A,\text{extract}} / C_{A,\text{raffinate}}$ | Distribution coefficient | LLE design |
| 9 | $y_{n+1} = (L_0/V_{N+1}) \cdot x_n + \text{const}$ | Operating-line equation | McCabe–Thiele plot |
| 10 | $\text{Recovery} = (V_N \cdot y_N)/(L_0 \cdot x_0)$ | Fractional solute recovery | Performance check |

> [!TIP]
> For LLE, the **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ is the equivalent of *N* in leaching — it dictates the equilibrium tie-lines on a triangular phase diagram. Always read off the *tie-line* slope, never the binodal curve.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Stage Leaching Material Balance

**Given:** A feed of $L_0$ kg of insoluble (inert) solid carrying $L_0 \cdot x_0$ kg of solute, contacted with $V_{N+1}$ kg of pure solvent. The underflow leaves with *N* kg solvent per kg inert.

**Step 1 — Express the underflow mass.** The underflow contains inert solid + solute + retained solvent:

$$L_N = L_0 \cdot (1 + x_N + N)$$

**Step 2 — Express the overflow mass.** Solvent in = solvent retained by solid + solvent in overflow:

$$V_N = V_{N+1} - L_0 \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — Solute balance across the stage.** Solute in (with feed) = solute out (in overflow + in underflow):

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1} = V_N \cdot y_N + L_N \cdot x_N$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3** to express the exit extract concentration:

$$V_N \cdot y_N = L_0 \cdot x_0 - L_N \cdot x_N + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for the exit concentration *y_N***:

$$y_N = \frac{L_0 \cdot (x_0 - x_N) + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1}}{V_{N+1} - L_0 \cdot N}$$

This single equation solves the single-stage problem completely. ✔ **The 7-mark standard form.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Operating Line for Countercurrent Leaching

Consider an *N′-stage* countercurrent cascade. Stages are numbered 1 to *N′*. The feed (with $x_0$) enters stage 1, the fresh solvent ($y_{N+1}$) enters stage *N′*, and the underflows and overflows flow countercurrently.

**Step 1 — Solute balance around stages 1 through *n* (a "shell" balance):**

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{n+1} \cdot y_{n+1} = L_n \cdot x_n + V_N \cdot y_N$$

**Step 2 — Inert-solid balance** (insoluble solid is conserved through every stage):

$$L_0 = L_n \quad \text{for all } n$$

**Step 3 — Total solvent balance** across the same shell:

$$V_{n+1} = V_N + L_n \cdot N - L_0 \cdot N$$

For **constant underflow** ($L_n = L_0$ for all *n*), this simplifies to a *constant* solvent flow between stages:

$$V_{n+1} = V_N + \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the constant-underflow result into the solute balance:**

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{n+1} \cdot y_{n+1} = L_0 \cdot x_n + V_N \cdot y_N$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $y_{n+1}$**:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L_0}{V_{n+1}} \cdot (x_n - x_0) + y_N$$

Rearranging to the standard KTU form (passing through the terminal points $(x_0, y_1)$ and $(x_N, y_{N+1})$):

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}} \cdot x_n + \left( y_{N+1} - \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}} \cdot x_N \right)$$

**This is the operating line used in the McCabe–Thiele construction of countercurrent leaching stages.** ✔ **7-mark question, must appear in the answer sheet.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Number of Ideal Stages

**Step 1 — Define the slope of the equilibrium line.** From Section 2.4:

$$y = \frac{x}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{slope}_{eq} = \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Define the slope of the operating line.**

$$\text{slope}_{op} = \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the effective equilibrium ratio *K*.**

$$K = \frac{\text{slope}_{op}}{\text{slope}_{eq}} = \frac{L_0 / V_{N+1}}{1/N} = \frac{L_0 \cdot N}{V_{N+1}}$$

**Step 4 — Write the difference equation for one ideal stage.** Since the operating line and equilibrium line are both straight, the composition change per stage is geometric. Between stages *n* and *n + 1*:

$$y_{n+1} - y_n = \frac{L_0}{V_{N+1}} \cdot (x_n - x_{n+1})$$

Using equilibrium $y_n = x_n / N$ → $x_n = N \cdot y_n$:

$$y_{n+1} - y_n = \frac{L_0 \cdot N}{V_{N+1}} \cdot (y_n - y_{n+1})$$

**Step 5 — Collect terms.**

$$y_{n+1}(1 + K) = y_n(1 + K) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{y_{n+1} - y^*}{y_n - y^*} = K$$

where $y^* = y_{N+1}/K$ is the "pinch" composition.

**Step 6 — Geometric series over *N′* stages:**

$$\frac{y_1 - y^*}{y_{N+1} - y^*} = K^{N'}$$

**Step 7 — Convert *y* to *x* using equilibrium** ($x = N \cdot y$):

$$\boxed{N' = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K}\right]}{\ln K}}$$

✔ **Master equation — KTU examiners will present a numerical problem requiring this directly.**

## 3.4 Worked Example — Single Stage Leaching (14-mark pattern)

> **Problem:** $1000 \text{ kg}$ of flaked soybean containing **20 % oil** is contacted with $500 \text{ kg}$ of fresh hexane. The underflow holds **0.5 kg hexane per kg insoluble solid**, and the equilibrium is $y^* = x/N$. Find the composition of the extract and the % oil recovery.

**Step 1 — Calculate the inert solid feed basis.** Oil is the solute; insoluble matter (defatted solids) is the inert.

$$L_0 = 1000 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 800 \text{ kg inert solid}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{\text{oil}}{\text{inert}} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.25 \text{ kg oil/kg inert}$$

**Step 2 — Compute underflow mass leaving the stage.**

$$L_N = L_0 \cdot (1 + x_N + N) \quad \text{with } N = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Apply solute balance.** Fresh hexane is pure ($y_{N+1} = 0$):

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 = L_N \cdot x_N + V_N \cdot y_N$$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium $y_N = x_N / N$:**

$$800 \cdot 0.25 = 800 \cdot (1 + x_N + 0.5) \cdot x_N + V_N \cdot \frac{x_N}{0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Compute overflow mass** from solvent balance:

$$V_N = V_{N+1} - L_0 \cdot N = 500 - 800 \cdot 0.5 = 100 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and solve for $x_N$:**

$$200 = 800 \cdot (1.5 + x_N) \cdot x_N + 100 \cdot 2 x_N$$

$$200 = 1200 \cdot x_N + 800 \cdot x_N^{2} + 200 \cdot x_N$$

$$200 = 1400 \cdot x_N + 800 \cdot x_N^{2}$$

Dividing by 200:

$$1 = 7 x_N + 4 x_N^{2}$$

$$4 x_N^{2} + 7 x_N - 1 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x_N = \frac{-7 + \sqrt{49 + 16}}{8} = \frac{-7 + \sqrt{65}}{8} = \frac{-7 + 8.062}{8} = 0.1328 \text{ kg oil/kg inert}$$

**Step 7 — Extract concentration** $y_N = x_N / N$:

$$y_N = \frac{0.1328}{0.5} = 0.2656 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane}$$

**Step 8 — % Oil Recovery:**

$$\%\text{ Recovery} = \frac{V_N \cdot y_N}{L_0 \cdot x_0} \cdot 100 = \frac{100 \cdot 0.2656}{200} \cdot 100 = 13.28\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single stage recovers only **13.28 %** — this is why the industry uses **countercurrent multistage leaching** (typical commercial oil recovery is ≥ 95 %).

| Equipment | Best For | Solute Location | Particle Size | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Percolation (Bollman, Hildebrandt)** | Easy-to-drain solids (soy flakes) | Cellular, intact | Coarse | [KTU Dec 2022] |
| **Diffusion Battery (Carousel)** | Sugar beet cossettes | Cellular | Medium | [KTU June 2023] |

| **Rotocel** | High-tonnage oilseeds | Porous | Flaked | [KTU June 2024] |
| **Belt Extractor** | Long residence, low solvent ratio | Cellular | Flaked/coarse | [KTU model] |
