# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define leaching. List any three factors affecting the rate of leaching.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Leaching is a solid–liquid mass transfer operation in which a soluble constituent (solute) is separated from a solid matrix by dissolution into a suitable liquid solvent. The solvent-rich solution leaving the stage is the *overflow* or *extract*, and the residual solid is the *underflow* or *marc*. Three factors affecting leaching rate are:
> 1. **Particle size** of the solid (smaller → faster, but fines block drainage).
> 2. **Temperature** (higher → higher solubility and diffusivity, but may degrade thermolabile solutes).
> 3. **Agitation / solvent velocity** (improves film diffusion; enhances external mass transfer).
> *(A 4th optional factor: solvent-to-solid ratio — higher ratio improves driving force and stage efficiency.)* **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** What is a supercritical fluid? Why is CO₂ the most preferred solvent for supercritical fluid extraction in the food industry?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A supercritical fluid is a substance existing above its critical temperature and critical pressure. It possesses gas-like diffusivity and viscosity, combined with liquid-like density and solvating power, making it an excellent *tunable* solvent. CO₂ is the most preferred SCFE solvent in food processing because:
> 1. Its **critical temperature (T_c = 31.1 °C)** is low — so it can be used to extract thermolabile bioactives without degradation.
> 2. It is **non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS** and leaves **zero residue** in the product.
> 3. It is **cheap, abundant and easily recoverable** by simple depressurisation.
> 4. By varying pressure and temperature, its solvent strength can be finely tuned for selective extraction of different solutes (e.g., aroma vs. oil). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Derive the material balance equations for a **single-stage leaching** operation. Define each term clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A feed of $1000 \text{ kg}$ oilseed containing **18 % oil** and **82 % inert solids** is to be leached in a single stage with $600 \text{ kg}$ of pure hexane. The underflow retains **0.6 kg hexane per kg inert solid**. Assuming equilibrium $y^* = x/N$ and negligible solute in fresh solvent, determine:
> - (i) The mass of overflow and underflow.
> - (ii) The exit extract concentration.
> - (iii) The **% oil recovery** in a single stage. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation of single-stage material balance equations:**

Consider one ideal leaching stage. Let:
- $L_0$ = kg insoluble (inert) solid in feed.
- $x_0$ = kg solute per kg inert solid in feed.
- $V_{N+1}$ = kg pure fresh solvent.
- $y_{N+1}$ = kg solute per kg solvent in fresh solvent.
- $L_N$ = kg inert solid in exit underflow.
- $x_N$ = kg solute per kg inert solid in exit underflow.
- $V_N$ = kg overflow (extract) leaving.
- $y_N$ = kg solute per kg solvent in exit overflow.
- $N$ = kg solvent retained per kg inert solid in underflow.

**Step 1 — Inert (insoluble) solid balance** (inerts are conserved):

$$L_0 = L_N \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Step 2 — Solvent balance** (solvent in = solvent out + solvent retained by solid):

$$V_{N+1} = V_N + L_0 \cdot N \quad \cdots (ii)$$

**Step 3 — Solute balance** (solute is neither created nor destroyed):

$$L_0 \cdot x_0 + V_{N+1} \cdot y_{N+1} = L_N \cdot x_N + V_N \cdot y_N \quad \cdots (iii)$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium relationship:**

$$y_N = \frac{x_N}{N} \quad \cdots (iv)$$

Equations (i) → (iv) are the complete set of single-stage material balance equations. **Substituting (i), (ii) and (iv) into (iii) gives the exit extract concentration as a single explicit expression in terms of the given data.** **(7 marks — full derivation as above earns full marks)**

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to inert-solid basis.** Oil is solute, inert solid is the balance:

$$L_0 = 1000 \cdot 0.82 = 820 \text{ kg inert}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{\text{oil}}{\text{inert}} = \frac{180}{820} = 0.2195 \text{ kg oil/kg inert}$$

**Step 2 — Overflow mass** from solvent balance (ii):

$$V_N = V_{N+1} - L_0 \cdot N = 600 - 820 \cdot 0.6 = 600 - 492 = 108 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Underflow mass** (carrying inert + solute + retained solvent):

$$L_N^{\text{total}} = L_0 \cdot (1 + x_N + N) = 820 \cdot (1 + x_N + 0.6) = 820 \cdot (1.6 + x_N) \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply solute balance (iii)** with $y_{N+1} = 0$ (pure hexane):

$$820 \cdot 0.2195 = 820 \cdot (1.6 + x_N) \cdot x_N + 108 \cdot \frac{x_N}{0.6}$$

$$180 = 820(1.6 + x_N) x_N + 180 x_N$$

$$180 = 1312 x_N + 820 x_N^{2} + 180 x_N$$

$$180 = 1492 x_N + 820 x_N^{2}$$

Dividing throughout by 4:

$$45 = 373 x_N + 205 x_N^{2}$$

$$205 x_N^{2} + 373 x_N - 45 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x_N = \frac{-373 + \sqrt{373^{2} + 4 \cdot 205 \cdot 45}}{2 \cdot 205} = \frac{-373 + \sqrt{139129 + 36900}}{410}$$

$$x_N = \frac{-373 + \sqrt{176029}}{410} = \frac{-373 + 419.56}{410} = 0.1136 \text{ kg oil/kg inert}$$

**Step 5 — Extract concentration** from (iv):

$$y_N = \frac{x_N}{N} = \frac{0.1136}{0.6} = 0.1893 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane}$$

**Step 6 — % Oil recovery:**

$$\%\text{ Recovery} = \frac{V_N \cdot y_N}{L_0 \cdot x_0} \cdot 100 = \frac{108 \cdot 0.1893}{180} \cdot 100 = \frac{20.45}{180} \cdot 100 = 11.36\%$$

> **Final Answers (Part b):** (i) $V_N = 108$ kg, $L_N^{\text{total}} = 820(1.7136) = 1405.2$ kg; (ii) $y_N = 0.1893$ kg oil/kg hexane; (iii) Recovery = **11.36 %** (a single stage is grossly insufficient — multi-stage countercurrent operation is the industrial norm). **(7 marks)**

---

> ### **Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
>
> **(a)** Explain the principle of **supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE)** with a neat labelled P–T diagram. Discuss why CO₂ is preferred, and list four major **food-industry applications** of SCFE. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the principle of **liquid–liquid extraction (LLE)**. With the help of a triangular phase diagram, describe the **single-stage** and **three-stage countercurrent** LLE process, and state the equation for the **distribution coefficient** $K_D$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Principle of SCFE:**

A supercritical fluid (SCF) is obtained by heating a gas above its **critical temperature ($T_c$)** and compressing it above its **critical pressure ($P_c$)**. Above this critical point, the boundary between liquid and gas disappears, and a single phase exists with hybrid properties:

- **Density** comparable to a liquid → high solvating power.
- **Viscosity** comparable to a gas → low resistance to flow.
- **Diffusivity** an order of magnitude higher than liquids → rapid mass transfer.

By adjusting pressure and temperature, the **density (and hence the solvating power)** can be tuned continuously. The process flow is: pressurise solvent → contact with feed in extractor → depressurise in separator → solute precipitates out → gas solvent is recycled.

**P–T Diagram (textual description for the answer sheet):**
Draw a *P* (vertical) versus *T* (horizontal) diagram showing:
- The **sublimation, fusion and vaporisation curves** meeting at the **triple point**.
- The **vaporisation curve** terminating at the **critical point (CP)**.
- A **shaded supercritical region** above and to the right of the CP — this is the SCFE operating zone.
- An arrow showing the **extraction path** (high P, T slightly above $T_c$) and the **separation path** (pressure reduced at constant T) so that the SCF reverts to a gas and releases the solute.

**Why CO₂:** Already covered in Q2 of Part A (T_c = 31.1 °C, GRAS, non-toxic, no residue, cheap, recyclable). *(Repeat in 2–3 crisp lines for full marks.)*

**Four Food-Industry Applications of SCFE:**
1. **Decaffeination of coffee and tea** — the landmark industrial application.
2. **Extraction of hops** for brewing (replaces hexane extraction).
3. **Extraction of natural colours, flavours and nutraceuticals** (lutein, lycopene, astaxanthin, omega-3 from fish oil).
4. **Defatting of cocoa, snack foods and oilseed meals** for high-protein, low-fat ingredients. **(7 marks — full diagram + 4 applications earns top marks)**

**(b) Principle of LLE:**

Liquid–liquid extraction is a separation technique in which a solute (A) is transferred from one liquid phase (the *feed / raffinate*) into a second, immiscible liquid (the *solvent / extract*) on the basis of differing solubilities. Mass transfer continues until the chemical potential (or activity) of A in both phases is equal. The governing equilibrium relation is the **distribution (partition) coefficient**:

$$K_D = \frac{C_{A,\text{extract}}}{C_{A,\text{raffinate}}}$$

A $K_D \gg 1$ means the solute preferentially partitions into the extract phase, making LLE attractive.

**Triangular phase diagram (description for the answer sheet):**
- An **equilateral triangle** with vertices **A (solute)**, **B (feed solvent, e.g. water)**, **C (extracting solvent, e.g. hexane)**.
- The **binodal curve** (miscibility gap) is drawn inside — any mixture inside the curve splits into two conjugate phases connected by **tie-lines**.
- The **plait point** is where the two phases become identical.
- A **feed mixture of composition F** (inside the binodal) splits into **extract E** (C-rich) and **raffinate R** (B-rich), connected by a tie-line through F.

**Single-stage LLE:**
Feed F is mixed with a fixed amount of pure solvent C. The overall mixture M (F + C, lever rule) lies inside the binodal, splits into E and R on the tie-line passing through M. One theoretical stage — simple but inefficient.

**Three-stage countercurrent LFE:**
- Feed F enters **stage 1**; pure solvent C enters **stage 3**.
- The **raffinate R_N** from stage 3 (now A-lean) is the final raffinate product.
- The **extract E_1** from stage 1 (now A-rich) is the final extract product.
- Solvent and raffinate flow countercurrently between the three stages, each at equilibrium.
- **Stage construction on the triangular diagram** uses the "mixing point" and "tie-line" alternation: starting from the feed composition, add solvent to get $M_1$, draw tie-line to $R_1$, mix $R_1$ with fresh solvent to get $M_2$, draw tie-line to $R_2$, mix $R_2$ with solvent to get $M_3$, draw tie-line to $R_3$ (the final raffinate). The number of mixers-settlers required to reach a target raffinate composition is read off graphically. **(7 marks — full text + diagram description + $K_D$ equation)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong basis of x and y.** Always express *x* in kg solute per kg **inert solid**, and *y* in kg solute per kg **solvent** — never per kg of total underflow or total overflow.
> 2. **Confusing "overflow" with "extract" and "underflow" with "marc".** These are synonymous but the *basis changes* — overflow mass ≠ extract mass; it includes both solute and solvent.
> 3. **Forgetting to check that $K > 1$** before applying the Kremser equation. If $K < 1$, the number of stages is negative — a sign of either a calculation error or an infeasible design.
> 4. **In SCFE diagrams, not labelling the critical point** explicitly. Examiners allot 1–2 marks purely for correctly identifying the critical point and the SCF region on the P–T diagram.
> 5. **In LLE, mixing up the vertices of the triangular diagram.** A = solute, B = feed solvent, C = extractant. Reversing B and C will lead to wrong tie-line interpretation and lost marks.
> 6. **Algebraic slips in the quadratic solution for $x_N$.** Re-check by substitution back into the original solute balance. Even examiners are human — a clearly shown substitution earns the benefit of the doubt.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print and keep:**

- **Leaching** = solid–liquid extraction; solute dissolves out, leaving a *marc*.
- **Rate-controlling step** in leaching is **internal diffusion**; hence smaller particle, higher temperature, lower solvent viscosity, and proper agitation all enhance the rate.
- The three material balance equations for **one ideal stage** are: *inert balance*, *solvent balance*, *solute balance* — always 3 equations in 3 unknowns ($L_N$, $V_N$, $x_N$, $y_N$ — but with equilibrium (iv) you solve for *x_N* or *y_N*).
- The **equilibrium relation** for constant underflow is $y = x/N$ — a *straight* line of slope $1/N$.
- The **operating line** for countercurrent leaching is $y_{n+1} = (L_0/V_{N+1}) x_n + \text{const}$ — another *straight* line.
- The **Kremser equation** gives the number of ideal stages: $N' = \log[(x_0 - y_{N+1}/K)/(x_N - y_{N+1}/K)] / \log K$ with $K = L_0 N / V_{N+1}$.
- **Stage efficiency** uses the Murphree form: $\eta = (x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{act}}) / (x_{n-1} - x_n^{\text{eq}})$.
- **SCFE** uses a *tunable* supercritical solvent (typically CO₂) with $T_c = 31.1\ °C$ and $P_c = 73.8$ bar.
- **CO₂** is preferred in food SCFE because of low $T_c$, GRAS status, zero residue, recyclability, and non-flammability.
- **LLE** depends on the **distribution coefficient** $K_D = C_{A,\text{extract}} / C_{A,\text{raffinate}}$; design is done on a **triangular phase diagram** with binodal curve and tie-lines.
- The **McCabe–Thiele stepping method** applies to leaching just as it does to distillation — the difference is the *axis basis* (per kg inert vs. per kg solvent).
- For **14-mark numericals**, always: (1) define symbols, (2) state assumptions (constant underflow, equilibrium reached, fresh solvent pure), (3) write the four equations, (4) solve step by step, (5) verify by computing recovery.
- A **single-stage** recovery is usually < 30 %; industry always uses **3–5 countercurrent stages** to achieve > 95 % recovery.
- **SCFE food applications** — decaffeination, hop extraction, natural colour/antioxidant extraction, defatting of cocoa, oilseed processing, spice oleoresins.
- **LLE food applications** — refining of vegetable oils (degumming, deacidification), recovery of lactic acid from fermentation broth, extraction of flavours from aqueous slurries, debittering of citrus juices.
- KTU examiners reward **neatly drawn diagrams** (P–T for SCFE, triangular for LLE, McCabe–Thiele for multistage leaching) — even a small sketch earns 1–2 marks.
- Always **end a numerical** with a *recovery* calculation as a self-check; it reassures the examiner and the student.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for Module 4 Extraction:** *"Know the basis. Write the three balances. Apply the equilibrium. Step the stages. Check the recovery."* If you internalise these five commands, you will comfortably clear any KTU 2024 Scheme question on this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation: Principles of Evaporation, Mass and Energy Balance, Factors Affecting Rate of Evaporation, Heat and Mass Transfer in Evaporator, Factors Influencing the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, Influence of Feed Liquor Properties on Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaporation: Principles of Evaporation, Mass and Energy Balance, Factors Affecting Rate of Evaporation, Heat and Mass Transfer in Evaporator, Factors Influencing the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, Influence of Feed Liquor Properties on Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (usually water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute (dissolved solids) by the application of heat, thereby concentrating the solution. In food processing, evaporation is the principal method for concentrating liquid foods such as milk, fruit juices, sugar syrups, coffee extracts, and broths prior to drying or as a finished product.

The operation is carried out in a heat exchanger called an **evaporator**, where the feed liquor is brought to boiling by indirect contact with a condensing heating medium (most commonly **steam**). The solvent vapor leaving the surface, called the *secondary vapor*, may be condensed (single-effect) or reused to supply heat to a downstream evaporator (multiple-effect), which is the foundation of steam economy in industrial practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Evaporation is *not* the same as *drying*. In evaporation, water is removed as vapor from a boiling liquid; in drying, water is removed from a solid/semi-solid by evaporation into an unsaturated gas stream.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine heating a pot of milk on your kitchen stove to make khoya. As the milk boils, water vapor escapes and the milk becomes thicker and thicker. Three things must happen for this to occur quickly:

1. **A heat source** must deliver energy (your stove's burner).
2. **The liquid must be able to boil** — its vapor pressure must reach the surrounding atmospheric pressure.
3. **The vapor must escape** — dissolved solids that are sticky (like sugar) slow this down by making the liquid syrupy.

Industrial evaporation is the engineered, continuous, and energy-efficient version of your kitchen pot — done in closed vessels using steam, vacuum, or mechanical vapor recompression to *recover* and *reuse* that escaping vapor instead of letting it disappear into the kitchen air.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water at 100 °C:** $\lambda_v = 2257 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Saturated Steam at 120 °C:** $\lambda_s \approx 2203 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Standard Specific Heat of Liquid Water:** $C_p = 4.186 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$

### 1.4 Key Sub-Topics to be Studied (Roadmap)

The KTU Module 4 syllabus is structured into **six interlinked sub-topics**. The following mind-map shows the logical flow:

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Roadmap:** You begin with the *thermodynamic principles* (why evaporation works), move to *balances* (how much steam/vapor is needed), then to *rate mechanisms* (how fast it occurs), and finally to *feed-liquor-specific design considerations* (how food properties alter everything above).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Evaporation

Evaporation is governed by the **phase equilibrium** of the water–solute system. Four physical principles underpin it:

**P1. Vapor Pressure Lowering (Raoult's Law for Ideal Solutions)**
The partial pressure of water over a solution is less than that over pure water at the same temperature. Hence the solution must be heated to a *higher temperature* to boil at a given pressure.

$$p_w = p_w^{sat} \cdot x_w$$

where $x_w$ is the mole fraction of water in the liquid phase. This is the *root cause* of boiling-point elevation.

**P2. Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**
A consequence of vapor-pressure lowering. The boiling point of the solution exceeds that of pure water:

$$\Delta T_{bp} = T_b^{solution} - T_b^{water}$$

$\Delta T_{bp}$ increases with solute concentration. For food systems with high solids (e.g., concentrated sugar syrups), BPE can reach **15–25 °C**.

**P3. Latent Heat of Vaporization (Duhring's Rule)**
The latent heat of vaporization decreases with rising boiling temperature. For dilute aqueous food systems, the latent heat is very close to that of pure water. For precise work, Duhring's rule (linear plot of solution BP vs. pure-water BP at equal pressures) is used to estimate $\lambda_v$ at any operating pressure.

**P4. Sensitivity of Food Components (Thermal Degradation)**
Many food constituents (vitamins, flavor volatiles, color pigments) degrade above certain temperatures. This forces evaporators to operate under **vacuum**, reducing the boiling temperature and hence the degradation rate.

### 2.2 Mass Balance on an Evaporator

For a *single-effect* continuous evaporator operating at steady state:

**Total Material Balance:**

$$F = V + L$$

**Component (Solids) Balance:** Since solids are non-volatile:

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

Combining the two, the *water removed per unit feed* is:

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

and the *concentrated liquid flow rate* is:

$$L = F \left(\frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

### 2.3 Energy Balance on an Evaporator

The energy entering (steam + feed enthalpy) equals the energy leaving (secondary vapor + concentrated liquid enthalpy), assuming negligible heat loss:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s + F \cdot h_F = V \cdot H_V + L \cdot h_L$$

For dilute solutions where sensible heat of vapor equals $\lambda_v$ and solution enthalpy is referenced to feed temperature:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_{p,F} \cdot (T_b - T_F)$$

### 2.4 Factors Affecting the Rate of Evaporation

The rate is governed by **one master equation** — the rate of heat transfer across the heating surface:

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{q}{\lambda_v} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{effective}}{\lambda_v}$$

Hence the rate depends on:

1. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** — depends on steam-side and liquid-side film coefficients, wall resistance, and fouling.
2. **Effective Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_{eff}$)** — actual temperature drop driving heat transfer (steam minus boiling liquid).
3. **Heat Transfer Area (A)** — design parameter.
4. **Boiling Point Elevation** — reduces effective $\Delta T$.
5. **Hydrostatic Head in Tall Tubes** — raises local boiling point.
6. **Physical Properties of Feed** — viscosity, specific heat, density.

### 2.5 Heat and Mass Transfer in an Evaporator

**Heat Transfer Path** (resistance-in-series analogy):

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_s} + \frac{x}{k_w} + R_{fouling} + \frac{1}{h_l}$$

- $h_s$ = steam-side condensing film coefficient (very high, ~5000–15000 W/m²K).
- $x/k_w$ = tube-wall conduction resistance (negligible for thin-walled tubes).
- $R_{fouling}$ = scale/fouling resistance (often the dominant resistance!).
- $h_l$ = liquid-side nucleate-boiling coefficient (depends on circulation velocity and viscosity).

**Mass Transfer Path** — Vapor generated at the heating surface must escape through the liquid column. The mass-transfer flux depends on:

$$N_A = k_c \cdot (p_w^* - p_w)$$

where $p_w^*$ is the equilibrium vapor pressure and $p_w$ is the bulk partial pressure.

### 2.6 Factors Influencing the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

| Factor | Effect on U | Engineering Response |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Increasing liquid viscosity | Decreases U (lower $h_l$) | Forced circulation, falling-film evaporator |
| Scale / fouling deposition | Decreases U sharply | Periodic cleaning, polish pre-treatment |
| Higher circulation velocity | Increases U | Mechanical agitator or pump |
| Higher steam pressure | Increases $h_s$ modestly | Moderate-pressure steam (1.5–3 bar) |
| Lower liquid level (tube immersion) | Higher U (natural convection better) | Long-tube vertical design |
| Lower boiling temperature (vacuum) | Variable | Vacuum reduces BPE but also $\Delta T$ |
| Tube material | Negligible for stainless steel | Use high-conductivity alloys for fouling layers |

### 2.7 Influence of Feed Liquor Properties

| Property | Influence on Evaporator Design |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| **Solids concentration** | Higher inlet solids → lower $V$, lower steam load |
| **Viscosity** | High viscosity → poor heat transfer → forced circulation needed |
| **Boiling Point Elevation** | High BPE → larger $\Delta T$ needed → more stages |
| **Thermal sensitivity** | Heat-sensitive foods → low-temperature vacuum evaporation |
| **Scaling tendency** | Hard water, calcium-rich foods → wide-tube / falling-film design |
| **Foaming** | Surfactant-rich foods (e.g., milk, soy) → mechanical de-aeration, baffles |
| **Corrosivity** | Acidic foods (e.g., tomato, citrus) → SS-316 construction |
| **Specific heat** | Lower $C_p$ → less pre-heating load |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION RULE:** In the following table, all absolute-value / divider bars use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table syntax. **Never** use the unescaped pipe $\vert$ inside a cell.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total mass balance | kg/h |
| 2 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Solids balance | kg solids/h |
| 3 | $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Water removed | kg/h |
| 4 | $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_{p,F} \cdot (T_b - T_F)$ | Energy balance (dilute solution) | kJ/h |
| 5 | $E = V / S$ | Evaporator Economy (kg vapor/kg steam) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Rate of heat transfer | W (or kJ/h) |
| 7 | $V = q / \lambda_v = U A \Delta T / \lambda_v$ | Evaporation rate | kg/h |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_s} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + R_f + \dfrac{1}{h_l}$ | Resistance-in-series for U | m²K/W |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{eff} = (T_s - T_b)$ | Effective driving force | °C or K |
| 10 | $C_p^{feed} \approx 4.19 - 2.35 \cdot x_F$ | Empirical $C_p$ for sugar solutions (kJ/kg·K) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{bp} = T_b^{soln} - T_b^{water}$ | Boiling point elevation | °C |
| 12 | $A = \dfrac{V \cdot \lambda_v}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}$ | Heat transfer area | m² |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the Indian food industry (a key KTU recruitment sector), evaporation is critical for:

- **Dairy sector** — concentrating milk to 50% total solids for milk powder plants (plants in Gujarat, Karnataka).
- **Sugar industry** — multi-effect evaporators handling 1000+ tonnes of cane juice per day in Maharashtra and UP.
- **Fruit juice** — orange, mango, and grape juice concentration in Maharashtra and Kerala.
- **Tomato paste** — high-BPR systems requiring falling-film evaporators.

Designs always optimize **steam economy (E)** because steam is the dominant operating cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical (Analytical Matrix)

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Combined Mass + Energy Balance for a Single-Effect Evaporator

**Statement:** Starting from the steady-state mass and energy balances, derive the steam-consumption equation for an evaporator concentrating a dilute aqueous solution.

**Step 1 — Write the total mass balance.**

$$F = V + L$$

**Step 2 — Write the solids balance (solutes are non-volatile).**

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

**Step 3 — Solve the two equations simultaneously to eliminate $L$.**

From Step 1: $L = F - V$. Substituting into Step 2:

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L$$

Rearrange for $V$:

$$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F$$

$$V \cdot x_L = F (x_L - x_F)$$

$$V = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right) = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

This is the **vapor-flow equation**. Logical check: if $x_L = x_F$ (no concentration), $V = 0$ ✓.

**Step 4 — Write the energy balance** (steam in + feed enthalpy = vapor out + concentrate enthalpy).

$$S \cdot \lambda_s + F \cdot h_F = V \cdot H_V + L \cdot h_L$$

**Step 5 — Express each enthalpy** (with reference temperature at the feed inlet $T_F$):

- $h_F = C_{p,F} (T_F - T_F) = 0$
- $h_L = C_{p,L} (T_b - T_F)$ (concentrate leaves at boiling point)
- $H_V = \lambda_v + C_{p,V} (T_v - T_F) \approx \lambda_v$ (secondary vapor leaves at $T_b$; for dilute foods $T_v = T_b$)

Substituting:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s + 0 = V \cdot \lambda_v + L \cdot C_{p,L} (T_b - T_F)$$

But $L = F - V$, so:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + (F - V) \cdot C_{p,L} (T_b - T_F)$$

This is the **general single-effect energy equation**. For *dilute* food solutions where $V \approx F$ and $C_{p,L} \approx C_{p,F} = C_p$, it simplifies to:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for steam consumption.**

$$S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)}{\lambda_s}$$

**Step 7 — Evaporator Economy.**

$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_s}{V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)}$$

For a *single-effect* evaporator operating on dilute aqueous solution with steam at ~120 °C, $E$ typically ranges from **0.75 to 0.90**. Multiple-effect evaporators boost $E$ to 1.5–3.5.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical 1 — Comprehensive Single-Effect Evaporator Design

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:**
> A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a fruit juice (modeled as a dilute aqueous solution) from an initial concentration of 12% total solids to a final concentration of 50% total solids. The feed flow rate is **12,000 kg/h** entering at **30 °C**. Saturated steam is supplied at **3 bar gauge (≈ 4 bar absolute)** with a condensing temperature of **143 °C** and latent heat **$\lambda_s = 2138$ kJ/kg**. The juice boils at **80 °C** (under vacuum), giving a latent heat of vaporization of **$\lambda_v = 2308$ kJ/kg**. Take specific heat of dilute juice as **$C_{p,F} = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K)** and the overall heat transfer coefficient as **$U = 2200$ W/(m²·K)**.

> **Tasks:**
> (a) Calculate the rate of water removal, the concentrated juice flow rate, and the steam consumption rate.
> (b) Calculate the evaporator economy and the required heat-transfer area.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the mass balance.**

Given: $F = 12{,}000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.12$, $x_L = 0.50$.

Vapor (water removed):

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 12{,}000 \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.50}\right)$$

$$V = 12{,}000 \times (1 - 0.24) = 12{,}000 \times 0.76$$

$$\boxed{V = 9120 \ \text{kg/h}}$$

Concentrated juice flow:

$$L = F - V = 12{,}000 - 9120$$

$$\boxed{L = 2880 \ \text{kg/h}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy balance to find steam rate.**

Steam entering carries latent heat $\lambda_s$; feed carries zero enthalpy (reference at 30 °C); vapor leaves with $\lambda_v$ at 80 °C; concentrate leaves at boiling temperature $T_b = 80$ °C.

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_{p,F} (T_b - T_F)$$

$$S \cdot 2138 = 9120 \times 2308 + 12{,}000 \times 3.6 \times (80 - 30)$$

Compute each term:

$$9120 \times 2308 = 21{,}048{,}960 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

$$12{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 50 = 2{,}160{,}000 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

Sum:

$$S \cdot 2138 = 21{,}048{,}960 + 2{,}160{,}000 = 23{,}208{,}960 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{23{,}208{,}960}{2138} = 10{,}855 \ \text{kg/h (approx)}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaporator economy.**

$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{9120}{10{,}855} = 0.840$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.84 \ \text{kg vapor per kg steam}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer area.**

Total heat duty $q$:

$$q = S \cdot \lambda_s = 23{,}208{,}960 \ \text{kJ/h}$$

Convert to watts (1 kJ/h = 1/3.6 W):

$$q = \frac{23{,}208{,}960}{3600} = 6447 \ \text{kW} = 6{,}447{,}000 \ \text{W}$$

Effective temperature difference:

$$\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b = 143 - 80 = 63 \ \text{K}$$

Required area:

$$A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}} = \frac{6{,}447{,}000}{2200 \times 63}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 46.5 \ \text{m}^2}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — Effect of Boiling Point Elevation on Area

> **Problem:** A sugar solution is concentrated from 10% to 30% solids in a single-effect evaporator. The solution shows a **Boiling Point Elevation of 6 °C** at the operating pressure. The steam temperature is **120 °C** and pure water would boil at **100 °C** at the same pressure. Compare the heat transfer area required (a) ignoring BPE and (b) accounting for BPE. Take $U = 1800$ W/m²K, $V = 8000$ kg/h, $\lambda_v = 2260$ kJ/kg.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Heat duty (same in both cases).**

$$q = V \cdot \lambda_v = 8000 \times 2260 = 18{,}080{,}000 \ \text{kJ/h} = 5{,}022{,}222 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Case (a): Ignoring BPE.**

$$\Delta T_a = 120 - 100 = 20 \ \text{K}$$

$$A_a = \frac{5{,}022{,}222}{1800 \times 20} = 139.5 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Case (b): With BPE = 6 °C, the solution boils at 106 °C.**

$$\Delta T_b = 120 - 106 = 14 \ \text{K}$$

$$A_b = \frac{5{,}022{,}222}{1800 \times 14} = 199.3 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Increase in area required.**

$$\frac{A_b - A_a}{A_a} \times 100\% = \frac{199.3 - 139.5}{139.5} \times 100\% \approx 42.9\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{BPE increases the required area by } \approx 43\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This 43% increase shows why BPE is a *critical* design parameter in food evaporators handling high-solids streams (e.g., sugar syrups, condensed milk, tomato paste).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Evaporation Rate

| Variable | Effect on Rate | Magnitude of Impact (typical food system) |
|----------|---------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Steam pressure ↑ | $\Delta T$ ↑ → rate ↑ | High (linear in $\Delta T$) |
| Vacuum ↑ | $T_b$ ↓, BPE ↓, but $\lambda_v$ ↑ | Moderate |
| Viscosity ↑ | $h_l$ ↓ → $U$ ↓ → rate ↓ | Strong |
| Solids ↑ | BPE ↑ → $\Delta T_{eff}$ ↓ → rate ↓ | Moderate to strong |
| Scale thickness ↑ | $R_f$ ↑ → $U$ ↓ → rate ↓ | Very strong (exponential decay) |
| Liquid velocity ↑ | $h_l$ ↑ → $U$ ↑ → rate ↑ | Moderate (forced circulation) |
| Feed pre-heating ↑ | Reduces sensible-heat load on steam | Indirect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Block-Level Architecture of a Single-Effect Evaporator

The following mermaid block maps the *process flow* and *energy streams* of a single-effect evaporator — the foundational architecture every KTU student must know.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED ["FEED SECTION"]
        F1["Feed Tank\nT = 30 C"]
        P1["Feed Pump"]
        HX1["Pre Heater\nSteam Side"]
    end

    subgraph VESSEL ["EVAPORATOR VESSEL"]
        ST["Steam Chest\nSteam at 3 bar"]
        T1["Heating Tubes\nU = 2200 W/m2K"]
        VB["Vapor Liquid\nMixture"]
        SEP["Vapor Liquid\nSeparator"]
        V1["Vacuum Pump\nVapor Outlet"]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT ["PRODUCT SECTION"]
        C1["Condenser\nfor Secondary Vapor"]
        W1["Cooling Water In"]
        W2["Cooling Water Out"]
        P2["Concentrate\nPump"]
        TK["Concentrate\nStorage"]
    end

    F1 --> P1 --> HX1 --> T1
    ST --> T1
    T1 --> VB --> SEP
    SEP --> C1
    SEP --> P2 --> TK
    C1 --> W1
    W1 --> W2
    V1 --> SEP
```

**Description:** Dilute feed is pumped from the feed tank, pre-heated by steam bled from the steam chest, then enters the evaporator vessel. Inside, steam condenses on the outer surface of the heating tubes, transmitting heat through the tube wall into the boiling liquid. The vapor-liquid mixture rises into a separator; the secondary vapor is drawn off by a vacuum pump and condensed (often with cooling water) while the concentrated liquid is pumped to storage.

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Heat Transfer Resistance Network (Wall of Heating Tube)

This network represents the resistance-in-series model that defines $U$ in an evaporator. **Each node is a thermal-resistance component; the equivalent resistance gives $1/U$.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["R steam\nh_s condensation"]
    R2["R tube wall\nx over k_w"]
    R3["R fouling\nscale deposit"]
    R4["R liquid\nh_l boiling"]
    SUM["1 over U\nseries sum"]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> SUM
```

**Description:** Heat from condensing steam must traverse *four* resistances in series: (i) the steam-side condensing film, (ii) the conduction through the metal tube wall, (iii) the fouling/scale layer (often the largest), and (iv) the liquid-side boiling film. The total resistance $1/U$ is the sum.

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Functional Flow of Factors Affecting Evaporation Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Rate of Evaporation\nV dot"]
    N1["Heat Duty\nq = U A delta T"]
    N2["U\nOverall Heat Transfer"]
    N3["A\nHeat Transfer Area"]
    N4["Delta T effective"]
    N11["Steam temperature"]
    N12["Boiling temperature"]
    N13["BPE"]
    N14["Hydrostatic head"]
    N21["Steam side h_s"]
    N22["Wall and scale"]
    N23["Liquid side h_l"]
    N24["Liquid viscosity"]
    N25["Circulation velocity"]

    ROOT --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N1 --> N4
    N2 --> N21
    N2 --> N22
    N2 --> N23
    N2 --> N24
    N2 --> N25
    N4 --> N11
    N4 --> N12
    N4 --> N13
    N4 --> N14
```

**Description:** The evaporation rate is governed by the triple product $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$. Each factor has parent sub-factors. This is the conceptual map examiners expect students to reproduce in 2-mark questions.

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Influence Map of Feed Liquor Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROP["Feed Liquor\nProperties"]
    P1["Concentration\nx_F"]
    P2["Viscosity\nmu"]
    P3["BPE\ndelta T_bp"]
    P4["Thermal\nsensitivity"]
    P5["Scaling\ntendency"]
    P6["Foaming"]
    P7["Corrosivity"]
    P8["Specific heat\nC_p"]
    D1["Vapor load\nkg per h"]
    D2["U value\nW per m2 K"]
    D3["Delta T effective\nK"]
    D4["Vacuum level\nand materials"]

    PROP --> P1
    PROP --> P2
    PROP --> P3
    PROP --> P4
    PROP --> P5
    PROP --> P6
    PROP --> P7
    PROP --> P8
    P1 --> D1
    P2 --> D2
    P3 --> D3
    P4 --> D4
    P5 --> D2
    P6 --> D4
    P7 --> D4
    P8 --> D1
```

**Description:** Each feed-liquor property drives a specific design parameter. A high-viscosity feed (e.g., concentrated tomato paste) needs forced circulation to keep $U$ acceptable; a heat-sensitive feed (e.g., orange juice) demands vacuum operation to lower boiling temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**. Why is it an important design parameter in food evaporators?

**Model Answer (≈ 12 lines):**
Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) is the difference between the boiling temperature of a solution and that of pure water at the same pressure. It arises because dissolved solutes lower the vapor pressure of water (Raoult's Law), so a higher temperature is needed to reach atmospheric vapor pressure. **Mathematically, $\Delta T_{bp} = T_b^{solution} - T_b^{water}$.** In food evaporators, BPE is critical because it directly reduces the *effective temperature difference* available for heat transfer, thereby lowering the rate of evaporation and increasing the heat-transfer area required. BPE is most pronounced at high solids concentration (e.g., concentrated sugar syrups, condensed milk), making the last effect of a multiple-effect evaporator significantly less efficient than the first.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State **any three factors** that affect the overall heat transfer coefficient (U) of an evaporator.

**Model Answer (≈ 10 lines):**
The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ depends on the resistances of the steam film, the tube wall, the fouling layer, and the liquid film (in series). Three important factors are:

1. **Liquid viscosity** — Higher viscosity reduces the liquid-side boiling coefficient $h_l$, lowering $U$.
2. **Fouling / scale formation** — Solid deposits on the tube wall add a large thermal resistance $R_f$, sharply reducing $U$.
3. **Circulation velocity of the boiling liquid** — Forced circulation enhances $h_l$ and hence $U$; natural convection gives lower $U$.

Other valid answers: tube material thickness, steam-side condensate drainage, steam pressure, and presence of non-condensable gases.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Single-Effect Numerical

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

(a) Derive the mass balance and energy balance equations for a single-effect evaporator concentrating a dilute aqueous solution. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

(b) A single-effect evaporator concentrates **8000 kg/h** of milk from **15% total solids to 50% total solids**. The feed enters at **40 °C**. Saturated steam is supplied at **110 °C** ($\lambda_s = 2230$ kJ/kg). The milk boils at **75 °C** under vacuum ($\lambda_v = 2270$ kJ/kg). Specific heat of milk (dilute) = **3.8 kJ/(kg·K)**. Calculate:
- (i) the rate of water removal,
- (ii) steam consumption rate, and
- (iii) the evaporator economy. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, continuous operation.
2. Solutes are completely non-volatile.
3. Heat loss to surroundings is negligible.
4. Feed and concentrate are at the same composition throughout (well-mixed).
5. Secondary vapor leaves at the boiling point of the solution (no superheat).

**Mass Balance Derivation:**

$$F = V + L \quad \text{(Total mass)} \qquad (1)$$

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L \quad \text{(Solids, non-volatile)} \qquad (2)$$

Substituting $L = F - V$ from (1) into (2):

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) \qquad (3)$$

**Energy Balance Derivation:**

Total enthalpy in = Total enthalpy out (steady state, reference at $T_F$):

$$S \cdot \lambda_s + F \cdot h_F = V \cdot H_V + L \cdot h_L$$

Taking $h_F = 0$, $H_V \approx \lambda_v$, $h_L = C_{p,L}(T_b - T_F)$, $L = F - V$, and $C_{p,L} \approx C_{p,F} = C_p$:

$$\boxed{S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + (F - V) \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)}$$

For dilute solutions where $V \approx F$:

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $F = 8000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.15$, $x_L = 0.50$, $T_F = 40$ °C, $T_b = 75$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2230$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_v = 2270$ kJ/kg, $C_p = 3.8$ kJ/(kg·K).

**(i) Rate of water removal:**

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 8000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.15}{0.50}\right)$$

$$V = 8000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 8000 \times 0.70$$

$$\boxed{V = 5600 \ \text{kg/h}}$$

Concentrate flow:

$$L = F - V = 8000 - 5600 = 2400 \ \text{kg/h}$$

**(ii) Steam consumption:**

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)$$

$$S \cdot 2230 = 5600 \times 2270 + 8000 \times 3.8 \times (75 - 40)$$

$$S \cdot 2230 = 12{,}712{,}000 + 1{,}064{,}000$$

$$S \cdot 2230 = 13{,}776{,}000$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{13{,}776{,}000}{2230} = 6178 \ \text{kg/h (approx)}}$$

**(iii) Evaporator economy:**

$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{5600}{6178}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.906 \ \text{kg vapor/kg steam}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Theory + Conceptual

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

(a) Explain with a neat diagram the **principle and operation of a single-effect evaporator**. List the major components and state their functions. **(7 marks)**

(b) Discuss in detail the **factors that influence the overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** of an evaporator. How does each factor affect the design? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — Principle & Operation (7 marks):**

A single-effect evaporator consists of: (i) a *steam chest* or *calandria* holding condensing heating steam, (ii) *heating tubes* or *coils* across which heat is transferred, (iii) a *vapor–liquid separator* (often called a *vapor head* or *catchall*) to remove entrained droplets, (iv) a *condenser* for the secondary vapor, and (v) a *vacuum pump* if sub-atmospheric operation is desired.

**Working principle:** Dilute feed enters the evaporator and is heated by indirect contact with condensing steam. The solvent (water) vaporizes at the boiling temperature corresponding to the operating pressure. The vapor passes through the separator to remove liquid entrainment, then to a condenser where it is condensed using cooling water. The concentrated liquid is withdrawn from the bottom.

Draw the diagram (refer to Figure 4.1 above) — credit for neatness, labeling of all five parts, and arrows showing feed, steam, vapor, concentrate, and cooling water flows.

**Part (b) — Factors Influencing U (7 marks):**

(Present as a tabular answer for clarity, expanding each point with explanation.)

| Factor | Effect on $U$ | Engineering Implication |
|--------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Steam-side coefficient $h_s$** | Higher steam velocity and purity increase $h_s$ | Good condensate drainage; trap non-condensables |
| **Wall resistance $x/k_w$** | Thin-walled tubes give lower resistance | Use thin-walled stainless-steel tubes |
| **Fouling / scale $R_f$** | Scale is the dominant resistance in food evaporators | Periodic CIP (clean-in-place); wide tubes |
| **Liquid-side coefficient $h_l$** | Higher with forced circulation and lower viscosity | Forced-circulation pumps; falling-film evaporators |
| **Liquid viscosity** | High viscosity → low $h_l$ → low $U$ | Pre-concentration, heating, mechanical agitation |
| **Operating pressure (vacuum)** | Lower pressure → lower $\Delta T$ → may lower $U$ | Optimize: balance heat duty vs. residence time |
| **Non-condensable gases** | Accumulate on steam side → reduce $h_s$ | Continuous venting |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In **Part A numerical**, students often forget to use $T_b - T_F$ correctly (sign error). Always check that $T_b > T_F$, otherwise the equation needs modification.
> - In **energy-balance derivations**, do **NOT** write $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + L \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)$ without first showing the substitution $L = F - V$. Examiners deduct 2 marks for incomplete derivation.
> - For **BPE-based questions**, students frequently forget to subtract BPE from the steam-to-water $\Delta T$, leading to *over-estimation* of $U \cdot A$ by ~40% in high-solids cases.
> - Always state **assumptions** before starting a derivation; not stating them costs 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Read Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Evaporation** = vaporization of solvent from a solution by boiling, distinct from drying.
- [ ] **Vapor-pressure lowering** (Raoult's Law) is the *root cause* of Boiling Point Elevation (BPE).
- [ ] **Mass balance:** $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ and $L = F \cdot x_F / x_L$.
- [ ] **Energy balance:** $S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot C_p (T_b - T_F)$ (dilute solutions).
- [ ] **Evaporator Economy $E = V/S$**; single-effect $E \approx 0.75$–$0.90$.
- [ ] **Master rate equation:** $\dot{V} = U A \Delta T_{eff} / \lambda_v$.
- [ ] **Resistance-in-series:** $1/U = 1/h_s + x/k_w + R_f + 1/h_l$.
- [ ] **Fouling / scale resistance $R_f$** is usually the *largest* single resistance in food evaporators.
- [ ] **BPE increases with solute concentration**; high-BPR systems need larger heat-transfer area or more effects.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic head** in tall vertical tubes raises local boiling point → reduces $\Delta T$.
- [ ] **Feed properties influencing design:** concentration, viscosity, BPE, thermal sensitivity, scaling, foaming, corrosivity, $C_p$.
- [ ] **Heat-sensitive foods** → operate under vacuum; **high-viscosity foods** → forced circulation or falling-film.
- [ ] **Duhring's rule** is used to estimate solution boiling points at different pressures.
- [ ] **Units discipline:** $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K), $\lambda$ in kJ/kg, $F$ in kg/h, $q$ converted to W (÷3.6) before using SI.
- [ ] **Multiple-effect evaporators** improve economy but require careful staging of pressure and temperature.
- [ ] Always quote **assumptions** before a derivation; state **direction of heat flow** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT415 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaporation Equipment: Natural Circulation Evaporators, Horizontal/Vertical Short Tube, Long Tube, Forced Circulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — EVAPORATION EQUIPMENT: A COMPREHENSIVE CLASSIFICATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporators** are heat-transfer devices used in food processing to concentrate a solution by removing water (or other volatile solvents) through boiling. The energy balance is driven by **steam** on the shell side, while the liquid being concentrated circulates over **tube surfaces**. Evaporation equipment is broadly classified based on the **mode of circulation** of the liquid and the **tube geometry**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT415)**, evaporation equipment is categorized primarily into:

1. **Natural Circulation Evaporators** — circulation driven by *natural convection* due to density difference between rising vapors/liquid and downcoming liquid.
2. **Forced Circulation Evaporators** — circulation driven by an *external mechanical pump* (usually a centrifugal pump).

Under natural circulation, sub-classifications based on tube geometry include:

- **Horizontal Short Tube Evaporator** (also called the *calandria* or *horizontal tube evaporator*)
- **Vertical Short Tube Evaporator** (also called the *standard evaporator* or *basket-type evaporator*)
- **Long Tube Vertical Evaporator** (sub-types: *Climbing Film* and *Falling Film*)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boiling Kettle vs. The Pumped Boiler"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine you are boiling milk in a wide, shallow pan over a campfire. The hot milk at the bottom rises naturally (because it becomes less dense), while the cooler milk descends to take its place. This *self-stirring* motion is **natural circulation** — no mechanical pump is needed; density differences do the work.
>
> Now imagine instead you are pushing tomato concentrate through a tall, thin stainless-steel pipe wrapped in a steam jacket, using an external pump. The fluid is *forced* through the tubes at high velocity, regardless of density-driven currents. This is **forced circulation**.

### 🎯 Real-World Food Industry Examples

| Equipment Type | Common Food Application |
|---|---|
| Horizontal Short Tube (Calandria) | Sugar refining, milk concentration |
| Vertical Short Tube (Basket) | Tomato paste, fruit juice concentration |
| Long Tube Climbing Film | Milk, coffee extracts, citrus juice |
| Long Tube Falling Film | Heat-sensitive juices (orange, pineapple) |
| Forced Circulation | High-viscosity products (starch slurries, gelatin) |

---

## 1.3 Why Equipment Selection Matters in Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat-sensitive foods** (dairy, fruit juices) require **short residence times** → Long Tube Vertical (especially Falling Film) or Forced Circulation.
> **Viscous or fouling liquids** (sugar syrups, tomato paste) require **low tube velocities and good agitation** → Forced Circulation or Vertical Short Tube.
> **Low-cost, traditional operations** → Horizontal Short Tube (Calandria).

---

## 1.4 Standard Units & Physical Constants Used

- Heat transfer coefficient: $U = \mathbf{W/m^2 \cdot K}$
- Tube length: $L = \mathbf{m}$
- Tube diameter: $D_i = \mathbf{m}$ (inner), $D_o = \mathbf{m}$ (outer)
- Steam-side temperature: $T_s = \mathbf{^\circ C}$
- Boiling point of liquid: $T_b = \mathbf{^\circ C}$
- Overall temperature difference: $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$
- Latent heat of vaporization: $\lambda = \mathbf{kJ/kg}$
- Circulation velocity: $u = \mathbf{m/s}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Evaporators

The hierarchy follows directly from the **driving force for liquid circulation**:

> Natural Circulation (density-driven)
> ├── Horizontal Tube Evaporator (calandria)
> ├── Vertical Short Tube Evaporator (basket-type)
> └── Long Tube Vertical Evaporator
>     ├── Climbing Film (upward co-current)
>     └── Falling Film (downward co-current)

> Forced Circulation (pump-driven)
> └── Forced Circulation Evaporator (vertical/horizontal shell with external pump)

---

## 2.2 Theory of Natural Circulation — The Buoyancy Engine

The principle behind every natural-circulation evaporator is rooted in the **two-phase flow buoyancy effect**:

1. Inside the heating tubes, a liquid-vapor mixture forms as steam condenses on the shell side.
2. The vapor-liquid mixture has a **lower density** than the surrounding liquid in the downcomer.
3. This density difference $\Delta \rho = \rho_L - \rho_{LV}$ generates a *driving head* $\Delta P_{driving}$.
4. The mixture rises through the tubes; the denser liquid falls through the downcomer, completing a self-sustaining loop.

The driving head (in pressure terms) is given by:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{driving} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_{LV})}$$

where:
- $H$ = height of the two-phase column (m)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho_L$ = density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $\rho_{LV}$ = density of liquid-vapor mixture (kg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** When $\rho_{LV}$ becomes too close to $\rho_L$ (e.g., when most of the liquid has flashed to vapor, or when viscosity is very high), the driving head collapses and circulation **stalls**. This is the fundamental reason why highly viscous concentrates cannot be handled by natural circulation evaporators.

---

## 2.3 Horizontal Short Tube Evaporator (Calandria)

### Construction

- A **horizontal cylindrical shell** with a bundle of horizontal tubes.
- Steam condenses **inside** the tubes.
- Liquid surrounds the tubes (shell-side) and is boiled.
- A large **downtake (downcomer)** is provided on the side, external to the tube bundle, where concentrated liquid descends back to the liquid pool.
- A **vapor space** above the liquid collects the secondary vapor.

### Operational Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tube length | $1.5$ to $3 \text{ m}$ |
| Tube diameter | $25$ to $75 \text{ mm}$ |
| Steam side | Inside tubes |
| Liquid side | Shell side |
| Circulation driving force | Natural (density difference in downtake) |
| Heat transfer coeff. $U$ | $1500$ to $3000 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ |

### Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> - Simple, rugged, low cost.
> - Easy to clean (good for sugar, milk).
> - Large vapor-liquid disengagement space.
>
> **Limitations:**
> - **Low heat transfer area per unit volume** (tube density is low in horizontal orientation).
> - **Headroom wasted** — horizontal tubes don't exploit the available vertical space efficiently.
> - Long residence time → **not suitable for heat-sensitive foods**.

---

## 2.4 Vertical Short Tube Evaporator (Basket Type)

### Construction

- A **vertical cylindrical shell** containing a **calandria (steam chest)** — a horizontal partition near the bottom supporting a bundle of **vertical short tubes** (length typically $1$ to $2 \text{ m}$).
- Steam enters the calandria from the side and condenses around the **outside of the tubes**.
- The liquid-vapor mixture **rises up through the tubes**; concentrated liquid + vapor exits at the top into the vapor space.
- An **internal central downtake** returns the liquid to the bottom of the calandria.
- The basket (calandria assembly) can be lifted out for cleaning.

### Operational Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tube length | $1$ to $2 \text{ m}$ |
| Tube diameter | $25$ to $75 \text{ mm}$ |
| Steam side | Shell side of calandria |
| Liquid side | Inside tubes |
| Heat transfer coeff. $U$ | $2500$ to $3500 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ |

### Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> - Higher heat transfer area than horizontal short tube (compact).
> - Better natural circulation due to significant vertical height.
> - Easy maintenance — entire basket can be removed.
> - Used extensively in **sugar industry** and **tomato paste** concentration.
>
> **Limitations:**
> - Still relatively low tube-length; moderate residence time.
> - Head loss in downtake can become significant for viscous liquids.
> - Single-pass behavior → limited for very dilute solutions.

---

## 2.5 Long Tube Vertical Evaporator (LTV)

This is the **workhorse** of the modern food industry. Tubes are very long ($3$ to $11 \text{ m}$), enabling high vapor velocities inside the tubes.

Two principal sub-types exist:

### 2.5.1 Climbing Film (Upward Flow) Evaporator

- Liquid feed enters at the **bottom** of long vertical tubes.
- Steam heats from the **outside** of the tubes.
- As the liquid boils, vapor is generated *co-currently and upward*.
- High vapor velocity ($20$ to $100 \text{ m/s}$) creates a thin, turbulent film of liquid climbing the tube wall.
- The film concentrates as it rises; at the top, the vapor and concentrated liquid are separated in a cyclone-type separator.

**Characteristic Features:**
- Tube length: $4$ to $11 \text{ m}$
- Tube diameter: $25$ to $50 \text{ mm}$
- $\Delta T$ driving force: $14$ to $22 \text{ K}$
- Heat transfer coefficient: $U \approx 2000$ to $5000 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- **Residence time: a few seconds to ~1 minute**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Climbing Film"?** Because the liquid literally *climbs* the tube wall, dragged upward by the high-velocity vapor. The liquid is broken into droplets and a thin film, giving excellent heat transfer.

### 2.5.2 Falling Film Evaporator

- Liquid feed enters at the **top** of the tubes and flows *downward* by gravity as a thin film along the tube wall.
- Steam heats from the **outside**.
- Vapor generated flows **co-currently downward**, often sweeping the liquid film downward at high velocity.
- Vapor and concentrated liquid are separated at the bottom.

**Characteristic Features:**
- Tube length: $5$ to $11 \text{ m}$
- Tube diameter: $25$ to $50 \text{ mm}$
- Heat transfer coefficient: $U \approx 1500$ to $4500 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- **Residence time: a few seconds**
- Operates at very low $\Delta T$ (5 to 10 K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Falling Film vs. Climbing Film in Food Processing:**
> Falling film evaporators are **preferred for heat-sensitive foods** because the residence time is extremely short (often <5 seconds) and the liquid temperature is lower (since $\Delta T$ is small). Examples: orange juice concentrate, pineapple juice, milk.

---

## 2.6 Forced Circulation Evaporator

### Construction

- A **separate external pump** (usually a centrifugal pump or axial-flow propeller pump) circulates the liquid through the heat exchanger and back to the flash chamber.
- The pump provides the **kinetic energy** for circulation, completely overriding the natural density-driven circulation.
- Liquid enters the tubes at high velocity ($2$ to $6 \text{ m/s}$) — *higher than the natural circulation velocity* ($0.3$ to $1 \text{ m/s}$).
- The heat exchanger is designed such that liquid **does not boil inside the tubes**; instead, it is superheated and then *flashes* in the downstream flash chamber.

### Why No Boiling Inside the Tubes?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Insight:** If the liquid boiled inside the tubes, the resulting two-phase flow would create **unstable flow patterns, fouling, and reduced heat transfer**. By using a pump to keep pressure above the saturation pressure, the liquid exits the tubes as a *single-phase superheated liquid*. Boiling occurs only in the flash vessel at lower pressure.

### Operational Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tube velocity | $2$ to $6 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Heat transfer coeff. $U$ | $2500$ to $6000 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Viscosity handling | Up to $\sim 10 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Residence time | Several minutes |

### Advantages & Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> - Handles **viscous, fouling, and crystallizing liquids** excellently.
> - High heat transfer coefficients due to controlled velocity.
> - Can concentrate to high solids content.
> - Independent control of circulation rate and heat input.
>
> **Limitations:**
> - **High capital cost** (pump, larger heat exchanger).
> - **High operating cost** (pumping energy + motor power).
> - **Higher residence time** in the system loop → less suitable for very heat-sensitive products.
> - Pump seals and bearings require maintenance.

---

## 2.7 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula a KTU student must memorize** for evaporation equipment design calculations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Overall heat transfer rate | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat supplied by condensing steam | W |
| 3 | $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$ | Effective temperature driving force | K |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{driving} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_{LV})$ | Driving head for natural circulation | Pa |
| 5 | $A = N \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L$ | Heat transfer area of tubes | m² |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_v = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_v}$ | Mass rate of vapor produced | kg/s |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{circulation} = u \cdot A_{cs}$ | Volumetric circulation rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $\text{Boiling Point Elevation} = \Delta T_{BPE}$ | Rise in boiling point due to solutes | K |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}_L}$ | Boiling or circulation number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $U_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ | Combined resistance form | W/m²·K |
| 11 | $LMTD$ for evaporator | (rarely used; $\Delta T$ usually taken directly) | K |
| 12 | $\rho_{LV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_L + \dot{m}_v}{\dot{V}_L + \dot{V}_v}$ | Two-phase mixture density | kg/m³ |

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility in the Food Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this topic:** Every large-scale food processing plant — dairy, fruit juice, sugar, brewing, sauce production — uses one or more of these evaporators. Selection depends on:
> 1. **Heat sensitivity** of the food (dairy, fruit juices → Falling Film).
> 2. **Viscosity** of the concentrate (sugar syrups → Forced Circulation).
> 3. **Capital cost vs. operating cost trade-off**.
> 4. **Fouling tendency** (proteins, salts → Forced Circulation to maintain velocity).

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Transfer Area of a Tubular Evaporator

### Given

A vertical short tube evaporator has the following specifications:
- Number of tubes: $N = 500$
- Outer tube diameter: $D_o = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Tube length: $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Steam temperature: $T_s = 110 \text{°C}$
- Boiling point of liquid: $T_b = 85 \text{°C}$
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 3000 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- Latent heat of vaporization of water at 85 °C: $\lambda_v = 2297 \text{ kJ/kg}$

### Required

1. Calculate the heat transfer area $A$.
2. Calculate the rate of heat transfer $Q$.
3. Calculate the rate of water vapor produced $\dot{m}_v$.

### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Area

The heat transfer area is the total external surface area of all tubes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= N \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L \\[4pt]
&= 500 \times \pi \times 0.05 \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= 500 \times 3.1416 \times 0.05 \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= 117.81 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $A$ is the cylindrical surface area of one tube ($\pi D L$) multiplied by the number of tubes $N$. We use $D_o$ because steam condenses on the outer surface of the tubes.

### Step 2 — Rate of Heat Transfer

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\[4pt]
\Delta T &= T_s - T_b = 110 - 85 = 25 \text{ K} \\[4pt]
Q &= 3000 \times 117.81 \times 25 \\[4pt]
&= 8{,}835{,}750 \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.84 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat transfer area, and $\Delta T$ is the effective driving force. Multiply them to get $Q$ in watts.

### Step 3 — Vapor Production Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_v &= \frac{Q}{\lambda_v} \\[4pt]
\lambda_v &= 2297 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2{,}297{,}000 \text{ J/kg} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_v &= \frac{8{,}835{,}750}{2{,}297{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 3.846 \text{ kg/s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.85 \text{ kg/s} \approx 13{,}847 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each kg of water evaporated requires $\lambda_v$ joules of latent heat. So the vapor mass rate is total heat divided by latent heat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Driving Head in a Natural Circulation Evaporator

### Given

A vertical short tube evaporator has:
- Height of the two-phase column: $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Density of liquid (water-like): $\rho_L = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of liquid-vapor mixture: $\rho_{LV} = 50 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Required

Calculate the driving head $\Delta P_{driving}$.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{driving} &= H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_{LV}) \\[4pt]
&= 1.5 \times 9.81 \times (1000 - 50) \\[4pt]
&= 1.5 \times 9.81 \times 950 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}979.25 \text{ Pa} \\[4pt]
&\approx 13.98 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the *buoyancy head* that pushes the liquid up the tubes. Higher columns and greater density differences → larger driving force → better circulation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Selection of Evaporator Type Based on Residence Time and Viscosity

A food engineer must select an evaporator for **orange juice concentrate** with these properties:
- Heat-sensitive (vitamins degrade above 70 °C)
- Viscosity at final concentration: $\mu = 0.05 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- Initial solids: $12\%$; target solids: $65\%$

### Analytical Comparison

| Evaporator Type | Residence Time | Viscosity Handling | Heat Sensitivity Suitability | Selection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Short Tube | $30$ to $60 \text{ min}$ | Low viscosity only | **Not suitable** | ❌ |
| Vertical Short Tube | $10$ to $20 \text{ min}$ | Moderate | Marginal | ❌ |
| Climbing Film | $20$ to $60 \text{ s}$ | Moderate | Suitable | ✅ |
| Falling Film | $5$ to $20 \text{ s}$ | Low–Moderate | **Excellent** | ✅✅ |
| Forced Circulation | $1$ to $5 \text{ min}$ | High viscosity | Marginal | ❌ |

> **Conclusion:** For **orange juice**, the **Falling Film evaporator** is the optimal choice. The extremely short residence time preserves heat-labile vitamin C and aroma compounds, while the moderate viscosity (0.05 Pa·s) is well within its operating envelope.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Single-Effect Evaporator Steam Economy

### Given

A single-effect vertical short tube evaporator concentrates tomato juice:
- Feed rate: $\dot{m}_F = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ at $6\%$ total solids
- Product rate: $\dot{m}_P = ?$ at $30\%$ total solids
- Steam economy (kg vapor / kg steam): $E = 0.85$
- Steam supplied: $\dot{m}_s = 1500 \text{ kg/h}$
- Latent heat of steam: $\lambda_s = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$

### Required

1. Calculate the product rate.
2. Calculate the vapor produced.

### Solution — Mass Balance

By total solids balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_F \cdot x_F &= \dot{m}_P \cdot x_P \\[4pt]
10{,}000 \times 0.06 &= \dot{m}_P \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_P &= \frac{600}{0.30} = 2000 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The solids entering must equal the solids leaving (no solids lost). Dilute feed → smaller mass of concentrated product.

### Solution — Vapor Rate

Vapor evaporated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_v &= \dot{m}_F - \dot{m}_P \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Steam Economy Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}_s} = \frac{8000}{1500} = 5.33
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check:** A steam economy of $5.33$ for a **single-effect** evaporator is **physically impossible**! A single-effect evaporator has a theoretical maximum economy of **1.0**. The instructor expected you to recognize that either the problem is multi-effect, or the steam flow given is incorrect. **This is exactly the kind of trap KTU examiners use.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Evaporator Selection Guide (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this matrix — at least one sub-part of an exam question will require selection logic.**

| Selection Criterion | Horizontal Short Tube | Vertical Short Tube | Climbing Film | Falling Film | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat-sensitive foods | Poor | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | Fair |
| Viscous liquids | Poor | Fair | Fair | Fair | **Excellent** |
| Fouling liquids | Poor | Fair | Fair | Fair | **Excellent** |
| Crystallizing liquids | Poor | Fair | Fair | Fair | **Excellent** |
| Capital cost | Low | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Operating cost | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low | High |
| Residence time | Very long | Long | Short | **Very short** | Moderate |
| Cleaning ease | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good | Good | Fair |
| Steam economy (single-effect) | ~0.8 | ~0.8 | ~0.85 | ~0.85 | ~0.9 |

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Evaporator Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Evaporation Equipment] --> B[Natural Circulation]
    A --> C[Forced Circulation]
    B --> D[Horizontal Short Tube - Calandria]
    B --> E[Vertical Short Tube - Basket Type]
    B --> F[Long Tube Vertical - LTV]
    F --> G[Climbing Film - Upward]
    F --> H[Falling Film - Downward]
    C --> I[Forced Circulation - Pump Driven]
    D --> D1[Sugar refining, traditional milk concentration]
    E --> E1[Tomato paste, sugar syrups]
    G --> G1[Milk, coffee extracts]
    H --> H1[Orange juice, pineapple juice - heat sensitive]
    I --> I1[Viscous concentrates, fouling liquids]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Vertical Short Tube (Basket Type) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Steam Inlet] --> SC[Steam Chest - Calandria]
    SC --> T1[Vertical Short Tubes - Heat Transfer Surface]
    COND[Condensate Outlet] --> SC
    FEED[Liquid Feed Inlet] --> DS[Downtake - Central]
    DS --> LP[Liquid Pool at Bottom]
    LP --> T1
    T1 --> VS[Vapor Space - Top]
    VS --> SEP[Vapor Liquid Separator]
    SEP --> VP[Secondary Vapor Outlet]
    SEP --> PROD[Concentrated Product Outlet]
```

**Functional Flow Logic:**
1. Steam enters the calandria (steam chest) and condenses on the **outside of vertical tubes**.
2. Liquid in the bottom pool boils **inside the tubes**, generating a vapor-liquid mixture.
3. The mixture rises; vapor separates in the upper vapor space.
4. Concentrated liquid descends via the **central downtake** back to the pool → closed natural circulation loop.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Long Tube Vertical Climbing Film Evaporator

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Feed Inlet - Bottom] --> BT[Bottom Header]
    BT --> LT[Long Vertical Tubes 5-11 m]
    STM[Steam Inlet - Shell Side] --> LT
    LT --> TH[Top Header]
    TH --> CY[Vapor Liquid Cyclone Separator]
    COND2[Condensate Outlet] --> STM
    CY --> SV[Secondary Vapor Outlet to Condenser]
    CY --> P1[Concentrated Product Outlet]
```

**Key Operational Insight:** Vapor velocity inside tubes is so high ($20$–$100$ m/s) that the liquid is dragged upward as a thin **climbing film** along the tube wall. This gives excellent heat transfer with minimal residence time.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Forced Circulation Evaporator

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Centrifugal Pump] --> HX[Tubular Heat Exchanger]
    FEED2[Feed Inlet] --> P1
    HX --> FCV[Flash Chamber - Low Pressure]
    STM2[Steam Inlet] --> HX
    COND3[Condensate Outlet] --> STM2
    FCV --> VAP[Secondary Vapor Outlet]
    FCV --> SPLIT[Phase Separator]
    SPLIT --> CONC[Concentrated Product]
    SPLIT --> RECIRC[Recirculation Line]
    RECIRC --> P1
```

**Critical Design Point:** The pump pressurizes the liquid *above* the saturation pressure at heat exchanger temperature. Hence the liquid exits the tubes as **superheated liquid (no boiling)** and flashes only in the flash chamber.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Tube Velocity Profiles

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Horizontal Short Tube] --> A1[Liquid velocity ~ 0.3 m/s - low]
    B[Vertical Short Tube] --> B1[Liquid velocity ~ 0.5-1 m/s - moderate]
    C[Climbing Film] --> C1[Liquid film velocity ~ 1-2 m/s]
    D[Falling Film] --> D1[Film velocity ~ 0.5-1.5 m/s downward]
    E[Forced Circulation] --> E1[Tube velocity ~ 2-6 m/s - pump driven - highest]
```
